epics.title: Monorepo scaffolding & CI
epics.body: Tracks everything needed for the loombox pnpm monorepo to exist as a buildable, testable, releasable codebase before any subsystem is built inside it: the package skeletons for every workspace named in SPEC §10.1, shared TypeScript/lint/test tooling, the GitHub Actions gate (lint+typecheck+test), Changesets-based release automation, and the AGPL-contamination process gate protecting the MIT license commitment (SPEC §13). Also owns bootstrapping (not implementing) packages/protocol and packages/crypto so downstream subsystems have a package to import against from day one. Spec: §10, §10.1, §13; AGENTS.md "Layout" and "Conventions".

epics.title: Wire protocol
epics.body:
Tracking issue for `packages/protocol`: the shared, versioned Zod wire schema used by node, relay, and client, plus per-connection protocol-version negotiation modeled on ACP's `initialize` handshake.

Spec: §10 (protocol version negotiated once per connection, versioned Zod schema shared across node/relay/client).

This epic owns the schema package and negotiation logic; the Relay epic owns wiring negotiation into the actual relay WS upgrade path.

epics.title: Relay platform
epics.body:
Tracking issue for the self-hosted relay service: Fastify + WebSocket server, Postgres + Redis, Docker/compose packaging for prodbox, fan-out backpressure, ciphertext blob store, health/observability, abuse limits, retention, backup/DR, and cross-node session-lease ownership.

Spec: §5.3 (relay), §7.16 (fan-out backpressure), §7.21 (/health), §9 (self-host topology, retention, backup/DR, session ownership across nodes).

The relay only ever handles ciphertext and account-scoped session metadata (§8's bridge bullet) — it never sees session/resource content in plaintext. This epic covers the relay's own infrastructure; the E2E crypto and protocol schema it carries are owned by the Crypto and Protocol epics respectively.

epics.title: Node daemon
epics.body:
The orchestrator node: a daemon that manages sessions, spawns agents on execution targets, holds SSH/provider credentials, and connects outbound to the relay as an E2E device.

Spec: §5.1 (Orchestrator node), §6 (Node/Session/Device concepts), §10 (proposed tech stack), §10.1 (packages/node).

Covers: daemon scaffolding and lifecycle, node identity keypair, node-to-relay connection with protocol-version handshake, the target registry a node publishes, session bookkeeping (create/list/pause/resume), and same-folder session safety for non-worktree projects.

epics.title: Agent-supervisor & persistent sessions
epics.body:
The per-host agent-supervisor daemon that owns each agent as a child process over piped stdio (never a PTY), persists a structured resumable transcript, survives client/node disconnects, and keeps sessions alive and continuable while the driving node is offline. Also covers remote deployment of the supervisor onto ssh: targets, a tmux/screen zero-install fallback, and session-ownership leasing across nodes.

Spec: §5.6 (Agent-supervisor), §7.22 (Persistent sessions & offline continuation), §9 (session ownership across nodes), §10.

epics.title: PWA client (app shell & companion interactions)
epics.body:
Owns the web-PWA app shell: installable SvelteKit PWA, navigation, cross-node/cross-target session list, live session view, follow-up composer, stop/interrupt, offline action queueing, command palette, and mobile-specific interaction details (touch targets, narrow-viewport layout, device-switch state).

Spec: SS5.4, SS7.3 (also touches SS8's account-scoped session-list bridge and SS7.16's offline/backpressure rules where they surface in the client).

Covers: PWA scaffold and installability; session list/board bound to relay metadata; live streaming session view; composer with mid-turn queueing; Stop/interrupt; IndexedDB offline outbox; stale approve/deny discard; fuzzy quick-switcher and shortcuts; touch/haptic affordances; narrow-viewport permission layout; device-switch state preservation (v2 polish).

epics.title: Execution targets (local + SSH)
epics.body:
The ExecutionTarget abstraction that lets a session run locally or on a remote host over SSH: the local target, a pooled/reconnecting SSH transport, the ssh: target built on it, the login-shell PATH/mise fix, remote filesystem operations, and git worktree creation across both target kinds.

Spec: §5.2 (Execution targets), §6 (Target/Worktree concepts), §9 (mise/PATH gotcha), §10.

Design is reimplemented clean-room; emdash's core/ssh/* is a reference, never a source (§5.2, §13).

epics.title: SSH connection setup & remote auto-provisioning
epics.body:
The guided flow for adding an ssh: target: autodetection from ~/.ssh/config and ssh-agent, verifying and persisting the connection, and automatically provisioning the remote host (OS/arch detection, runtime bootstrap, signed supervisor binary install, idempotent re-provision, one-tap update, optional resident-node systemd install, and clean decommissioning).

Spec: §7.23 (SSH connection setup & remote auto-provisioning), §9 (self-host topology), §14 (remote auto-provisioning trust).

epics.title: Port forwarding
epics.body:
Automatic and manual port forwarding between an ssh: target and the client: the underlying SSH tunnel mechanism, manual forwarding rules, and automatic detection of a dev server an agent starts (PTY-output URL sniffing + probe, not a port-table scan).

Spec: §7.8 (Port forwarding), reference: emdash's port-forward tunnel, reimplemented clean-room (§16).

epics.title: E2E crypto & device lifecycle
epics.body:
Tracking issue for `packages/crypto` and the device lifecycle it powers: ECDH P-256 + AES-256-GCM AAD-bound envelopes, the Account Master Key (AMK) and its HMAC-SHA512 key-tree derivation, the device registry, QR/short-code pairing, recovery-code escrow bootstrap, device revocation with AMK-epoch rotation and wrap-fan-out, and node-side secrets-at-rest via OS keyring.

Spec: §8 (security & trust model, in full), §14 (multi-device E2E key distribution — flagged as v1's single riskiest dependency stack).

This is the hardest engineering area in v1 per §14; issues here are deliberately fine-grained.

epics.title: Account login & auth
epics.body:
Identity: self-hosted Better Auth mounted on the relay's Fastify server, Google/GitHub OAuth social login, the Bearer plugin for non-cookie (WebSocket) clients, the deliberate bounded bridge from OAuth identity to account-scoped session-list metadata, and — far-future — a code-free WebAuthn PRF convenience unlock layered on the existing Account Master Key escrow (§8): register a passkey with a PRF extension, derive a wrapping key from its PRF output via HKDF, and escrow a second wrapped-AMK blob under it. The PRF path is gated on re-confirming platform PRF support and Better Auth passkey-plugin readiness, since §14 cut this specifically for gaps (Firefox-Android, some CTAP2 keys) that may since have narrowed.

Spec: §8, §14.

This is identity only — it answers "which human is using loombox," never "which content can this device read" (that's the AMK, owned by the E2E crypto epic).

epics.title: Agent transcript & interaction UX
epics.body:
Owns the ACP session/update rendering contract: the append-by-id reducer for messages/thoughts, thinking/reasoning display, streaming mechanics, the two-tier tool-call system (bespoke widgets plus generic ToolKind fallback), the diff viewer, display-only terminals, plan rendering, the inline FIFO permission queue with its cross-session/nested/keyboard rules, and the model/mode/reasoning-effort bar. This is the primary interface of loombox, built directly on ACP's own vocabulary so it works uniformly across providers.

Spec: SS7.24 (cross-references SS7.9, SS7.19, SS5.5, SS7.3).

v1 covers the full flat-list baseline (reducer through permission queue through model/mode bar). v2 adds subagent/nested tool-call trees, the persistent plan sidebar, the tier-3 burst/group summary card, and CSS Custom Highlight API transcript search.

epics.title: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)
epics.body:
Owns the client-side attach/encrypt/upload pipeline for images: local preview and validation, client-side E2E encryption and optimistic upload to the relay blob store, the encrypted file-event handoff over the session channel, upload failure/retry UX, the supervisor-side fetch+decrypt step, and each provider's own image hand-off quirk. Also owns the cheaper @file reference picker.

Spec: SS7.25 (cross-references SS8's E2E scheme, SS7.16's backpressure rule, SS5.5's capability negotiation, SS7.4's file tree).

v1 ships the full image pipeline for Claude Code and Codex plus the generic ACP ResourceLink/temp-file fallback; HEIC/HEIF is rejected client-side with real conversion deferred past v1 with no committed version.

epics.title: Notifications (desktop + mobile, presence-aware)
epics.body:
Owns self-owned Web Push end to end: VAPID key custody, service-worker push subscription, presence-aware delivery routing (suppress on the actively-viewed session, route to the active device, fall back to push elsewhere), rendering the four notification-worthy event classes, actionable mobile push, and per-project mute/quiet-hours.

Spec: SS7.11 (cross-references SS7.13's event classes).

v1 ships the full presence-aware push loop for desktop and mobile with self-owned VAPID keys and no vendor push relay.

epics.title: Cross-project attention inbox
epics.body:
Owns the one unified view of everything needing the user's attention across every project and node: pending tool-call approvals, finished/errored sessions, CI failures, and review requests, all inline-actionable and kept in sync with the same per-session FIFO permission queue rather than a separate copy.

Spec: SS7.13 (cross-references SS7.24's cross-session surfacing bullet and SS7.11's notification triggers).

v1 ships a basic inbox; v2 adds richer node/target observability surfaced inline.

epics.title: Editor, file tree & diff
epics.body:
Owns browsing a project's file tree (local or ssh target) and opening files. v1 ships only the minimal read-only tree needed to back the @file reference picker (SS7.25); the full integrated editor with syntax highlighting/light quick-edit and the working-tree split diff viewer are v2 per the roadmap (SS12).

Spec: SS7.4.

Not a full IDE (SS11): a viewer plus light edit plus diff, nothing more.

epics.title: Integrated terminals (local + SSH)
epics.body:
Owns full PTY terminals reachable through the relay on any target, local or ssh, from desktop and phone, with multiple terminals per project sharing the session's working directory or worktree.

Spec: SS7.5.

v1 ships the interactive terminal end to end for both target kinds; v2 hardens backpressure/resize behavior for slow mobile clients under SS7.16's bounded-queue rule.

epics.title: ACP core & capability negotiation
epics.body:
Owns packages/providers/core — the generic ACP layer every provider adapter builds on: initialize/capability negotiation, session lifecycle (new/resume/list) and replay, the append-by-id message/thought/tool-call/plan/usage reducer, the session/request_permission FIFO queue (data layer), config-option state, and the enrich() extension point that per-provider modules register into. This is the architectural spine of §5.5's layered ACP design: a provider that implements nothing beyond bare ACP still gets a fully working cockpit through this package alone, and a session's own negotiated `initialize` capabilities are what gate optional UI affordances — never which provider brand is in use.

Spec: §5.5, §6, §7.24 (data-layer portions only — the actual rendering components belong to the separate agent transcript & interaction UX epic), §10, §10.1.

Boundary notes: the agent transcript & interaction UX epic owns the UI components that read this package's state; the provider adapters epic owns the per-provider modules that register into this package's extension points (registry, enrich hook).

epics.title: Provider adapters (Claude Code, Codex, generic; Gemini reserved)
epics.body:
Owns packages/providers/claude, packages/providers/codex, and packages/providers/generic — the per-provider adapter modules and the zero-code fallback tier from §5.5. Each adapter module supplies exactly the two things ACP deliberately leaves to the client: an `enrich()` hook (registered into packages/providers/core's registry from the ACP-core epic) and provider-specific UI wiring — a bespoke per-tool-name widget table and that provider's real permission verb set. v1 ships Claude Code and Codex, with Codex gated on a build-time spike confirming its ACP completeness; packages/providers/gemini is reserved as an empty stub in v1, with its adapter module built in v2.

Spec: §5.5, §7.24 (tool-call widget/permission-verb bullets), §10, §12 (roadmap gating for Codex/Gemini), §16 (Codex/Gemini build-time-verification grounding).

Out of scope here: the image attachment encrypt/upload/hand-off pipeline (§7.25) belongs to the rich-input & attachments epic; this epic only covers each adapter's capability check and widget/permission wiring.

epics.title: Agent configuration: MCP servers & plugins
epics.body:
Owns per-project and global configuration of coding-agent MCP servers and plugins/extensions: adding and enabling MCP servers (with quick-add presets), managing plugins, and — the security-critical piece — an explicit per-server secret grant so a configured MCP server only ever receives the project secrets a user explicitly hands it, never an ambient copy of everything the project holds. MCP/plugin content itself is treated as untrusted input once it reaches an agent, never as operator instruction.

Spec: §7.7, §7.17 (untrusted-input framing), §16 ('MCP per-server secret grant' — flagged novel, no reference implementation to copy).

Boundary notes: permission allow/deny command and network policy (also mentioned in §7.7's prose) is enforced by the separate guardrails/sandboxing/secrets epic; this epic only owns MCP server and plugin configuration plus the secret-grant mechanism.

epics.title: Tracker integration: native TrackerRecord + live TrackerBackend (GitHub/Jira)
epics.body: Per-project choice of tracking mode with no background sync and no local mirror of an external tracker (SPEC §7.10). Covers the TrackerMode/TrackerBackend shared types; the native local ticketing model (schema-driven TrackerRecord: fields bag + system object + indexed columns, per Nimbalyst's post-refactor shape) with its MCP tool contract (tracker_list/tracker_get/tracker_create/tracker_update/tracker_link_session); and the live external backends for GitHub (REST issues/comments + GraphQL Projects v2) and Jira (REST v3 + Agile API), delivered in the spec's three explicit slices: (1) issues+comments, (2) transitions, (3) boards/sprints/Projects v2, each independently shippable behind the same interface and capability flags. TrackerBackend runs server-side only (node/supervisor), consumes resolved credentials from the connected-accounts registry (§7.26) via resolveCredential(connectionId), and never performs its own OAuth or token storage.

epics.title: Connected accounts: multi-account GitHub/Jira registry + per-project pinning
epics.body: The ConnectedAccount registry answering "which external GitHub/Jira identity acts on this project's behalf" (SPEC §7.26), generalized from emdash's single-account GitHubAccountRegistry and Jira jira-connection-service. Covers the provider-agnostic data model and relay sync of metadata-only rows (tokens stay in the executing node's OS keyring, never the relay); GitHub connect paths (device flow default, gh CLI import, fine-grained PAT paste); Jira connect paths (API token, OAuth 2.0 3LO with multi-site accessible-resources discovery); per-project per-capability account pinning with hard-fail-on-mismatch resolution; the node-locality lazy secret-presence check across multiple execution nodes; and safe-disconnect scanning. This is a hard interface boundary consumed by §7.10 (trackers) and §7.14 (PR/CI), and is kept strictly separate from loombox account login (§8).

epics.title: AI-assisted git management
epics.body: Full git control for a project that is a repo, with the AI in the loop, working uniformly on local and ssh: targets (SPEC §7.6): commit graph and branch tree view, staged/unstaged diff inspection with hunk-level stage/unstage/discard, commit with AI-generated messages, branch create/switch/merge, stash, push, AI diff-explain, and AI-assisted merge-conflict resolution.

epics.title: PR & CI lifecycle
epics.body: Close the loop from prompt to merged (SPEC §7.14): open a PR from a session's branch via gh, watch CI checks and surface failures back to the agent for auto-iteration, handle review comments and merge, and write PR/merge linkage back uniformly across both tracker modes from §7.10 (native TrackerRecord.system fields, or live TrackerBackend addComment/update). A red check or review request lands in the attention inbox (§7.13).

epics.title: Test & verification runner
epics.body: A first-class surface to run a project's tests/lint/build, stream results, and let the agent iterate on failures automatically until green (SPEC §7.15), with per-project command configuration (once, or auto-detected), feeding the PR/CI loop and the attention inbox.

epics.title: Usage monitoring & spend caps
epics.body: Per-session and aggregate tracking of context-window usage, tokens, and estimated cost with a live meter (SPEC §7.9), including the subagent-exclusion rule for the context-fill percentage (excluded from % fill, still counted in cumulative cost/spend rollups), plus the per-project/per-session cost caps that auto-pause a session and raise it in the attention inbox (SPEC §7.16's spend-cap bullet).

epics.title: Concurrency & resource governance
epics.body: Running many agents at once must not melt the host (SPEC §7.16): per-target concurrency caps with an overflow queue, CPU/RAM/disk resource awareness per target so sessions are throttled or queued instead of OOM-ing, a load/limits UI, and bounded per-client fan-out backpressure (drop-oldest + resync marker) so a slow client never blocks faster ones or the supervisor's own persistence.

epics.title: Agent guardrails, sandboxing & per-project secrets
epics.body: Autonomous agents hold real credentials and run real commands (SPEC §7.17): a per-project permission policy (allow/deny command and network patterns), sandboxing of agent execution toward namespace/bind-mount scoping of the session's worktree (OS containers where available, documented weaker macOS fallback) that must not default to off once shipped, and secret/env injection scoped per project that never reaches the relay or clients. This is the enforcement layer that does not depend on the agent cooperating with ACP's discretionary session/request_permission.

epics.title: Session templates, project instructions & prompt library
epics.body: Speed across many projects (SPEC §7.18): reusable session templates/presets bundling target, provider, MCP set and starting prompt in one click; surfacing and editing a project's own AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md as its agent instructions; and a reusable prompt/snippet library.

epics.title: Session history, search & replay
epics.body: A searchable archive of every session's transcript and actions for audit (what an agent did, when, at what cost), with client-side search over already-decrypted content and the ability to fork or replay a past session as the start of a new one (SPEC §7.19), including the client-side transcript search via the CSS Custom Highlight API called out on the v2 roadmap.

epics.title: Checkpoint & rollback
epics.body: Snapshot a session's workspace at a point in time and roll back an agent's changes safely, independent of git commits (SPEC §7.20): git-based checkpointing for git projects, filesystem-snapshot/content-hash checkpointing for non-git projects, and the rollback flow itself.

epics.title: Node & target health / observability
epics.body: A status view of nodes and targets, reachability, agent-process health, and each target's CPU/RAM/disk (SPEC §7.21), so the user can see why a session stalled and whether a target is overloaded (pairs with §7.16's resource governance), plus the relay's own /health endpoint for an external uptime check.

epics.title: Voice (BYO-key, clean-room)
epics.body:
Tracks §7.12's bring-your-own-key voice feature: talk to a session, hear responses, and approve tool calls by voice, backend-abstracted (ElevenLabs / a realtime API / local) and driven entirely by the operator's own keys. Designed from HAPI's approach but clean-room per §13's hard licensing gate — HAPI is AGPL-3.0, so no HAPI code may be copied or cloned into the build environment. Deferred to v3 because the core cockpit loop is proven first (§7.12, §12).

Covers: the VoiceBackend abstraction and BYO-key credential storage, the first concrete ElevenLabs backend, the encrypted audio pipeline (reusing §7.25's attachment-delivery architecture so it works identically on `local` and `ssh:` targets), and voice-driven tool-call approval wired into §7.24's permission queue.

epics.title: Native mobile wrapper (Capacitor)
epics.body:
Tracks the v3 native mobile wrapper: the same SvelteKit web-PWA wrapped with Capacitor into `apps/mobile` (§5.4, §10, §10.1) — "a later addition, not a launch blocker," motivated sooner if iOS's PWA background-push/voice limits bite (§14).

Covers: the initial Capacitor spike (`cap init`/`add`/`sync`), native push notifications, a signed build/release pipeline for both stores, and native-specific UX (camera/file picker, haptics) that plugs into affordances already specified for the web PWA (§7.3, §7.25) rather than duplicating them.

epics.title: Multi-agent orchestration
epics.body:
Tracks §12's far-future "multi-agent collaboration" line: planner/implementer/reviewer roles, and best-of-N racing a task with a pick-best. This requires generalizing v2's vendor-`_meta`-derived `parentToolCallId` nested tool-call tree rendering (§7.24) from a single agent's own subagent signal into trees that loombox itself creates on purpose, by spawning and coordinating multiple real child sessions.

Covers: the session-tree design, role-preset orchestration, and best-of-N racing with a comparison/pick-best UI.

epics.title: Scheduling & automation (webhook/headless runs)
epics.body:
Tracks §12's far-future "scheduling and webhook-triggered/headless runs (nightly maintenance, or a tracker ticket that auto-spins a session)": a session that runs with no client ever attached, driven purely by the supervisor (§5.6, §7.22) and surfaced only through the attention inbox (§7.13) and push (§7.11).

Covers: the headless-run design, cron-based scheduled runs, and tracker-webhook-triggered runs on top of that primitive.

epics.title: Managed-cloud edition (§17)
epics.body: Tracks §17's far-future managed-cloud deployment edition: the identical self-hosted relay image operated at scale by loombox-the-company, multi-tenant via the same `owner_account_id` column §8 already introduces (§17.1-17.2), still E2E with the one honest client-trust gap named in §17.3, relay-tier-only — never the node or agent-supervisor (§17.4) — staying MIT (§17.5), with day-one config-level portability (§17.6). Explicitly gated on v1's AMK/account-login model surviving a real self-hosted lost-device/rebuilt-relay drill (§12, §14, §17) before any multi-tenant ops land on top of it.

epics.title: brand/design system
epics.body: Tracks loombox's visual identity per SPEC §4: the woven warp/weft logo mark, the dark-first ink/paper token system with its single warm thread accent, the Inter + JetBrains Mono typography setup, and the woven-thread loading/working animation motif. This is the shared design layer every client surface (PWA, landing site) draws from; it does not build any product feature itself.

epics.title: landing site (SvelteKit on prodbox)
epics.body: Tracks the separate loombox-landing repository: a SvelteKit (adapter-node) marketing site kept out of the product monorepo, built and deployed to prodbox behind Caddy on loombox.dev exactly like pitchbox and embertold. Spec: §10.1 "loombox-landing".

issues.title: Scaffold the pnpm monorepo with stub packages for every AGENTS.md package
issues.body:
Goal: stand up the pnpm workspace and a stub package for every entry in AGENTS.md's layout, so every later v0 issue has somewhere to land and the full monorepo shape exists from commit one.
Spec: AGENTS.md “Layout — a pnpm monorepo (SPEC §10.1)”; SPEC §10.1 “Repositories & monorepo layout”.
Acceptance:
- `pnpm-workspace.yaml` plus root `package.json`/`tsconfig.json` exist and `pnpm install` succeeds from a clean clone.
- Every package from AGENTS.md's list exists with a minimal `package.json`, `tsconfig.json`, one placeholder `src/index.ts`, and one passing placeholder test: `apps/web`, `apps/mobile`, `packages/node`, `packages/supervisor`, `packages/relay`, `packages/protocol`, `packages/crypto`, `packages/providers/{core,claude,codex,generic}` (gemini reserved but not stubbed if it adds no value yet), `packages/shared`, `tooling/`, `scripts/`.
- `pnpm -r build` and `pnpm -r typecheck` both succeed workspace-wide with nothing but stubs implemented.
- Root `LICENSE` is MIT (SPEC §13).
Notes: no §16 grounding entry for a monorepo skeleton itself; follow AGENTS.md's package layout literally rather than inventing package names.
Depends on: none.

issues.title: Add GitHub Actions CI gating lint, typecheck, and test
issues.body:
Goal: make every PR prove it lints, typechecks, and passes tests across the whole workspace before merge.
Spec: AGENTS.md “Conventions — Testing / CI”; SPEC §10 “Testing / CI”.
Acceptance:
- A GitHub Actions workflow runs on PR and push to main: `pnpm install`, lint, `pnpm -r typecheck`, `pnpm -r test` (Vitest).
- The workflow fails the check run on any red step, and the repo branch protection (or at least the workflow itself) is set up so this is visible on a PR.
- Uses the pnpm workspace's own scripts, not ad hoc per-package invocations.
Notes: no §16 entry; follow the AGENTS.md convention directly.
Depends on: Scaffold the pnpm monorepo with stub packages for every AGENTS.md package.

issues.title: Set up Changesets for versioning
issues.body:
Goal: wire up Changesets so every package change from here on carries a changeset, matching the project's stated release convention.
Spec: AGENTS.md “Conventions — Releases”; SPEC §10 “Release / versioning”.
Acceptance:
- `@changesets/cli` is installed and configured at the workspace root (`.changeset/config.json`).
- A README or CONTRIBUTING note explains how to add a changeset.
- A CI or manual-trigger workflow exists that can version/publish via Changesets (does not need to actually publish anything real yet, since v0 packages aren't released).
Notes: SPEC §16 — “Release/versioning — Changesets (`@changesets/cli` + action); deliberate departure from emdash/happy/hapi (none use it).”
Depends on: Scaffold the pnpm monorepo with stub packages for every AGENTS.md package.

issues.title: Define the v0 wire protocol schema (node/relay/client)
issues.body:
Goal: give node, relay, and PWA one shared, versioned Zod schema for the handful of messages v0 needs, instead of each side inventing its own shape.
Spec: SPEC §10 “Protocol: a versioned, Zod-typed wire schema shared across node/relay/client as one package… negotiated once per connection”; SPEC §12 v0 bullet.
Acceptance:
- `packages/protocol` exports Zod schemas and inferred TS types for at least: `NodeHello` (node registers with the relay), `ClientHello` (PWA registers), `SessionAnnounce` (node tells relay a session exists), `SessionUpdateEnvelope` (wraps an ACP `session/update` for fan-out, tagged with session id), and `PromptInject` (client asks the relay to forward a follow-up prompt to a session).
- Every message carries a protocol version field.
- Unit tests assert valid payloads parse and malformed ones are rejected for each schema.
- Node, relay, and the PWA client all import these types rather than redeclaring them.
Notes: SPEC §16 — “Protocol version negotiated once per connection — ACP `initialize` handshake; carry it in Socket.IO `handshake.auth`” (v0 does not need Socket.IO specifically; the version-negotiation pattern is the point, not the library).
Depends on: Scaffold the pnpm monorepo with stub packages for every AGENTS.md package.

issues.title: Build a minimal in-memory relay: WS fan-out plus node/client registration
issues.body:
Goal: stand up the thinnest relay that can accept a node connection, accept a client connection, and fan session output from one to the other — no persistence, no crypto, since v0 is transport-only over Tailscale.
Spec: SPEC §5.3 (relay fans out session state/messages between a node and its clients); SPEC §12 v0 (“output relayed to a minimal PWA”); SPEC §8 (“Transport-only fallback is a conscious option… TLS + Tailscale WireGuard”).
Acceptance:
- A Fastify server with a WebSocket endpoint accepts a node connection carrying `NodeHello` and a client connection carrying `ClientHello`, using the v0 protocol schema.
- When a node sends `SessionAnnounce`, any connected client sees that session appear.
- A `SessionUpdateEnvelope` sent by the node is fanned out to every client currently registered for that session.
- A `PromptInject` sent by a client is routed to the owning node's connection.
- No Postgres/Redis/Better Auth/E2E crypto is introduced — in-memory state only, explicitly disposable (SPEC §12 v0: “v0 data is disposable”).
Notes: SPEC §16 — “Relay stack (Fastify + WS + Postgres + Redis) — happy-server `sources/main.ts` + `Dockerfile.server`” (v0 subset: Fastify + WS only, skip Postgres/Redis).
Depends on: Define the v0 wire protocol schema (node/relay/client).

issues.title: Deploy the v0 relay reachable over Tailscale
issues.body:
Goal: get the v0 relay actually running somewhere on the tailnet so a phone can reach it, proving the transport-only deployment shape before any TLS/Caddy/prodbox work exists.
Spec: SPEC §12 v0 (“Transport-only over Tailscale”); SPEC §9 (self-host topology reference — relay normally on prodbox, Tailscale available for the private path).
Acceptance:
- The relay process runs and is reachable via `ws://` (or `wss://` if trivially available) on a Tailscale IP, from another device on the same tailnet.
- A start command (or a minimal systemd unit) is documented so the relay can be brought up repeatedly, not just run ad hoc once.
- A basic WS client (curl/websocat/a throwaway script) confirms connectivity from a phone-equivalent device on the tailnet before the PWA exists.
Notes: no §16 entry for this specific deployment step; grounded in §9's topology description rather than an external reference. Running on prodbox with Caddy/TLS is v1 (§9, §12 v1).
Depends on: Build a minimal in-memory relay: WS fan-out plus node/client registration.

issues.title: Implement node session model and git worktree creation for the local target
issues.body:
Goal: give the node the minimal ability to turn “a project folder + a provider” into a running session in an isolated git worktree, on the `local` execution target only.
Spec: SPEC §6 (Project, Session, Worktree concepts); SPEC §7.1 (session lifecycle: choose node/target/project/provider, worktree or in-place); SPEC §5.2 (`local` target); SPEC §12 v0 (“one Claude Code session in a worktree”).
Acceptance:
- Given a local git-repo folder path, the node creates a new git worktree on a fresh branch and records a Session object (`id`, `projectPath`, `worktreePath`, `target: 'local'`, `provider`).
- The session store is in-memory only for v0 (no DB), consistent with v0 data being disposable.
- A session can be created and looked up by id via the node's own internal API (no relay/network involvement in this issue).
- Passing a non-git folder is rejected for v0 (folder-not-repo support per §6 is real but the worktree path is what v0's acceptance requires; don't build the in-place/non-git branch here).
Notes: no §16 entry names the worktree-creation mechanism specifically; `git worktree add` is standard git, grounded in §6/§7.1's concept definitions rather than an external reference.
Depends on: Scaffold the pnpm monorepo with stub packages for every AGENTS.md package.

issues.title: Wire the node to the relay: register, announce session, pump events both ways
issues.body:
Goal: connect the node's session/supervisor machinery to the relay so a created session becomes visible to clients and steerable from them.
Spec: SPEC §5.1 (node “connects outbound to the relay and registers” — as a plain node for v0, E2E-device registration is v1); SPEC §12 v0.
Acceptance:
- The node opens an outbound WS connection to the v0 relay and sends `NodeHello`, then `SessionAnnounce` for each session it creates.
- Every `session/update` event emitted by the local supervisor (see the agent-supervisor spawn issue) is forwarded to the relay as a `SessionUpdateEnvelope` tagged with the session id.
- An inbound `PromptInject` for one of the node's sessions is delivered to that session's running supervisor instance.
- The connection reconnects with backoff if the WS drops, rather than requiring a node restart.
Notes: SPEC §16 — “Fan-out backpressure — Node `stream` highWaterMark + ring buffer keyed by a `seq`, or adopt socket.io `connectionStateRecovery`”: full backpressure handling is v1/v2 hardening (§7.16); this issue only needs the basic pump to work end to end, not bounded queues or resync markers yet.
Depends on: Implement node session model and git worktree creation for the local target; Build the agent-supervisor: spawn and own the Claude Code ACP child process; Build a minimal in-memory relay: WS fan-out plus node/client registration; Define the v0 wire protocol schema (node/relay/client).

issues.title: Implement the ACP core client subset needed for v0 (initialize, session/new, prompt, update parsing)
issues.body:
Goal: implement just enough of the generic ACP client role in `packages/providers/core` to drive one full turn with any ACP-speaking agent process: handshake, start a session, send a prompt, and parse the resulting message stream.
Spec: SPEC §5.5 (“Generic ACP core… owns everything ACP itself standardizes… A provider that implements nothing beyond the ACP spec still gets a fully working cockpit through this core alone”); SPEC §10 (“An ACP-generic backend on the Agent Client Protocol (ACP) reference types”).
Acceptance:
- A module that spawns/talks to a JSON-RPC-over-stdio child process, performs the ACP `initialize` handshake, and calls `session/new`.
- Sending a prompt via `session/prompt` and receiving `session/update` notifications for `agent_message_chunk` and `user_message_chunk` (append-by-`messageId`) works and is unit-tested against a small fixture ACP-agent script (not a real provider), so the core logic is testable without Claude Code installed.
- `tool_call`/`tool_call_update`, `plan_update`, `usage_update`, and `session/request_permission` are explicitly out of scope for this issue (v1, SPEC §7.24) — don't build the full reducer yet, just message-chunk parsing.
Notes: SPEC §16 — “Generic ACP fallback tier — grounded in ACP baseline (`ContentBlock::Text`/`ResourceLink` + 4 permission kinds)”; the full transcript reducer contract is v1 per §7.24, this issue only needs the message-chunk subset.
Depends on: Scaffold the pnpm monorepo with stub packages for every AGENTS.md package.

issues.title: Build the Claude Code provider adapter for v0
issues.body:
Goal: give the ACP core something real to drive by wiring up Claude Code's own ACP-speaking mode as `packages/providers/claude`.
Spec: SPEC §5.5 (per-provider adapter modules); SPEC §12 v0 (names “one Claude Code session” explicitly).
Acceptance:
- The adapter exports the spawn configuration (command, args, env) needed to launch Claude Code in ACP mode, consumed by the agent-supervisor.
- The adapter registers itself under provider id `'claude'`.
- The v0 adapter's `enrich()` hook is a no-op (the `_meta.claudeCode.parentToolUseId` promotion is v2, SPEC §7.24) — don't build it now.
- An integration test spawns Claude Code (or a stand-in that speaks its ACP surface) for one prompt → response turn inside a temp git worktree, using the ACP core from the previous issue.
Notes: SPEC §16 — “Image hand-off — grounded inline base64 for both Claude (`claude-agent-acp` `acp-agent.ts`) and Codex…” confirms Claude Code ships an ACP-speaking mode (`claude-agent-acp`); use it as the reference for how to invoke Claude Code over ACP.
Depends on: Implement the ACP core client subset needed for v0 (initialize, session/new, prompt, update parsing).
issues.epic: Provider adapters (Claude Code, Codex, generic; Gemini reserved)

issues.title: Build the agent-supervisor: spawn and own the Claude Code ACP child process
issues.body:
Goal: give `packages/supervisor` the minimal ability to spawn one agent process via a provider adapter, keep it alive independent of any single caller, and expose its event stream — the local, in-process case only.
Spec: SPEC §5.6 (“a small agent-supervisor daemon owns the agent processes: it spawns each agent as a child process… persists a structured, resumable transcript to disk, survives client/node disconnects” — v0 narrows this to “spawns and survives a caller detaching,” disk persistence is v1); SPEC §12 v0.
Acceptance:
- Given a workspace path and provider id, the supervisor spawns the provider's child process via the provider adapter and completes the ACP handshake/`session/new`.
- Exposes an API to submit a new prompt into the running session and an event stream/callback for incoming `session/update` notifications.
- The spawned process keeps running even if the caller (the node's WS connection) briefly disconnects and reconnects — it must not need to be respawned on every reconnect.
- If the child process crashes, the supervisor emits a terminal error event rather than hanging silently.
- No disk-persisted transcript is required for v0 (in-memory buffer only); resumable persistence is explicitly deferred to v1 (SPEC §7.22).
Notes: SPEC §16 — “SSH-deploy-then-detach supervisor (survives disconnect) — POSIX `setsid`/`nohup` detach (or systemd `KillMode=process`); precedent: mosh / VS Code Remote-SSH persistent server (novel plumbing)”: v0 only needs the local in-process spawn case, not the SSH-deploy-and-detach variant, which is v1.
Depends on: Build the Claude Code provider adapter for v0.

issues.title: Scaffold the SvelteKit PWA with PWA manifest and service worker
issues.body:
Goal: get `apps/web` bootstrapped as an installable PWA before building any real screens, since the spec flags this as its own spike.
Spec: SPEC §10 (“Client: a PWA in SvelteKit (installable, offline-tolerant)”); SPEC §16 (“SvelteKit PWA — needs a v0/v1 spike”).
Acceptance:
- `apps/web` is a SvelteKit app using `adapter-node`.
- `@vite-pwa/sveltekit` is configured with a minimal manifest and service worker; the app builds and serves.
- Installability is checked manually (add-to-homescreen on a phone browser, or a Lighthouse installability check) and noted as passing in the PR — full offline-tolerance is not required for v0.
Notes: SPEC §16 — “SvelteKit PWA — `@vite-pwa/sveltekit` + hapi `web/src/sw.ts` as a hand-rolled SW example; needs a v0/v1 spike (adapter-node + PWA)” — this issue is exactly that spike.
Depends on: Scaffold the pnpm monorepo with stub packages for every AGENTS.md package.

issues.title: PWA: connect to the relay and render a session's live transcript
issues.body:
Goal: let the PWA connect to the v0 relay, see the one announced session, and watch its transcript grow live.
Spec: SPEC §5.4 (“lists all sessions across all of the user's nodes/targets and lets the user view live output”); SPEC §12 v0 (“output relayed to a minimal PWA that can view”).
Acceptance:
- The PWA opens a WS connection to the relay and sends `ClientHello` using the v0 protocol schema.
- The session announced by the node appears in the PWA (even if it's the only one, and even with a bare-bones list of one item).
- Incoming `agent_message_chunk`/`user_message_chunk` events for that session render as a plain, append-only transcript (no tool-call widgets, no diffs, no thinking UI — those are v1, SPEC §7.24).
- Loading the PWA on a phone browser over Tailscale and watching a session run on the devbox works.
Notes: SPEC §16's tool-widget/reducer grounding (nimbalyst `CustomToolWidgets`, `ToolWidgetErrorBoundary`) is v1 scope; v0 only needs a plain append log.
Depends on: Scaffold the SvelteKit PWA with PWA manifest and service worker; Build a minimal in-memory relay: WS fan-out plus node/client registration; Define the v0 wire protocol schema (node/relay/client).

issues.title: PWA: add a composer to send a follow-up prompt into a running session
issues.body:
Goal: close the loop — let the user type a follow-up prompt in the PWA and see the agent act on it.
Spec: SPEC §7.3 (“send follow-up prompts”); SPEC §12 v0 acceptance (“inject one prompt that the agent acts on”).
Acceptance:
- A text input and send action in the PWA submits a `PromptInject` message over the existing WS connection, addressed to the visible session.
- The transcript shows the new user turn appended, followed by the agent's streamed response.
- Exercised from a phone on the tailnet against a session actually running on the devbox, matching SPEC §12's v0 acceptance line verbatim.
- Only one in-flight prompt is supported for v0; offline queueing of composed prompts is explicitly out of scope (SPEC §7.3's offline-actions bullet and §16's IndexedDB-outbox note are v1).
Notes: SPEC §16 flags the offline composer outbox as “novel” and v1-scoped; v0's composer is online-only.
Depends on: PWA: connect to the relay and render a session's live transcript; Wire the node to the relay: register, announce session, pump events both ways.

issues.title: Validate the full v0 loop end to end from a phone on the tailnet
issues.body:
Goal: prove the actual v0 thesis — “see and steer a devbox session from my phone” — with a written, repeatable validation pass across every piece built in this milestone, not just each piece's own unit tests.
Spec: SPEC §12 v0 (“Acceptance: from a phone on the tailnet, start nothing but observe a running session and inject one prompt that the agent acts on.”)
Acceptance:
- With the node and supervisor running on the devbox and a Claude Code session already active in a worktree, a phone browser on the tailnet loads the PWA and observes that session's live transcript without starting anything itself.
- From that same phone, sending one follow-up prompt results in a visible new agent turn in the transcript.
- The whole path is exercised over the relay deployed in “Deploy the v0 relay reachable over Tailscale,” not against a localhost shortcut.
- Findings (what worked, what didn't, any manual steps still required) are written up as the closing note on this issue, since SPEC §12 explicitly treats v0 as disposable validation, not a foundation to build v1 directly on top of.
Notes: this issue directly operationalizes SPEC §12's v0 Acceptance line; there is no separate §16 grounding entry because it's a validation pass over the rest of v0, not a new mechanism.
Depends on: Deploy the v0 relay reachable over Tailscale; Wire the node to the relay: register, announce session, pump events both ways; PWA: add a composer to send a follow-up prompt into a running session.

issues.title: Scaffold pnpm workspace monorepo layout with package skeletons
issues.body:
Goal: Stand up the pnpm workspace monorepo with the exact package layout the spec defines, so every subsystem has a place to land code from day one.
Spec: §10.1 "Repositories & monorepo layout"; AGENTS.md "Layout — a pnpm monorepo (SPEC §10.1)".
Acceptance:
- pnpm-workspace.yaml declares apps/web, apps/mobile (placeholder), packages/node, packages/supervisor, packages/relay, packages/protocol, packages/crypto, packages/providers/{core,claude,codex,generic} (gemini reserved as an empty stub), packages/shared, tooling/, scripts/
- Each package has a minimal package.json (workspace-scoped name), a stub src/index.ts, and installs cleanly with `pnpm install`
- Root package.json has workspace scripts (build/test/lint/typecheck) that fan out via pnpm -r
- Root README documents the layout and links to AGENTS.md
Notes: no specific §16 grounding item for the monorepo layout itself; follow §10.1 literally as the source of truth for package boundaries.
Depends on: none

issues.title: Bootstrap packages/protocol as a versioned Zod schema package
issues.body:
Goal: Create the packages/protocol package skeleton — the shared, versioned wire-schema package every other subsystem will import — with its versioning mechanism stubbed in, without yet defining the full session/message wire shapes (that belongs to the E2E protocol epic).
Spec: §10 "Protocol: a versioned, Zod-typed wire schema shared across node/relay/client"; §10.1 packages/protocol bullet.
Acceptance:
- packages/protocol builds, exports a PROTOCOL_VERSION constant and an empty/minimal Zod schema namespace
- Package is consumable from packages/node, packages/relay, apps/web via workspace dependency
- A stub test asserts the package exports the version constant
- Package README states this is a bootstrap only; full message/session schemas land under the "Wire protocol" epic
Notes: §16 grounds the negotiation pattern itself ("Protocol version negotiated once per connection — ACP `initialize` handshake") for whoever implements the real handshake later; this issue only bootstraps the package.
Depends on: Scaffold pnpm workspace monorepo layout with package skeletons

issues.title: Bootstrap packages/crypto with vetted primitive dependencies wired
issues.body:
Goal: Create the packages/crypto package skeleton with its chosen primitive library installed and a placeholder API surface, deferring the actual E2E envelope/AMK implementation to the security/crypto epic.
Spec: §10 "Crypto: vetted primitives (tweetnacl/libsodium-class + AES-256-GCM), per-device ECDH key wrapping"; §16 "E2E envelope crypto — nimbalyst TrackerEnvelopeCrypto.ts/ECDHKeyManager.ts (P-256/WebCrypto, AAD-bound) — pick ONE curve".
Acceptance:
- packages/crypto has its dependencies installed (WebCrypto-based ECDH P-256 per the spec's pinned curve choice, plus an AES-256-GCM helper); no live key-derivation logic yet
- Package exports typed but unimplemented (throwing) stubs for deriveKey/wrapKey/encryptEnvelope/decryptEnvelope so downstream packages can compile against the shape
- A stub test file exists and passes, establishing the test harness for future crypto unit tests
- README records the curve decision (P-256, not X25519) so it isn't relitigated later
Notes: §16 explicitly flags "pick ONE curve (don't blend with happy's X25519/tweetnacl); the spec's ECDH P-256 is grounded in nimbalyst only" — bake that decision into the package README now.
Depends on: Scaffold pnpm workspace monorepo layout with package skeletons

issues.title: Set up shared TypeScript, ESLint and Prettier configuration
issues.body:
Goal: Establish one shared TypeScript base config and lint/format rules so every package in the monorepo compiles and lints consistently.
Spec: AGENTS.md "Testing / CI" (lint+typecheck+test gate); §10 tech stack (TypeScript/Node throughout).
Acceptance:
- A root tsconfig.base.json (strict mode) is extended by every package's own tsconfig.json
- A shared ESLint flat config + Prettier config live in tooling/ and are referenced from every package
- `pnpm -r typecheck` and `pnpm -r lint` both run clean on the scaffolded skeleton packages
- Editor integration is documented in README
Notes: not called out in §16; standard TS monorepo tooling practice.
Depends on: Scaffold pnpm workspace monorepo layout with package skeletons

issues.title: Set up Vitest testing infrastructure across the monorepo
issues.body:
Goal: Wire up Vitest as the unit/integration test runner for every package so "every package ships tests from commit one" is mechanically enforced, not just a convention.
Spec: AGENTS.md "Testing / CI: Vitest (unit/integration) + Playwright (PWA e2e). Every package ships tests from commit one"; §10 "Testing / CI".
Acceptance:
- A shared root vitest.config.ts (or workspace config) is extended per-package
- Each scaffolded package has at least one passing placeholder test
- `pnpm -r test` runs every package's tests and exits non-zero on failure
- Coverage reporting is configured (no threshold enforced yet)
Notes: no specific §16 grounding item; generic tooling setup, not a novel mechanism.
Depends on: Scaffold pnpm workspace monorepo layout with package skeletons; Set up shared TypeScript, ESLint and Prettier configuration

issues.title: Add GitHub Actions CI workflow gating merge on lint, typecheck, and test
issues.body:
Goal: Add a GitHub Actions workflow that runs on every PR and blocks merge unless lint, typecheck, and test all pass across the workspace.
Spec: §10 "a GitHub Actions workflow gates merge on lint + typecheck + test"; AGENTS.md "Testing / CI".
Acceptance:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml runs pnpm install, then lint, typecheck, and test steps across all packages on push/PR to main
- The workflow is documented as the required status check for branch protection (branch-protection settings themselves are a repo-admin action outside version control)
- CI passes on the scaffolded skeleton (green build with only stub packages/tests)
- Uses pnpm's official GitHub Actions caching pattern for install speed
Notes: not called out in §16; standard GitHub Actions + pnpm recipe.
Depends on: Set up Vitest testing infrastructure across the monorepo

issues.title: Establish the AGPL-contamination process gate
issues.body:
Goal: Put a concrete, enforced process in place so no AGPL-3.0 code (specifically HAPI) is ever copied into the build environment, since the codebase commits to MIT and contamination would poison licensing.
Spec: §13 "Provenance & licensing... HAPI (AGPL-3.0) — BYO-key voice idea only. Do not copy or clone HAPI into the build environment. A process gate (checklist/review step) enforces no AGPL contamination"; AGENTS.md "Conventions... treat this as a hard process gate".
Acceptance:
- CONTRIBUTING.md states the clean-room rule explicitly: design inspiration from emdash/Happy/Nimbalyst/HAPI is fine, importing/copying code is not, HAPI specifically must never be cloned into any workspace this repo's agents touch
- The PR template requires an explicit "no code copied from HAPI or any AGPL source" acknowledgement checkbox
- A dependency-license scan (e.g. license-checker) runs in CI and fails the build if any dependency introduces AGPL/GPL-family licensing
- Root README links to this policy from its licensing section
Notes: §13 is the sole grounding for this gate; it's an internal process control, not a borrowed mechanism, so no external reference tool is cited.
Depends on: Add GitHub Actions CI workflow gating merge on lint, typecheck, and test

issues.title: Scaffold apps/web SvelteKit PWA shell
issues.body:
Goal: Create the apps/web package as a minimal SvelteKit app with PWA plumbing wired in, providing the shell the v0 spike's minimal PWA will be built into (by the PWA client epic, out of this scope).
Spec: §10 "Client: a PWA in SvelteKit (installable, offline-tolerant), wrapped with Capacitor for apps/mobile"; §10.1 apps/web bullet; §16 "SvelteKit PWA — @vite-pwa/sveltekit + hapi web/src/sw.ts as a hand-rolled SW example; needs a v0/v1 spike (adapter-node + PWA)".
Acceptance:
- apps/web is a SvelteKit app using @sveltejs/adapter-node (matching the relay-fronted deployment model used by pitchbox/loombox-landing)
- @vite-pwa/sveltekit is installed and configured with a minimal manifest + service worker so the app is installable
- `pnpm --filter web dev` runs a blank shell page locally
- A stub Playwright config file exists (full test wiring deferred to its own issue) so the e2e harness has a home
Notes: §16 flags this needs a v0/v1 spike explicitly — this issue is that spike, scoped to plumbing only, not session UI (owned by the PWA client epic).
Depends on: Scaffold pnpm workspace monorepo layout with package skeletons

issues.title: Scaffold the packages/node daemon package
issues.body:
Goal: stand up the packages/node TypeScript package with a CLI entrypoint, config loading, logging, and daemon lifecycle (start/stop/status/graceful shutdown), so every other node feature has a package to land in.

Spec: §5.1, §10 ("Node daemon: TypeScript/Node... packaged as a CLI/daemon"), §10.1 (packages/node).

Acceptance:
- `packages/node` builds and runs under the pnpm workspace.
- A CLI command boots the daemon, loads config from file/env, and logs startup.
- SIGTERM/SIGINT triggers a graceful shutdown.
- Unit tests cover config loading (valid/missing/invalid config).

Notes: foundational scaffolding; no specific §16 grounding entry (see §16 "Build & client" for the comparable SvelteKit/Capacitor scaffolding spikes).
Depends on: none

issues.title: Implement node identity keypair generation and persistence
issues.body:
Goal: give every node a stable ECDH P-256 identity keypair, generated on first run and persisted via OS-native secret storage, so the node can register as an E2E device with the relay.

Spec: §5.1 ("Connects outbound to the relay and registers as an E2E device"), §8 (per-device identity keypair, secrets at rest).

Acceptance:
- On first run with no existing keypair, the node generates one and persists it.
- Restarting the node reloads the same keypair (same public key).
- Storage goes through the keyring abstraction with a documented fallback when no keyring session exists.
- Unit tests cover generate/persist/reload.

Notes: §16 "Secrets at rest — @napi-rs/keyring (headless Node; not Electron safeStorage); decide fail-closed vs 0600-file fallback."
Depends on: Scaffold the packages/node daemon package

issues.title: Implement node-to-relay connection with protocol version handshake
issues.body:
Goal: the node connects outbound over WebSocket to a configured relay, performs the protocol-version handshake on connect, and reconnects automatically on drop.

Spec: §5.1, §5.3 (Relay), §10 ("protocol version negotiated once per connection... relay surfaces 'update required'").

Acceptance:
- Node opens an outbound WS connection to a configured relay URL.
- Handshake carries the node's supported protocol-version range.
- An "update required" response from the relay is surfaced rather than failing silently.
- Disconnects trigger automatic reconnect with backoff.
- Integration test against a mock relay covering connect/handshake/disconnect/reconnect.

Notes: §16 "Protocol version negotiated once per connection — ACP initialize handshake; carry it in handshake.auth."
Depends on: Implement node identity keypair generation and persistence

issues.title: Implement target registry: node publishes its execution targets to the relay
issues.body:
Goal: a node tracks its configured execution targets (local + ssh:) and publishes the current set and reachability status to the relay, so clients can start sessions on any target from any node.

Spec: §5.2 ("The set of targets a node exposes is published to the relay so clients can start sessions anywhere"), §6 (Target concept).

Acceptance:
- Node maintains a persisted list of configured targets (id, kind, label).
- On connect, and on any change, the node publishes its current target list to the relay.
- Each published target reports a reachability/status field.
- Unit and integration tests cover publish-on-connect and publish-on-change.

Depends on: Implement node-to-relay connection with protocol version handshake

issues.title: Implement session manager: create, list, pause, resume within a node
issues.body:
Goal: node-level bookkeeping for sessions — track each session's target, project, provider, and lifecycle state (running/paused/resumed), independent of the supervisor's own process-level concerns.

Spec: §6 (Session concept), §7.1 (Session lifecycle: "Sessions can be paused, resumed, and reconnected"), §5.1.

Acceptance:
- A session record captures node/target/project/provider and a lifecycle state.
- Sessions can be created, listed, paused, and resumed via the node's internal API.
- State transitions are validated (e.g. can't resume a session that was never paused).
- Unit tests cover the state machine, including invalid transitions.

Depends on: Implement target registry: node publishes its execution targets to the relay

issues.title: Enforce same-folder session safety for non-worktree projects
issues.body:
Goal: prevent two sessions from running in-place on the same plain folder at the same time, since a project can be a folder with no worktree isolation.

Spec: §7.2 ("Same-folder safety: because a project can be a plain folder with no worktree, two sessions may not run in place on the same folder at once — the second is queued or refused with a warning; using worktrees removes the restriction").

Acceptance:
- Starting a second in-place session on a folder that already has one running is refused or queued, with a clear reason shown.
- Two sessions on the same project using separate worktrees are unaffected by this restriction.
- Unit tests cover both the refusal/queue path and the worktree-exempt path.

Depends on: Implement session manager: create, list, pause, resume within a node

issues.title: Define the ExecutionTarget interface and the local target implementation
issues.body:
Goal: define the core ExecutionTarget abstraction shared by local and ssh: targets, and implement the local target: running agent/supervisor processes on the machine the node itself runs on.

Spec: §5.2 ("local — run the agent on the machine the node runs on"), §6 (Target concept).

Acceptance:
- An ExecutionTarget TypeScript interface covers spawn/exec and basic filesystem operations needed by the supervisor, editor, and terminal.
- The local implementation runs a command in a given working directory and returns output/exit code.
- Unit tests cover the local target's exec and error paths.

Notes: foundational abstraction defined by this spec; no direct §16 external-reference citation.
Depends on: Scaffold the packages/node daemon package

issues.title: Set up a Dockerized SSH test host fixture for execution-target integration tests
issues.body:
Goal: provide a reusable, scriptable SSH server fixture (Docker/testcontainers) that integration tests can spin up to exercise ssh: target behavior — pooled transport, the non-interactive PATH/mise gap, filesystem operations, worktrees, and port forwarding — without needing a real remote box.

Spec: §5.2 (ssh: target), §9 (mise/PATH gotcha as the concrete scenario to reproduce).

Acceptance:
- A docker-compose or testcontainers-based SSH host boots with a known user/key.
- The fixture includes a mise-managed node install that is reachable only via a login shell, reproducing the non-interactive PATH gap on purpose.
- At least one integration test in the repo connects to the fixture over an SSH client library.
- Fixture usage is documented in the relevant package README.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Implement pooled, reconnecting SSH transport for ssh: targets
issues.body:
Goal: build a connection manager that maintains a persistent, pooled SSH connection per remote host with keep-alive and automatic reconnect, so a flaky link or a sleeping laptop doesn't force the user to redo setup.

Spec: §5.2 ("pooled/reconnecting SSH transport"), §7.23 step 2 ("loombox tests the connection and keeps it pooled and persistent with keep-alive and automatic reconnect").

Acceptance:
- Multiple operations against the same host reuse a single pooled connection rather than opening a new one each time.
- A dropped connection is detected and automatically reconnected with backoff.
- The transport exposes a queryable health/status per host.
- Integration test against the Dockerized SSH fixture that kills and restores the connection mid-test.

Notes: §16 groups the design under "emdash's connection-manager approach" (§7.23), reimplemented clean-room per §5.2/§13; §10 ("SSH: a maintained Node SSH library, with a pooled connection manager").
Depends on: Define the ExecutionTarget interface and the local target implementation, Set up a Dockerized SSH test host fixture for execution-target integration tests

issues.title: Implement the ssh: execution target on top of the pooled SSH transport
issues.body:
Goal: implement the ssh:<host> ExecutionTarget — running agent/supervisor processes and basic remote operations over the pooled SSH transport.

Spec: §5.2 ("ssh:<host> — run the agent on a remote host over SSH... and remote worktree/filesystem operations").

Acceptance:
- The ssh: target executes a command on the remote host and returns its output/exit code.
- Errors (host unreachable, auth failure, command failure) surface as typed, distinguishable errors.
- The target reuses the pooled transport rather than opening ad-hoc connections.
- Integration tests run the same exec-path assertions against the Dockerized SSH fixture.

Depends on: Implement pooled, reconnecting SSH transport for ssh: targets

issues.title: Fix the non-interactive-shell PATH problem for ssh: targets (mise/login-shell capture)
issues.body:
Goal: capture a login-shell environment (or explicitly source mise's activation) when running commands on an ssh: target non-interactively, so node and agent CLIs resolve on a headless box exactly as they do interactively.

Spec: §5.2, §9 ("mise/PATH gotcha (must handle): on a headless box, runtime managers (mise → node) and agent CLIs are not on PATH in non-interactive/SSH shells. The ssh: target's environment capture must source a login shell (or explicitly eval \"$(~/.local/bin/mise activate bash)\") so node/the agent CLI resolve"), AGENTS.md build-order note.

Acceptance:
- A command run through the ssh: target resolves mise-managed binaries (node, the agent CLI) even though the SSH session itself is non-interactive.
- Verified against the Dockerized SSH fixture, which reproduces the exact gap (mise install not on the default non-interactive PATH).
- Both a login-shell-sourcing path and an explicit `eval "$(mise activate bash)"` fallback are exercised by tests.
- The fix and its rationale are documented in the target-setup output shown to the user.

Notes: §13 provenance — "emdash — SSH/worktree/mise-PATH mechanics" (design inspiration only, reimplemented clean-room).
Depends on: Implement the ssh: execution target on top of the pooled SSH transport

issues.title: Implement remote filesystem browsing operations for ssh: targets
issues.body:
Goal: expose directory listing, file read, and stat operations over the ssh: target's transport, so the file-tree/editor and the supervisor can browse and read files on a remote host.

Spec: §5.2 ("remote worktree/filesystem operations"), §7.4 ("Remote files are browsed and opened over the same transport as the session").

Acceptance:
- Listing a remote directory returns its entries (name, type, size, mtime).
- Reading a remote file returns its contents; stat returns size/mtime.
- Permission-denied and missing-path errors surface as typed errors, not generic failures.
- Integration tests run against the Dockerized SSH fixture.

Depends on: Implement the ssh: execution target on top of the pooled SSH transport

issues.title: Implement git worktree creation and removal across local and ssh: targets
issues.body:
Goal: support creating and removing an isolated git worktree for a session on either target kind, and support running directly in the working directory when the user opts out of a worktree.

Spec: §6 ("Worktree (optional)... The user chooses per session; worktree is not mandatory"), §7.1 ("whether to isolate in a new worktree or work in place").

Acceptance:
- Creating a session with "new worktree" produces an isolated git worktree on the chosen target.
- Choosing "work in place" runs the session directly in the project's working directory instead.
- Deleting a worktree-backed session cleans up its worktree.
- The same behavior is verified on both the local target and the ssh: target (via the Dockerized fixture).

Depends on: Fix the non-interactive-shell PATH problem for ssh: targets (mise/login-shell capture), Define the ExecutionTarget interface and the local target implementation

issues.title: Scaffold packages/supervisor and the ACP-over-stdio child process transport
issues.body:
Goal: create the supervisor package and its core loop — spawn a configured ACP agent CLI as a child process and route ACP JSON-RPC messages over piped stdio in both directions, never a PTY.

Spec: §5.6 ("spawns each agent as a child process (ACP JSON-RPC over piped stdio, not a PTY)"), §7.22, §10 ("Agent-supervisor: a small TypeScript/Node daemon... owning agent PTYs" — note: owns processes, PTYs are for terminals only per §5.6/§7.22).

Acceptance:
- The supervisor spawns a configured agent CLI as a child process with piped stdio.
- JSON-RPC requests/responses flow correctly in both directions between the driving side and the child process.
- A child-process crash or unexpected exit is detected and reported as an event.
- Unit tests use a fake ACP agent process (a small script that speaks minimal JSON-RPC) to verify the transport.

Notes: no direct §16 external-reference citation for the ACP-child-process pattern itself; grounded in ACP's own reference types (§10).
Depends on: none

issues.title: Persist a structured, resumable session transcript to disk
issues.body:
Goal: the supervisor buffers and persists every ACP session/update it relays to disk in a structured, replayable format, so a session's full history survives a supervisor restart and can be replayed on reconnect.

Spec: §5.6 ("persists a structured, resumable transcript to disk"), §7.22, §7.24 ("The same reducer runs identically for a live stream and for replayed history on reconnect — there is no separate 'replay renderer'").

Acceptance:
- Every ACP session/update is appended to an on-disk, per-session transcript log.
- Restarting the supervisor process and replaying the log reconstructs the same ordered event sequence.
- The transcript format is versioned to allow future schema evolution.
- Unit tests cover an append/restart/replay round-trip.

Depends on: Scaffold packages/supervisor and the ACP-over-stdio child process transport

issues.title: Implement supervisor attach/resume across client and node disconnects
issues.body:
Goal: a supervised session keeps running when its client or driving node disconnects, and a reconnecting client/node re-attaches and receives buffered output since the last acknowledged point.

Spec: §5.6 ("survives client/node disconnects, exposes attach/resume"), §7.22, §7.1 ("Sessions can be paused, resumed, and reconnected").

Acceptance:
- Killing the client connection mid-turn leaves the agent process running under the supervisor, unaffected.
- Re-attaching after a disconnect returns the buffered transcript since the last point that client/node had seen.
- A node restart re-attaches to a still-running supervised agent without losing session state.
- Integration test simulates a disconnect/reconnect cycle mid-turn.

Depends on: Persist a structured, resumable session transcript to disk

issues.title: Emit supervisor completion/attention events independent of any connected client
issues.body:
Goal: the supervisor emits session-completion and attention-worthy events (turn finished, errored, awaiting permission) on its own, whether or not a client is currently attached, so a node can relay them the moment it (re)connects.

Spec: §5.6 ("emits completion/attention events independently of any connected client"), §7.22, §7.13 (attention inbox is the eventual consumer of these events).

Acceptance:
- Turn-finished/errored/awaiting-permission events are recorded even with zero attached clients.
- A node that (re)connects later receives the backlog of such events since it was last connected.
- Unit tests verify event emission with no attached client present.

Depends on: Implement supervisor attach/resume across client and node disconnects

issues.title: Deploy and launch the supervisor on ssh: targets from the node
issues.body:
Goal: when a session starts on an ssh: target, the node deploys the supervisor binary to the remote host (if missing or outdated) and launches it there, so the agent runs under remote supervision and survives the SSH link dropping and the driving node exiting.

Spec: §5.6 ("for an ssh: target the node deploys and drives a supervisor on the remote host"), §7.22 ("the node deploys and launches the supervisor on the remote host; the agent runs under it and survives the SSH link dropping and the driving node exiting").

Acceptance:
- Starting a session on an ssh: target results in a running supervisor process on the remote host.
- Killing the SSH connection and reconnecting re-attaches to the still-running remote supervisor and agent.
- The driving node exiting entirely does not kill the remote agent process.
- Integration test runs against the Dockerized SSH fixture.

Notes: shares its deployment mechanism with the auto-provisioning issues in the SSH epic — this issue is the runtime deploy-and-launch path used once a target is already provisioned; first-time install/detection is covered separately.
Depends on: Implement supervisor attach/resume across client and node disconnects, Fix the non-interactive-shell PATH problem for ssh: targets (mise/login-shell capture)

issues.title: Add a tmux/screen fallback supervisor mode for hosts without the supervisor binary
issues.body:
Goal: on a remote host where the supervisor binary can't be dropped (unsupported arch/OS, no write access), fall back to a zero-install tmux/screen-based detach mechanism so the agent still survives disconnects.

Spec: §7.22 ("tmux/screen is kept only as a zero-install fallback for a remote host where the supervisor binary can't be dropped").

Acceptance:
- When supervisor deployment fails or is explicitly disabled, the node launches the agent inside a detached tmux or screen session on the remote host instead.
- A reconnect re-attaches to that tmux/screen session and can read its buffered output.
- The fallback is clearly surfaced as a fallback, not silently substituted for the real supervisor.
- Integration test covers both the deploy-failure trigger and the reattach path.

Notes: §16 "SSH-deploy-then-detach supervisor (survives disconnect) — POSIX setsid/nohup detach (or systemd KillMode=process); precedent: mosh / VS Code Remote-SSH persistent server. (novel plumbing)"
Depends on: Deploy and launch the supervisor on ssh: targets from the node

issues.title: Implement session ownership leasing across nodes
issues.body:
Goal: a session is owned by exactly one node via a renewable lease, so two nodes never drive the same supervisor at once; a second node may attach read-only while the lease is live and reclaim it once it expires.

Spec: §9 ("Session ownership across nodes: a session is owned by one node via a renewable lease; a second node may attach read-only while the lease is live and reclaim it on expiry (an explicit action in the PWA), so a Mac node and a devbox node never fight over the same supervisor").

Acceptance:
- Only the current leaseholder node may send prompts/control to a session's supervisor.
- A second node attaching to the same session while the lease is live gets read-only access.
- An expired lease can be explicitly reclaimed by another node.
- Unit tests cover acquire/renew/expire/reclaim.

Notes: §16 "Session ownership across nodes — Postgres pg_advisory_lock (heartbeat + expiry) or Redis SET NX PX lock. (novel for this app)" — the lock primitive itself may live server-side (relay epic); this issue is the node/supervisor-side leasing client and enforcement.
Depends on: Implement supervisor attach/resume across client and node disconnects, Implement node-to-relay connection with protocol version handshake

issues.title: Implement SSH host autodetection from ~/.ssh/config and ssh-agent
issues.body:
Goal: when adding an ssh: target, read the user's ~/.ssh/config, known hosts, and available keys/ssh-agent identities, so the common case is "just choose a host" instead of typing raw connection details.

Spec: §7.23 step 1 ("Connect... loombox reads your ~/.ssh/config, offers known hosts, and picks up your keys and ssh-agent, so in the common case you just choose a host").

Acceptance:
- Parses ~/.ssh/config Host entries (HostName, User, IdentityFile, Port).
- Lists known hosts as selectable candidates in the add-target flow.
- Detects available ssh-agent identities and surfaces them as usable auth options.
- Falls back to manual entry when nothing is discoverable.
- Unit tests run against fixture ~/.ssh/config files (single host, multiple hosts, malformed entries).

Depends on: Implement pooled, reconnecting SSH transport for ssh: targets

issues.title: Implement the 'verify & persist' step of the guided ssh: target setup
issues.body:
Goal: test a candidate SSH connection end-to-end and, on success, persist it as a pooled, keep-alive target, per step 2 of the guided SSH setup flow.

Spec: §7.23 step 2 ("Verify & persist. loombox tests the connection and keeps it pooled and persistent with keep-alive and automatic reconnect... so a flaky link or a sleeping laptop doesn't force a re-setup").

Acceptance:
- A "test connection" action reports success or a specific failure reason (auth failure, host unreachable, key rejected, etc.).
- On success, the target is persisted and immediately usable through the pooled transport.
- A failed test leaves no half-configured target behind.
- Integration test covers both the success and at least two distinct failure paths, against the Dockerized SSH fixture.

Depends on: Implement SSH host autodetection from ~/.ssh/config and ssh-agent

issues.title: Detect remote OS/arch and bootstrap the runtime the agent CLI needs
issues.body:
Goal: as part of auto-provisioning, detect the remote host's OS/architecture and, if the required Node/mise runtime is missing, bootstrap it so the agent CLI can actually run there.

Spec: §7.23 step 3 ("loombox sets up the components it needs on the remote automatically: it detects the remote OS/arch, installs or copies the agent-supervisor (and bootstraps the runtime the agent CLI needs if missing), handling the non-interactive-shell PATH problem (§9) so node/the agent resolve").

Acceptance:
- Detected OS/arch is reported to the user before anything is installed.
- If node/mise is missing, it is installed automatically, or the user is told it needs manual install on an unsupported host.
- The bootstrap shows exactly what it is about to do before running it, and nothing runs without explicit user confirmation.
- Integration test runs against a bare (runtime-not-installed) variant of the Dockerized SSH fixture.

Depends on: Fix the non-interactive-shell PATH problem for ssh: targets (mise/login-shell capture)

issues.title: Auto-provision the signed agent-supervisor binary on a new ssh: target
issues.body:
Goal: on a verified ssh: target, install or copy the correct agent-supervisor binary for the remote OS/arch, verifying its signature against the node's pinned public key before it is ever executed.

Spec: §7.23 ("Signed supervisor: the node ships a pinned public key; supervisor releases are signed (minisign/sigstore) and verified before execution, distributed via GitHub Releases with published checksums — the bootstrap never runs unverified remote code").

Acceptance:
- The node fetches the release binary matching the remote OS/arch from GitHub Releases.
- The binary's signature is verified against the node's pinned public key before it is copied to or executed on the remote host.
- A tampered, unsigned, or checksum-mismatched binary is refused with a clear error and nothing is executed.
- Unit tests cover valid-signature, invalid-signature, and missing-signature cases.

Notes: §16 "Signed supervisor binary — minisign (pinned Ed25519 key, matches 'pinned public key') or sigstore/cosign; distribute via GitHub Releases + checksums (hapi release.yml shows checksums)."
Depends on: Detect remote OS/arch and bootstrap the runtime the agent CLI needs

issues.title: Make remote supervisor provisioning idempotent and re-runnable on version bump
issues.body:
Goal: re-running provisioning against an already-provisioned target is a safe no-op unless the target's supervisor is out of date, in which case it upgrades in place.

Spec: §7.23 ("The bootstrap is idempotent and re-runs on version change; it shows exactly what it installs").

Acceptance:
- Running provisioning twice against an already-current target makes no changes on the second run.
- Bumping the pinned supervisor version and re-running provisioning upgrades the remote binary.
- Each run prints a diff of what it will change before applying anything.
- Test suite covers both the no-op path and the upgrade path.

Notes: §16 "Idempotent re-provision on version bump — ACP initialize.protocolVersion handshake as the version-check pattern; the install-then-verify recipe is (novel)."
Depends on: Auto-provision the signed agent-supervisor binary on a new ssh: target

issues.title: Detect an out-of-date target via protocol handshake and offer a one-tap update
issues.body:
Goal: when a target's supervisor/protocol version falls behind, surface it to the node's status API as an available "update this target" action, driven by the version handshake with that target.

Spec: §7.23 ("Keeping targets current: when a target is outdated (detected via the protocol version handshake, §10), the PWA offers a one-tap 'update this target'").

Acceptance:
- The node compares a target's reported protocol/supervisor version against the current pinned version and flags it as outdated.
- A node-level "update this target" action triggers the idempotent re-provision flow for that target.
- Outdated status is queryable so a client can render the one-tap affordance.
- Unit tests cover the version-comparison logic (current, behind, ahead/unknown).

Depends on: Make remote supervisor provisioning idempotent and re-runnable on version bump

issues.title: Implement resident-node systemd installation on a remote ssh: target
issues.body:
Goal: optionally install loombox as a resident node (systemd unit) on the remote host as part of provisioning, enabling autonomous agent continuation and offline push notifications even while the driving laptop is off.

Spec: §7.23 ("Optionally it also installs loombox as a resident node (systemd) on the remote, which is what enables autonomous continuation and offline notifications"), §7.22, §9.

Acceptance:
- An opt-in provisioning step installs and enables a systemd unit that runs the node daemon on the remote host.
- The unit restarts on failure and starts on boot.
- Declining this step still leaves the target fully usable, just without resident-node continuation/notification benefits.
- Integration test runs against a systemd-capable variant of the test SSH fixture.

Notes: §16 "Resident daemon (systemd) on Linux — hapi's docs/guide/installation.md systemd user-unit templates (Type=simple/Restart=always/KillMode=process) + systemd.service(5)."
Depends on: Auto-provision the signed agent-supervisor binary on a new ssh: target

issues.title: Implement target decommissioning (remove an ssh: target)
issues.body:
Goal: a clean "remove this target" action that stops/disables any installed remote units, revokes the target's device key, and optionally cleans up installed files on the remote host.
