Spec: §7.23 ("Removing a target: a decommission action stops/disables the remote units, revokes the device key, and offers to clean up installed files").

Acceptance:
- Decommissioning stops and disables any systemd unit installed for that target.
- The target's device key is revoked from the node's trusted set.
- The user is offered (and can accept or decline) removal of installed supervisor files on the remote host.
- The target no longer appears as usable after decommissioning; integration test covers the full flow.

Depends on: Implement resident-node systemd installation on a remote ssh: target

issues.title: Implement the local first-run guided setup (no SSH)
issues.body:
Goal: give the common local-only case its own guided first run — point at an existing relay or self-host one with a single command, auto-register the node, and start a first local-target session, without touching any SSH configuration.

Spec: §7.23 ("First run without SSH: the common local case is its own guided first-run — point at an existing relay or self-host one with a single command, auto-register the node, and start a first local session — not only the ssh: flow above").

Acceptance:
- A single command/flow configures the node with a relay URL (either an existing relay or a freshly self-hosted one).
- The node's identity is auto-registered with that relay as part of the same flow.
- The user can start a first local-target session at the end of the flow with no SSH setup involved.
- An end-to-end test exercises this path from a fresh, unconfigured node.

Depends on: Define the ExecutionTarget interface and the local target implementation, Implement node-to-relay connection with protocol version handshake

issues.title: Implement the SSH port-forward tunnel mechanism
issues.body:
Goal: build the underlying tunneling primitive, riding on the existing pooled SSH transport, that both manual and automatic port-forward rules use to move traffic between a remote host and the client.

Spec: §7.8 ("emdash's port-forward tunnel is the reference; reimplemented clean-room").

Acceptance:
- Given a remote port and a local listener, traffic to the local listener is proxied to the remote port over the existing SSH connection (no separate SSH process spawned per tunnel).
- The tunnel closes cleanly when its rule is removed or the underlying connection drops.
- Multiple simultaneous tunnels on the same host share the pooled connection.
- Integration test uses a simple TCP echo server on the remote side of the Dockerized SSH fixture.

Notes: §16 "Auto port-forward — emdash preview-servers/terminal-url-detector.ts (PTY-output URL sniff + probe, not port-table scan) + port-forward-tunnel.ts."
Depends on: Implement pooled, reconnecting SSH transport for ssh: targets

issues.title: Implement manual port-forward rules for ssh: targets
issues.body:
Goal: let the user define manual forwarding rules (remote port to local port) for a given ssh: target, independent of automatic dev-server detection.

Spec: §7.8 ("plus manual port-forward rules").

Acceptance:
- A manual rule can be created, listed, and removed for a given ssh: target.
- The forwarded port is reachable locally while the rule is active.
- Removing the rule tears down the tunnel cleanly.
- Integration test runs against the Dockerized SSH fixture with something listening on a remote port.

Depends on: Implement the SSH port-forward tunnel mechanism

issues.title: Implement automatic detection and forwarding of a dev server an agent starts
issues.body:
Goal: sniff a running agent's terminal/process output for a dev-server URL (e.g. a "Local: http://localhost:3000" banner) on an ssh: target and automatically set up a port-forward so it opens correctly from the client.

Spec: §7.8 ("automatic detection and forwarding of a dev server an agent starts (open it in your local browser or from the phone)").

Acceptance:
- Starting a common dev server (e.g. vite/next) inside a session on an ssh: target is detected from its stdout, not from scanning a port table.
- A forwarding rule is created automatically once detected, and a probe confirms the remote port is actually listening before it is exposed.
- The resulting local URL is reported back to the session so the user can open it.
- Test uses a fixture process that prints a known dev-server banner without actually needing a real dev server.

Notes: §16 "Auto port-forward — emdash preview-servers/terminal-url-detector.ts (PTY-output URL sniff + probe, not port-table scan) + port-forward-tunnel.ts."
Depends on: Implement the SSH port-forward tunnel mechanism, Persist a structured, resumable session transcript to disk

issues.title: Scaffold packages/relay: Fastify + WebSocket server skeleton
issues.body:
Goal: Stand up the self-hostable relay service skeleton — a Fastify HTTP server with a WebSocket upgrade path — as the foundation every later relay/protocol/auth feature is added to.
Spec: §5.3, §10 (Relay stack).
Acceptance:
- `packages/relay` exists in the pnpm workspace with a Fastify server exposing an HTTP port and a WS upgrade path
- `pnpm --filter @loombox/relay dev` boots locally against a local Postgres+Redis (docker-compose for dev)
- Vitest unit test covers server boot and a basic WS echo round trip
- lint+typecheck+test pass in CI
Notes: §16 "Relay stack (Fastify + WS + Postgres + Redis) — happy-server sources/main.ts + Dockerfile.server."
Depends on: none

issues.title: Add Postgres schema & migrations for relay core tables
issues.body:
Goal: Create the relay's Postgres schema (migrations) for the device registry, encrypted blob store, and session/resource metadata tables that every later relay feature reads or writes.
Spec: §5.3, §6, §8 (device registry), §9.
Acceptance:
- Migration tool set up (e.g. node-pg-migrate/drizzle) with a `migrate` script
- Tables created: device registry (id, public_key, status, last_seen, owner_account_id nullable), blob store (ref, ciphertext, size, created_at, ttl), session metadata (id, node_id, project, provider, status, last_active)
- Migrations run idempotently in CI and in the Docker image entrypoint
- A rollback/down migration exists for each table
Notes: none
Depends on: Scaffold packages/relay: Fastify + WebSocket server skeleton

issues.title: Implement Redis pub/sub fan-out between nodes and clients
issues.body:
Goal: Route ciphertext session/message events between a node and its connected clients through Redis pub/sub channels, so multiple connections (or relay processes) can share state without direct coupling.
Spec: §5.3 ("fans out session state and messages"), §10.
Acceptance:
- A channel-per-session (or per-device) scheme is implemented and documented
- A message published by one connection is delivered to all other subscribed connections for that session within a test-observable latency bound
- Integration test spins up two WS clients against one relay instance and verifies fan-out
Notes: none
Depends on: Scaffold packages/relay: Fastify + WebSocket server skeleton

issues.title: Implement bounded per-client output queues with drop-oldest backpressure
issues.body:
Goal: Protect fast clients and the supervisor's own persistence from a slow client (e.g. a phone on cellular) by giving each client connection a bounded output queue that drops the oldest buffered message and inserts a resync marker on overflow.
Spec: §7.16 ("Fan-out backpressure: bounded per-client output queues with drop-oldest + a resync marker on overflow").
Acceptance:
- Each WS connection has a configurable max queue depth (highWaterMark-style)
- On overflow, oldest messages are dropped and a single resync marker replaces them, never silently truncating without signaling the client
- A client receiving a resync marker knows to re-fetch current state rather than assume gapless history
- Load test (simulated slow consumer) confirms a fast client's delivery is unaffected by one slow client
Notes: §16 "Fan-out backpressure — Node stream highWaterMark + ring buffer keyed by a seq, or adopt socket.io connectionStateRecovery. (pick one)"
Depends on: Implement Redis pub/sub fan-out between nodes and clients

issues.title: Implement ciphertext blob store endpoints (upload/download by opaque ref)
issues.body:
Goal: Give the relay a generic encrypted-blob store — upload ciphertext, get back an opaque ref, download by ref — that the AMK recovery-code escrow builds on, with a server-side size cap as a second line of defense.
Spec: §5.3, §8 (wrapped-AMK blob upload to the relay), §7.25 (size-capped server-side, referenced pattern).
Acceptance:
- `POST` accepts ciphertext and returns an opaque ref; `GET /:ref` returns the stored ciphertext; the relay never inspects contents
- A per-blob and per-account size cap is enforced server-side, returning a clear error on excess
- Blobs are addressed by an opaque ref, not a predictable/sequential id
- Integration test covers upload, download, and cap rejection
Notes: none
Depends on: Add Postgres schema & migrations for relay core tables

issues.title: Add relay /health endpoint for external uptime monitoring
issues.body:
Goal: Expose a `/health` endpoint reporting relay liveness/readiness (DB and Redis connectivity) so an external uptime check can alert independently of the relay itself.
Spec: §7.21 ("The relay also exposes a /health endpoint watched by an external uptime check, since alerting can't depend on the relay itself").
Acceptance:
- `GET /health` returns 200 with DB+Redis status when healthy, non-200 when either dependency is unreachable
- Response is unauthenticated (safe for an external monitor) and leaks no session data
- Test covers healthy and degraded (DB down / Redis down) cases
Notes: none
Depends on: Scaffold packages/relay: Fastify + WebSocket server skeleton

issues.title: Add per-IP rate limiting and per-account storage quota to the relay
issues.body:
Goal: Protect the public relay endpoint from abuse with per-IP connection/enrollment rate limits and a per-account storage-exhaustion cap on the blob store.
Spec: §8 ("Public-relay abuse limits: per-IP connection/enrollment rate limits and a storage-exhaustion cap protect the public endpoint").
Acceptance:
- `@fastify/rate-limit` (or equivalent) caps connection/enrollment attempts per IP with a configurable window
- A scheduled job (or on-write check) enforces a per-account total blob-storage quota and rejects writes past it with a clear error
- Both limits are configurable via env/config, with sane documented defaults
- Test covers rate-limit rejection and quota rejection
Notes: §16 "Public-relay abuse limits — @fastify/rate-limit (per-IP) + a per-account storage-quota job. (quota is novel)"
Depends on: Implement ciphertext blob store endpoints (upload/download by opaque ref)

issues.title: Implement relay data retention: TTL/size-cap pruning via CLI
issues.body:
Goal: Give the relay a retention policy so old session/blob data doesn't grow unbounded — a TTL and/or size cap, prunable on demand via a CLI command.
Spec: §9 ("Relay data lifecycle: a retention policy (TTL / size cap, prunable via a CLI)").
Acceptance:
- A CLI command (`pnpm --filter @loombox/relay prune`) deletes session metadata and blobs older than a configurable retention window
- Pruning is safe to run repeatedly (idempotent) and logs what it deleted
- A dry-run mode reports what would be deleted without deleting
- Test covers pruning at/near the TTL boundary
Notes: §16 "Retention — scheduled DELETE … WHERE updated_at < now()-retention or partition DROP (blob TTL: S3/MinIO lifecycle)."
Depends on: Add Postgres schema & migrations for relay core tables

issues.title: Implement relay backup/DR: nightly encrypted dump + tested restore
issues.body:
Goal: Protect the relay's Postgres data — the sole copy of session history, device registry, Better Auth tables, and wrapped-AMK escrow blobs — with a nightly encrypted off-box backup and a documented, tested restore procedure.
Spec: §9 ("a backup/DR line — a nightly encrypted dump to off-box storage with a tested restore. The nightly dump also covers the Better Auth tables... and the wrapped-AMK escrow blobs from §8 — without them every user's recovery-code bootstrap path is stranded").
Acceptance:
- A systemd timer (or equivalent scheduler) runs `pg_dump --format=custom`, encrypts the dump (age or gpg), and ships it off-box (rclone/restic)
- The dump explicitly includes the Better Auth tables (`user`/`session`/`account`/`verification`) and the wrapped-AMK escrow blob table
- A documented restore runbook exists and has been executed once against a scratch Postgres to confirm it actually restores
- Backup job failures are logged/alertable, not silent
Notes: §16 "Relay backup/DR — pg_dump --format=custom | age/gpg | rclone/restic off-box on a systemd timer + a restore drill. (novel)"
Depends on: Add Postgres schema & migrations for relay core tables

issues.title: Implement session-ownership lease so two nodes never fight over one supervisor
issues.body:
Goal: Make a session owned by exactly one node at a time via a renewable lease; a second node may attach read-only while the lease is live and reclaim it explicitly once it expires, so a Mac node and a devbox node never race for the same supervisor.
Spec: §9 ("Session ownership across nodes: a session is owned by one node via a renewable lease; a second node may attach read-only while the lease is live and reclaim it on expiry (an explicit action in the PWA)").
Acceptance:
- A lease (Postgres `pg_advisory_lock`-style or Redis `SET NX PX`) is acquired by the owning node and renewed on a heartbeat
- A second node's attach request while the lease is live is served read-only, not given control
- An explicit reclaim action in the protocol lets a node take over a session whose lease has expired
- Test simulates lease expiry (owning node goes silent) and confirms reclaim succeeds only after expiry
Notes: §16 "Session ownership across nodes — Postgres pg_advisory_lock (heartbeat + expiry) or Redis SET NX PX lock. (novel for this app)"
Depends on: Implement Redis pub/sub fan-out between nodes and clients

issues.title: Package the relay as a Docker image with a prodbox compose file
issues.body:
Goal: Ship the relay as a self-hostable Docker image plus a docker-compose file modeled on the operator's existing pitchbox deployment, ready to run behind Caddy on prodbox's public subdomain.
Spec: §5.3, §9, §10 ("shipped as a Docker image with a compose file for prodbox").
Acceptance:
- A production Dockerfile builds a minimal image running the relay against externally configured Postgres/Redis
- A docker-compose file brings up relay + Postgres + Redis, modeled on the pitchbox compose overlay pattern
- Documented env vars cover DB/Redis URLs, VAPID keys, OAuth app credentials, and CORS/origin config
- A fresh `docker compose up` on a clean host reaches the `/health` endpoint green
Notes: §16 "Compose file for prodbox — copy Lorenzo's own pitchbox compose overlays (happy-server ships k8s, not compose)."
Depends on: Add relay /health endpoint for external uptime monitoring

issues.title: Define the versioned Zod wire-protocol schema package
issues.body:
Goal: Create `packages/protocol` — the shared, versioned Zod schema for every node↔relay↔client message — so all three components import one typed source of truth instead of hand-rolled shapes.
Spec: §10 ("a versioned, Zod-typed wire schema shared across node/relay/client as one package"), §15 (packages/protocol).
Acceptance:
- `packages/protocol` exports Zod schemas for the core message envelope and at least the v1 message kinds needed by later relay/node work (device auth, session state, control messages)
- Every schema is versioned (e.g. a `protocolVersion` literal per schema set)
- Both encode and decode paths are covered by Vitest unit tests, including a rejection test for a malformed payload
- Consumed by packages/relay in place of any ad hoc message typing
Notes: none
Depends on: none

issues.title: Implement per-connection protocol version negotiation
issues.body:
Goal: Negotiate the wire-protocol version once per connection — like ACP's `initialize` handshake — so relay, node, and client each declare a supported version range at connect time, and an outdated participant is told to update instead of failing silently.
Spec: §10 ("The protocol version is negotiated once per connection... the relay surfaces 'update required' instead of failing silently (rollout order: relay → nodes → clients)").
Acceptance:
- Connect handshake carries each side's supported version range (min/max)
- On overlap, the negotiated version is pinned for the connection's lifetime; on no overlap, the relay responds with an explicit "update required" message, not a silent close
- Handshake data is designed to ride in the WS handshake auth payload alongside the device-keypair signature (not a separate round trip)
- Test covers: matching versions, node-too-old, client-too-old, relay-too-old
Notes: §16 "Protocol version negotiated once per connection — ACP initialize handshake; carry it in Socket.IO handshake.auth." (loombox's transport is raw WS, not Socket.IO — apply the same handshake-auth pattern.)
Depends on: Define the versioned Zod wire-protocol schema package

issues.title: Wire protocol version negotiation into the relay's WS handshake
issues.body:
Goal: Connect the negotiation logic to the actual relay WS upgrade path, so a real client/node connecting to the relay goes through version negotiation before any session traffic flows.
Spec: §10, §5.3.
Acceptance:
- Relay's WS upgrade handler runs the negotiation handshake before accepting session-level messages
- A connection that fails negotiation is closed with the "update required" message, never silently dropped
- The negotiated version is available to the rest of the connection's message handling for the connection's lifetime
- Integration test connects a fake old-version client against the relay and asserts the update-required response
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement per-connection protocol version negotiation, Scaffold packages/relay: Fastify + WebSocket server skeleton

issues.title: Add protocol schema compatibility test suite
issues.body:
Goal: Guard against accidental breaking changes to the shared wire schema by testing that a payload built against the previous minor schema version still decodes (or is explicitly rejected with a clear error) under the current one.
Spec: §10.
Acceptance:
- A fixture set of serialized payloads per supported protocol version lives in the repo
- CI runs these fixtures against the current schema and fails on an unintentional silent shape change
- A deliberate breaking change requires updating the fixtures in the same PR (documented in the package README)
Notes: none
Depends on: Define the versioned Zod wire-protocol schema package

issues.title: Build packages/crypto: ECDH P-256 keys + AES-256-GCM AAD-bound envelopes
issues.body:
Goal: Implement the shared E2E crypto primitives — ECDH P-256 keypair generation, AES-256-GCM encrypt/decrypt, and an envelope format that binds ciphertext to its resource id via AAD to prevent a swap/spoof attack.
Spec: §8 ("Per-device end-to-end encryption... ECDH P-256... envelopes still bind ciphertext to its resource id (AAD)"), §10 ("vetted primitives (tweetnacl/libsodium-class + AES-256-GCM), per-device ECDH key wrapping").
Acceptance:
- `packages/crypto` exports keypair generation, ECDH shared-secret derivation, and AES-256-GCM encrypt/decrypt with AAD
- An envelope helper takes (resourceId, plaintext, key) and produces ciphertext bound to that resourceId; decrypting with the wrong resourceId as AAD fails loudly
- Uses one curve only (P-256), not blended with X25519, per the grounding note
- Vitest covers round-trip encrypt/decrypt, AAD-mismatch rejection, and a known-answer test vector
Notes: §16 "E2E envelope crypto — nimbalyst TrackerEnvelopeCrypto.ts/ECDHKeyManager.ts (P-256/WebCrypto, AAD-bound) — pick ONE curve (don't blend with happy's X25519/tweetnacl); the spec's 'ECDH P-256' is grounded in nimbalyst only."
Depends on: none

issues.title: Implement Account Master Key generation and HMAC-SHA512 key-tree derivation
issues.body:
Goal: Implement the Account Master Key (AMK) — a 256-bit key generated once per account — and the HMAC-SHA512 BIP32-style key-tree derivation that lets any device holding the AMK derive every session/resource key by itself.
Spec: §8 ("each account holds one 256-bit Account Master Key (AMK)... every session/resource key is derived from the AMK via an HMAC-SHA512 BIP32-style key tree").
Acceptance:
- AMK generation produces 256 bits of cryptographically secure random data
- `deriveChild(chainCode, data)` implements `hmac_sha512(chainCode, data)` per Happy's construction, not RFC 5869 HKDF
- Deriving the same (AMK, path) twice yields the same key; different paths yield independent keys
- Vitest includes a known-answer test vector and a test asserting this output is NOT HKDF-compatible
Notes: §16 "AMK-derivation mechanism — Happy's actual key-tree construction, deriveSecretKeyTreeRoot/deriveSecretKeyTreeChild (happy/packages/happy-app/sources/encryption/deriveKey.ts:8-30), an HMAC-SHA512 BIP32-style tree (hmac_sha512(chainCode, data) per child), not RFC 5869 HKDF."
Depends on: Build packages/crypto: ECDH P-256 keys + AES-256-GCM AAD-bound envelopes

issues.title: Implement the device registry (schema, endpoints, revoke/rotate)
issues.body:
Goal: Build the device registry that both device-lifecycle bootstrap paths converge on — id, public key, status, last-seen, and owner_account_id — plus relay endpoints to register, list, and revoke a device.
Spec: §8 ("Both paths converge on the same device registry (id, public key, status, last-seen, now also owner_account_id)... Revoking a device removes it from the registry and rotates...").
Acceptance:
- Registry table/endpoints exist for register, list-for-account, mark-last-seen, and revoke
- Revoking a device sets its status and excludes it from future fan-out/wrap-fan-out immediately
- `owner_account_id` is populated at registration time from the authenticated account (nullable until account login lands, wired fully by the account-scoping issue)
- Test covers register → list → revoke → confirm a revoked device can't reconnect
Notes: none
Depends on: Add Postgres schema & migrations for relay core tables, Build packages/crypto: ECDH P-256 keys + AES-256-GCM AAD-bound envelopes

issues.title: Implement QR/short-code device pairing (fast path)
issues.body:
Goal: Let an already-trusted device display a QR/short-code that hands a new device a wrapped copy of the AMK directly, for the common case of two devices being physically together.
Spec: §8 ("QR/short-code pairing (fast path, unchanged mechanism)... strictly better UX than typing a code, so it stays the recommended path whenever it's available").
Acceptance:
- An existing trusted device can generate a short-lived pairing code/QR payload containing what a new device needs to fetch and unwrap the AMK
- The new device's registration flow, given the pairing payload, ends up holding the AMK and a registered device keypair without any Recovery Code entry
- Pairing payload expires after a short window and is single-use
- Test covers the full pair → register → AMK-held sequence and expiry/reuse rejection
Notes: §16 "QR device pairing — happy hooks/useConnectTerminal.ts + ui/auth.ts (the audit's authQRStart.ts cite was wrong)."; "Short-code pairing — RFC 8628 (OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant); no in-repo precedent."
Depends on: Implement Account Master Key generation and HMAC-SHA512 key-tree derivation, Implement the device registry (schema, endpoints, revoke/rotate)

issues.title: Implement recovery-code AMK escrow: generation, wrap, and relay storage
issues.body:
Goal: Implement the default no-device-to-scan-from bootstrap path — generate a high-entropy Recovery Code, wrap the AMK under a key derived from it, and store the wrapped blob on the relay under the OAuth-authenticated account.
Spec: §8 ("Recovery-code escrow (the new default for 'no device to scan from')... the wrapped-AMK blob is uploaded to the relay under the OAuth-authenticated account; per the ciphertext-only rule the relay only ever stores ciphertext of the AMK, never the AMK or the code itself").
Acceptance:
- Recovery Code is generated with the same legibility formatting as a 1Password secret key (base32, dash-grouped)
- The AMK is wrapped with AES-256-GCM under a key derived from the Recovery Code and uploaded to the relay's blob store as ciphertext only
- Given the same Recovery Code, unwrapping recovers the original AMK; given a wrong code, unwrapping fails
- Vitest covers wrap/unwrap round trip and wrong-code rejection; the "I saved this" confirmation UI is out of scope for this issue (belongs to the PWA client epic)
Notes: §16 "Account Master Key escrow shape — Signal's PIN + Secure Value Recovery precedent... adapted to a high-entropy generated code"; "Recovery-code formatting reused as-is: secretKeyBackup.ts:81-102 (formatSecretKeyForBackup, base32 dash-grouped, 1Password-secret-key-style)."
Depends on: Implement Account Master Key generation and HMAC-SHA512 key-tree derivation, Implement ciphertext blob store endpoints (upload/download by opaque ref)

issues.title: Implement new-device bootstrap via recovery code (relay + unwrap logic)
issues.body:
Goal: Implement the new-device side of recovery-code bootstrap: after OAuth login, fetch the account's wrapped-AMK blob, unwrap it locally with a user-entered Recovery Code, and register the new device's own ECDH keypair into the registry.
Spec: §8 ("New-device bootstrap: OAuth login (proves identity, no QR, no other device involved) → client fetches its account's wrapped-AMK blob over that session → user enters the Recovery Code once → unwrap locally → device holds the AMK → generates its own device ECDH P-256 keypair and registers into the device registry").
Acceptance:
- An authenticated (OAuth) session can fetch its account's wrapped-AMK blob from the relay
- Given the Recovery Code, the fetched blob unwraps to the original AMK client-side
- On successful unwrap, a new device keypair is generated and registered with `owner_account_id` set from the session
- No previously-trusted device needs to be online for this flow; test simulates a fresh device with only OAuth + Recovery Code
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement recovery-code AMK escrow: generation, wrap, and relay storage, Mount Better Auth on the relay with Postgres-backed OAuth login

issues.title: Implement device revocation with AMK epoch rotation and wrap-fan-out
issues.body:
Goal: When a device is revoked, mint a fresh AMK epoch (not derivable from the old one) and ECDH-wrap it for every other currently registered device, depositing the wrapped envelopes on the relay for those devices to fetch on next connect — so the revoked device cannot recompute future keys.
Spec: §8 ("Revoking a device removes it from the registry and rotates: the acting (already-unlocked, online) device mints a new AMK epoch from fresh random entropy (not derivable from the old AMK...) and ECDH-wraps that new epoch for each other currently registered device's already-known public key").
Acceptance:
- Revoke action generates a new AMK epoch from fresh entropy, independent of the prior epoch
- The new epoch is ECDH-wrapped per remaining registered device and stored as a fetchable envelope keyed to that device
- On next connect, each remaining device fetches and unwraps its envelope and continues operating on the new epoch
- The revoked device's old AMK cannot derive the new epoch (test asserts this directly)
Notes: §16 "Revoke + rotate keys — compose happy machinesRoutes.ts DELETE with nimbalyst archiveCurrentOrgKey+generateAndStoreOrgKey; add a relay-rebuild re-enrollment integration test. (composition is novel)"
Depends on: Implement the device registry (schema, endpoints, revoke/rotate), Implement Account Master Key generation and HMAC-SHA512 key-tree derivation

issues.title: Add a relay-rebuild re-enrollment integration test
issues.body:
Goal: Prove the disaster-recovery path actually works: after the relay's data is wiped/rebuilt, existing nodes and clients can re-enroll from their own keypairs plus a fresh AMK-escrow round trip, with no server-held plaintext of the AMK or content at any point.
Spec: §8 ("if the relay is rebuilt, nodes/clients re-enroll from their existing keypairs plus a fresh AMK-escrow round trip. There is still no server-held plaintext of the AMK or content, by design"), §14 (the lost-device/rebuilt-relay drill v1's AMK design must survive).
Acceptance:
- Test scenario: populate a relay, capture device state, wipe the relay's DB, restore only the backup produced by the backup/DR issue, and confirm devices can reconnect
- A second scenario simulates a relay rebuilt from zero (no backup) and confirms a device can still re-bootstrap via Recovery Code without any prior server data
- At no point in either scenario does a test assertion find plaintext AMK or session content server-side
- Test is part of CI (or a documented manual drill runbook if too heavy for CI)
Notes: §16 "add a relay-rebuild re-enrollment integration test. (composition is novel)"
Depends on: Implement device revocation with AMK epoch rotation and wrap-fan-out, Implement relay backup/DR: nightly encrypted dump + tested restore

issues.title: Implement node-side secrets-at-rest via native OS keyring
issues.body:
Goal: Store SSH keys, provider tokens, and per-project secrets on the node using OS-native secure storage (`@napi-rs/keyring`) rather than plaintext files, with a documented fallback for a headless box with no keyring session.
Spec: §8 ("Secrets at rest on the node use OS-native storage via a native keyring binding usable from a headless Node daemon (@napi-rs/keyring...) — macOS Keychain, libsecret/keyring on Linux; on a headless box with no keyring session, fall back to permission-scoped encryption tied to the node's own keypair. Covers SSH keys, provider tokens, and per-project secrets").
Acceptance:
- A secrets-storage module wraps `@napi-rs/keyring` for get/set/delete, covering macOS Keychain and Linux libsecret/keyring
- On a headless box with no keyring session, the module falls back to permission-scoped encryption (0600 file) tied to the node's own keypair, and this fallback is explicitly logged (not silent)
- The fail-closed-vs-fallback decision is documented in the package README, per the open grounding question
- Test covers both the keyring path (mocked) and the headless-fallback path
Notes: §16 "Secrets at rest — @napi-rs/keyring (headless Node; not Electron safeStorage); decide fail-closed vs 0600-file fallback."
Depends on: none

issues.title: Mount Better Auth on the relay with Postgres-backed OAuth login
issues.body:
Goal: Embed Better Auth in-process on the relay's Fastify server at `/api/auth/*`, sharing the relay's own Postgres, adding the `user`/`session`/`account`/`verification` tables Better Auth needs.
Spec: §8 ("loombox runs Better Auth (MIT, self-hosted, embeddable TS library) mounted in-process on the relay's existing Fastify server at /api/auth/*, sharing the relay's own Postgres (adds user/session/account/verification tables alongside the relay's existing blob/metadata tables — no separate auth service to operate)").
Acceptance:
- Better Auth is mounted at `/api/auth/*` on the existing relay Fastify instance (no separate auth service/process)
- Better Auth's Postgres tables are created via the relay's own migration tooling
- `multiSession` plugin is deliberately NOT enabled (single-account v1 scope), documented as a conscious choice
- Vitest/integration test hits `/api/auth/*` and confirms a session is created end to end (a test/dev provider or mocked provider callback is acceptable)
Notes: §16 "OAuth login, self-hosted — Better Auth (MIT): Fastify mounting recipe (docs/content/docs/integrations/fastify.mdx)..." — consult context7 `/better-auth/better-auth` for the exact mounting recipe before implementing.
Depends on: Scaffold packages/relay: Fastify + WebSocket server skeleton

issues.title: Configure Google and GitHub OAuth social login providers
issues.body:
Goal: Wire Better Auth's `socialProviders.google` and `socialProviders.github` for loombox's own identity login (distinct from a connected GitHub/Jira account, §7.26), using narrow identity-only scopes.
Spec: §8 ("Login is via Google or GitHub OAuth (socialProviders.google/socialProviders.github, Better Auth's /api/auth/callback/:provider convention)... this is a narrower login than a self-hoster might expect: it registers loombox's own OAuth App/Client with Google/GitHub for identity only (read:user user:email-class scopes)").
Acceptance:
- Both providers are configured via env-supplied client id/secret, with documented setup steps for a self-hoster to register their own OAuth Apps
- `/api/auth/callback/:provider` completes login for both Google and GitHub in a manual/integration test
- Requested scopes are limited to identity-only (no `repo` or other connected-account-style scopes) — explicitly distinct from §7.26's connected-account OAuth
- Login failure (denied consent, provider error) surfaces a clear error, not a silent hang
Notes: §16 "Provider-side OAuth app setup — GitHub OAuth Apps... Google OAuth 2.0 web-server flow."
Depends on: Mount Better Auth on the relay with Postgres-backed OAuth login

issues.title: Add Better Auth Bearer plugin for non-cookie clients (WS handshake auth)
issues.body:
Goal: Enable Better Auth's Bearer plugin so a non-cookie client (the relay's own WebSocket handshake, a future Capacitor shell) can convert an `Authorization: Bearer` header into an internal session, riding in the WS handshake alongside the device-keypair signature.
Spec: §8 ("a non-cookie client... authenticates via Better Auth's Bearer plugin — deliberately not the JWT plugin... Bearer converts an Authorization: Bearer header straight into an internal session, which is exactly the shape the relay's WS handshake needs — riding in handshake.auth alongside, not instead of, the existing device-keypair signature").
Acceptance:
- Bearer plugin is enabled and a bearer token obtained from a browser session can be exchanged for the same internal session Better Auth would give a cookie-based request
- The relay's WS handshake accepts a bearer token in its auth payload alongside the device-keypair signature, validating both, not replacing one with the other
- The JWT plugin is explicitly not used, with a one-line rationale comment/doc referencing Better Auth's own guidance
- Test covers WS connect with valid bearer + valid device signature, and rejection when either is missing/invalid
Notes: §16 (Login, accounts & key custody) — Bearer plugin (`plugins/bearer.mdx`), chosen deliberately over JWT. Consult context7 `/better-auth/better-auth` for the plugin's exact config surface.
Depends on: Mount Better Auth on the relay with Postgres-backed OAuth login, Wire protocol version negotiation into the relay's WS handshake

issues.title: Bridge OAuth identity to account-scoped session-list metadata
issues.body:
Goal: Populate the device registry's `owner_account_id` at pairing/bootstrap time from the logged-in Better Auth `user.id`, and add a `GET /api/devices` (or `/api/sessions`) endpoint filtered by the caller's account — the one deliberate, bounded exception to "ciphertext only."
Spec: §8 ("The bridge, and the one narrow exception to 'ciphertext only.' The device registry gains an owner_account_id column, populated once at pairing/bootstrap time from the logged-in Better Auth user.id. A GET /api/devices (or /api/sessions) call, authorized by the Better Auth cookie session or Bearer token, filters WHERE owner_account_id = session.user.id").
Acceptance:
- Device/session registration sets `owner_account_id` from the authenticated Better Auth user at bootstrap time
- `GET /api/devices` (and/or `/api/sessions`) requires a valid Better Auth cookie or Bearer session and returns only rows matching the caller's `owner_account_id`
- Returned fields are limited to session existence/metadata (name, project, provider, status, last-active) — never session content
- Test confirms account A never sees account B's devices/sessions even with a valid session of its own
Notes: none
Depends on: Add Better Auth Bearer plugin for non-cookie clients (WS handshake auth), Implement the device registry (schema, endpoints, revoke/rotate)

issues.title: Decide and document account-scoped session-metadata storage: plaintext vs. encrypted
issues.body:
Goal: Make the explicit call the spec flags as open — whether account-scoped session-list metadata is stored in the relay's Postgres in cleartext (v1-simple, the spec's recommendation) or encrypted to a per-account shared key — and record the decision and rationale before implementation drifts into one by default.
Spec: §14 ("Account-scoped session-list metadata storage: is it stored in the relay's Postgres in cleartext (v1-simple, recommended — §8 already accepts this as a bounded, explicit exception to 'ciphertext only') or encrypted to a per-account shared key (stronger, adds real complexity for arguably little gain...)? A decision to make explicitly, not fall into.").
Acceptance:
- A short decision doc (in the relay or protocol package README, or `docs/decisions/`) states the chosen approach and why
- If cleartext is chosen, the doc explicitly states this is a bounded, disclosed exception to ciphertext-only, matching §8's bridge bullet
- The chosen approach matches what "Bridge OAuth identity to account-scoped session-list metadata" actually implements (no drift between decision and code)
Notes: none
Depends on: none

issues.title: Decide and document OAuth app registration model: self-registered vs. centralized broker
issues.body:
Goal: Make the explicit call the spec flags as open — whether each self-hoster registers their own Google/GitHub OAuth Apps (and later, a Jira 3LO app) or loombox centralizes a hosted broker the way emdash does — and record the decision.
Spec: §14 ("OAuth broker centralization vs. self-registered apps: emdash's own login/link flows go through a centrally-run hosted broker it operates (auth.emdash.sh)... Decide explicitly whether each self-hoster registers their own Google/GitHub OAuth Apps and Jira 3LO app... or loombox centralizes a broker... record the choice here rather than defaulting into one silently.").
Acceptance:
- A short decision doc states the chosen model and the tradeoff against §3/§11's self-hosting thesis
- Setup documentation for a self-hoster matches the chosen model (e.g. if self-registered, step-by-step OAuth App creation instructions exist for Google and GitHub)
- The decision is referenced from "Configure Google and GitHub OAuth social login providers"'s implementation
Notes: none
Depends on: none

issues.title: Scaffold the SvelteKit PWA app shell with manifest and service worker
issues.body:
Goal: Stand up apps/web as an installable PWA (SvelteKit + adapter, web manifest, registered service worker caching the static app shell) that the rest of the client epics build on.
Spec: SS5.4, SS10 (SvelteKit PWA stack), SS16 build-and-client grounding (`@vite-pwa/sveltekit`, hapi `web/src/sw.ts` as a hand-rolled SW example).
Acceptance:
- apps/web builds and passes lint/typecheck/test in CI
- a web manifest and icon set make the app installable (Lighthouse installability check passes)
- a service worker registers and serves the static shell offline
- Playwright smoke test loads the shell and asserts the SW registered
Notes: SS16 flags this needs a v0/v1 spike (adapter-node + PWA) before committing to the pattern.
Depends on: none

issues.title: Build app navigation and cross-node session list bound to relay metadata
issues.body:
Goal: Render the primary navigation shell and a session list that lists every session across all of the user's nodes and targets, sourced from the relay's account-scoped session-list metadata.
Spec: SS5.4 ("lists all sessions across all of the user's nodes/targets"), SS8's bridge bullet (account-scoped session-list metadata visible on OAuth login alone).
Acceptance:
- session list shows project, provider, target, and status per session across all nodes
- list updates live as session status changes
- empty and loading states are handled
- works with a stub/mock relay feed if the real relay auth isn't ready yet, behind a typed data interface
Notes: none
Depends on: Scaffold the SvelteKit PWA app shell with manifest and service worker

issues.title: Build the live session view streaming transcript output
issues.body:
Goal: Open a session from the list into a live view that renders the streaming session/update fan-out for that session.
Spec: SS5.4 ("view live output"), SS7.3 ("watch live output").
Acceptance:
- opening a session subscribes to its live update stream and renders incoming events
- reconnect re-attaches and replays buffered output without duplicating already-rendered items
- view degrades gracefully (clear reconnecting state) on a dropped relay connection
Notes: renders through the transcript reducer built in the Agent transcript epic; this issue owns the session-view shell and subscription plumbing, not the reducer itself.
Depends on: Build app navigation and cross-node session list bound to relay metadata

issues.title: Build the follow-up prompt composer with mid-turn queueing
issues.body:
Goal: Add a composer that stays enabled while a turn is streaming; a submitted prompt during an active turn queues as the next turn and renders in the transcript in a pending "queued" state rather than interrupting or blocking.
Spec: SS7.3, SS7.24 mid-turn composer state bullet.
Acceptance:
- composer accepts input and submits while a turn is in flight
- a mid-turn submission shows as "queued" in the transcript until the agent picks it up
- this is visibly distinct from the explicit Stop action
Notes: none
Depends on: Build the live session view streaming transcript output

issues.title: Implement Stop/interrupt distinct from rollback
issues.body:
Goal: Add a one-tap Stop control on desktop and mobile that cancels the in-flight agent turn outright, distinct from post-hoc checkpoint rollback.
Spec: SS7.3 ("Stop/interrupt any running agent turn with one tap... distinct from post-hoc rollback").
Acceptance:
- Stop is reachable from the live session view on desktop and mobile
- pressing Stop cancels the current turn via the ACP cancellation path
- Stop does not touch workspace state (no rollback behavior bundled in)
Notes: multi-request permission-queue cancellation semantics on Stop are covered by a separate transcript-epic issue; this issue is the button and the turn-cancel call itself.
Depends on: Build the follow-up prompt composer with mid-turn queueing

issues.title: Implement IndexedDB offline composer outbox
issues.body:
Goal: Let a follow-up prompt composed while offline queue locally and send automatically on reconnect, rather than being lost or blocking the composer.
Spec: SS7.3 ("a follow-up prompt composed offline queues and sends on reconnect").
Acceptance:
- a prompt submitted with no relay connection is stored in an IndexedDB outbox and shown as pending in the composer/transcript
- on reconnect, queued prompts send in order automatically
- outbox survives a full page reload
Notes: SS16 grounding: IndexedDB outbox, not the Background Sync API, since iOS/Safari doesn't support Background Sync.
Depends on: Build the follow-up prompt composer with mid-turn queueing

issues.title: Implement stale approve/deny discard handling
issues.body:
Goal: When an approve/deny action is submitted for a tool call that has already expired or been resolved on another device, discard it with a "no longer applies" message instead of silently applying it.
Spec: SS7.3 ("a stale approve/deny is discarded with a 'no longer applies' note rather than silently applied").
Acceptance:
- resolving the same permission request from two devices results in the second action being shown as stale, not double-applied
- the stale note is visible on the device that submitted the late action
- covered by a test that simulates two clients racing the same request id
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement inline FIFO tool-call permission queue with provider-adapted button sets

issues.title: Build fuzzy command palette and keyboard shortcuts
issues.body:
Goal: Add a fuzzy jump-to-session/project quick-switcher plus keyboard shortcuts for common actions, as a cross-cutting requirement of the companion UX.
Spec: SS7.3 ("Keyboard & command palette are a cross-cutting requirement").
Acceptance:
- a global shortcut opens a fuzzy quick-switcher over sessions/projects
- at least the common actions (open session, stop, approve/deny focus) have bound shortcuts
- shortcuts are documented in one discoverable place (palette itself or a help overlay)
Notes: none
Depends on: Build app navigation and cross-node session list bound to relay metadata

issues.title: Add touch-optimized permission and plan controls with haptic feedback
issues.body:
Goal: On a coarse (touch) pointer, enlarge hit targets for tool-call cards, permission buttons, and plan checklist items, and trigger haptic feedback (Vibration API) specifically on confirm/deny taps since those are irreversible.
Spec: SS7.3 ("Touch affordances for transcript widgets").
Acceptance:
- `pointer: coarse` media query drives a larger hit-target variant for these controls
- confirm/deny taps trigger the Vibration API where available, with a no-op fallback where it isn't
- verified on at least one mobile viewport in Playwright/manual test
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement inline FIFO tool-call permission queue with provider-adapted button sets

issues.title: Implement narrow-viewport permission footer and scrollable option lists
issues.body:
Goal: On a narrow viewport, collapse the permission card's button row to its two primary actions plus an overflow control for the rest of that provider's option set, and cap the height of any inline option list (model picker, long `options[]` list) so it scrolls internally instead of pushing actionable buttons off-screen.
Spec: SS7.3 ("Narrow-viewport permission footer", "Scrollable option lists").
Acceptance:
- below a defined width breakpoint, the permission card shows two primary buttons plus an overflow menu
- an option list longer than the visible area scrolls internally, primary buttons stay visible
- covered by a Playwright test at a phone viewport width
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement inline FIFO tool-call permission queue with provider-adapted button sets

issues.title: Implement the append-by-id transcript reducer for ACP v1 message/thought chunks
issues.body:
Goal: Build the single reducer that turns ACP v1's `user_message_chunk`/`agent_message_chunk`/`agent_thought_chunk` streams into transcript items, appending to an existing item on a repeated `messageId` and starting a new one on a new id. The same reducer must run identically for a live stream and for replayed history on reconnect.
Spec: SS7.24 ("One reducer, append-only by id").
Acceptance:
- a same-id chunk sequence appends into one transcript item; a new id starts a new item
- transcript-item ids are scoped by turn plus kind, not raw `messageId` alone, so a provider reusing an id across a thought and a message in the same turn doesn't collide
- the identical reducer function drives both live streaming and reconnect replay (one code path, unit-tested against both)
- a fixture-based test covers interleaved message/thought chunks from a captured Claude Code and Codex transcript
Notes: forward-compat note in the spec: do not build this around upsert/patch semantics; ACP v2's `user_message`/`agent_message`/`agent_thought` patch-replace variants are a separate future item.
Depends on: none (assumes the providers/core ACP session/update event stream exists; providers epic, out of this scope)

issues.title: Implement thinking/reasoning display with live and settled headers
issues.body:
Goal: Render a live "Thinking Ns" ticking header while `agent_thought_chunk` streams for a turn, settling to a static "Thought for Ns" the instant real message content starts arriving, without blocking or delaying the answer render. Collapsed by default once done, expandable on tap, visually muted from assistant text.
Spec: SS7.24 thinking/reasoning bullet.
Acceptance:
- the ticking timer starts on first thought chunk and stops the instant the first message chunk for that turn arrives
- thought content is collapsed by default post-turn and expandable
- reasoning display never delays or blocks the message content rendering behind it
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement the append-by-id transcript reducer for ACP v1 message/thought chunks

issues.title: Decouple streaming render rate from chunk arrival with a smoothing buffer
issues.body:
Goal: Add a small smoothing buffer between chunk arrival and render/animation rate so a fast, low-latency agent doesn't cause per-frame jank, and keep transcript-item ids stable across ticks so a virtualized transcript never remounts a row mid-stream and loses scroll position.
Spec: SS7.24 streaming mechanics bullet.
Acceptance:
- a synthetic high-frequency chunk stream does not visibly jank the UI (frame-rate check or a manual perf pass documented in the PR)
- item ids remain stable across ticks in the virtualized list; a mid-stream scroll position is preserved by an automated test
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement the append-by-id transcript reducer for ACP v1 message/thought chunks

issues.title: Render mid-turn queued prompts inside the transcript
issues.body:
Goal: Render a prompt submitted mid-turn (from the composer) as a pending "queued" transcript item until the agent picks it up as the next turn.
Spec: SS7.24 mid-turn composer state bullet.
Acceptance:
- a queued prompt appears in the transcript in a visually distinct pending state
- the item transitions out of "queued" once the agent starts the next turn
Notes: shared feature with the PWA composer issue; this issue owns the transcript-side rendering of that state.
Depends on: Implement the append-by-id transcript reducer for ACP v1 message/thought chunks, Build the follow-up prompt composer with mid-turn queueing

issues.title: Build the tier-1 bespoke tool-call widget table (Claude + Codex)
issues.body:
Goal: Build the per-provider, per-tool-name bespoke widget table for the tools worth custom rendering in v1: Claude Code's Edit/Write/Bash/TodoWrite and Codex's patch/diff/bash. Wrap each widget in its own error boundary so one bad widget can't take down the transcript.
Spec: SS7.24 tool-calls tier-1 bullet.
Acceptance:
- each listed tool name renders through its own bespoke widget, not the generic fallback
- every widget is wrapped in an error boundary; a thrown error in one widget doesn't crash the rest of the transcript (tested by forcing a throw)
- widgets render correctly from both a live `tool_call_update` stream and replayed history
Notes: SS16 grounding: nimbalyst `CustomToolWidgets/` and `ToolWidgetErrorBoundary.tsx` for the tier/error-boundary pattern.
Depends on: Implement the append-by-id transcript reducer for ACP v1 message/thought chunks

issues.title: Build the generic ToolKind fallback tool-call row
issues.body:
Goal: Build the generic `ToolKind`-driven fallback row (read/edit/delete/move/search/execute/think/fetch/other) for any tool call without a bespoke widget, suppressed for a call already covered by a bespoke diff/edit widget so streaming never briefly shows a duplicate placeholder.
Spec: SS7.24 tool-calls tier-2 bullet.
Acceptance:
- a tool call with no matching bespoke widget renders through the generic row keyed on its `ToolKind`
- a tool call that does have a bespoke widget never also renders the generic row, including mid-stream before the bespoke widget's first paint
- this is the guaranteed baseline for the generic ACP adapter tier with no per-tool-name knowledge
Notes: none
Depends on: Build the tier-1 bespoke tool-call widget table (Claude + Codex)

issues.title: Build the diff viewer for ACP v1 Diff blocks
issues.body:
Goal: Build a client-side diff viewer for ACP v1's `{path, oldText, newText}` Diff shape, with line diffing and syntax-aware coloring, falling back to structural-only rendering (still a real diff card, not blank) when patch text is absent for binary/symlink changes.
Spec: SS7.24 diffs bullet.
Acceptance:
- a text diff renders with line-level highlighting and language-aware syntax coloring
- a binary/symlink change (no oldText/newText) still renders a structural diff card, never a blank one
- the same component is reused by the working-tree diff viewer in the Editor epic, not duplicated
Notes: v1 is `{path, oldText, newText}` only; the richer `changes[]`/operation shape is ACP v2, gate it as a future item.
Depends on: Build the tier-1 bespoke tool-call widget table (Claude + Codex)

issues.title: Build the display-only tool-call terminal component
issues.body:
Goal: Reuse the interactive terminal component to render a tool call's terminal content (as opposed to a user-opened terminal), buffering partial UTF-8/ANSI escape sequences across output chunks rather than decoding chunk-by-chunk.
Spec: SS7.24 display-only terminals bullet.
Acceptance:
- a streamed tool-call terminal output renders correctly even when a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence or ANSI escape is split across chunks
- the same terminal rendering component backs both this display-only mode and the interactive terminal from the Integrated terminals epic, not a fork of it
Notes: none
Depends on: Build the tier-1 bespoke tool-call widget table (Claude + Codex), Build the PTY terminal component wired to a relay-proxied terminal session

issues.title: Render the current plan inline in the transcript
issues.body:
Goal: Render the session's single current plan (ACP replaces the whole entry list on every update, never diffed client-side) inline in the transcript at the point it was emitted, as a collapsible checklist that shimmers while the turn generating it is still active.
Spec: SS7.24 plans bullet (inline portion).
Acceptance:
- a `plan_update` replaces the rendered plan wholesale, never merged/diffed against the previous one
- the inline plan card shimmers while its generating turn is active and settles once done
- the card is collapsible and remembers collapse state during the session
Notes: the persistent per-session sidebar view of the same plan data is a separate v2 issue; this issue is the inline rendering only.
Depends on: Implement the append-by-id transcript reducer for ACP v1 message/thought chunks

issues.title: Implement the inline FIFO tool-call permission queue
issues.body:
Goal: Build the per-session FIFO permission queue, rendered inline at the tool-call/composer site as one focused card at a time, never a blocking modal, with provider-adapted button sets and verbs (Claude's Allow-once/Allow-all-edits/Bypass-everything/Allow-for-session/Deny; Codex's Yes/Yes-for-session/Stop-and-explain; the generic tier's plain Allow/Deny plus "always"), rendering fields directly off the request's `toolCall` (title, rawInput, content, locations).
Spec: SS7.24 tool-call permissions bullet (core).
Acceptance:
- pending requests for a session queue FIFO and only one is focused/actionable at a time
- the rendered card never blocks interaction with a different session
- button set/verbs match the resolved provider adapter; the generic ACP tier maps `options[]`/`kind` (allow_once/allow_always/reject_once/reject_always) onto Allow/Deny(+always)
- card content is read straight off `toolCall`, not re-derived, so mobile approval cards show the real command/diff
Notes: none
Depends on: Build the tier-1 bespoke tool-call widget table (Claude + Codex), Build the generic ToolKind fallback tool-call row

issues.title: Wire the permission queue as the single source of truth for cross-session surfacing
issues.body:
Goal: Make the per-session FIFO permission queue the single source of truth that both the session view and the cross-project attention inbox render from, so resolving a request from either surface resolves it everywhere and an already-resolved request disappears from the inbox rather than going stale.
Spec: SS7.24 "Cross-session surfacing" bullet, SS7.13.
Acceptance:
- resolving a request from the inbox updates the session view immediately and vice versa
- a request resolved on another device is removed from the inbox rather than left actionable
- covered by a test simulating resolution from two different UI entry points
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement the inline FIFO tool-call permission queue, Build the unified attention-inbox view aggregating approvals, session outcomes, CI failures and reviews

issues.title: Implement permission-queue nested-visibility rule
issues.body:
Goal: Ensure a pending permission request whose tool call sits inside a collapsed group forces that group's ancestor chain open, and always additionally surfaces a copy of the card at the top-level composer site regardless of collapse state, so a pending approval is never hidden behind a fold.
Spec: SS7.24 "Nested visibility" bullet.
Acceptance:
- a pending request nested inside a collapsed ancestor auto-expands that ancestor chain
- a composer-site copy of the pending card is always visible independent of any collapse state
- behaves correctly as a no-op in v1's flat list (no nesting yet) and is exercised for real once subagent trees ship in v2
Notes: this rule is explicitly called out as v1 scope in the roadmap even though nested groups themselves (subagent trees) are v2; build the hook now so it composes cleanly with that later work.
Depends on: Implement the inline FIFO tool-call permission queue

issues.title: Implement Stop and multi-request permission-queue ordering rules
issues.body:
Goal: On Stop, resolve every open permission request in that session as cancelled immediately and optimistically, without waiting for the agent's own update. On a plain Deny of one request while others from the same turn are still queued, leave the rest queued and surface them one by one as normal.
Spec: SS7.24 "Multi-request ordering" bullet.
Acceptance:
- pressing Stop clears every open request for that session to a cancelled state with no spinner surviving past the press
- denying one request from a batch does not auto-resolve sibling requests from the same turn
- covered by a test with 3+ simultaneously queued requests
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement the inline FIFO tool-call permission queue, Implement Stop/interrupt distinct from rollback

issues.title: Implement permission-card keyboard shortcuts
issues.body:
Goal: Bind digit keys 1..n to the focused permission request's own `options[]` in order, and Esc to defer the request (leave it queued, move focus away) without resolving it.
Spec: SS7.24 "Keyboard shortcuts" bullet.
Acceptance:
- pressing a digit key resolves the focused card with the matching option in `options[]` order
- Esc removes focus from the card without resolving it, and the request stays queued
- shortcuts only fire while a permission card is focused, not globally
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement the inline FIFO tool-call permission queue

issues.title: Build the model/mode/reasoning-effort bar bound to ACP config options
issues.body:
Goal: Build one persistent bar next to the composer, bound directly to the session's negotiated ACP config options: `model` and `model_config` options, `thought_level` as its own selector, `mode` as a segmented control, and a per-session context/cost meter anchored at the end. Always re-render the full control set from the complete config-option list on any change, including an unprompted `config_option_update` (e.g. an automatic model downgrade after a rate limit), which must also land in the attention inbox. An unrecognized config-option category still renders generically rather than being hidden.
Spec: SS7.24 "Model, mode & reasoning effort" bullet.
Acceptance:
- the bar renders `model`/`model_config`/`thought_level`/`mode` from the session's negotiated options, not hardcoded per provider
- a user change and an unprompted `config_option_update` both trigger a full re-render of the control set, never a single-control patch
- an automatic fallback event (e.g. cheaper-model switch) also creates an attention-inbox item
- an unknown config-option category renders with a generic control rather than being dropped
- a minimal per-session context-fill percentage meter is rendered at the end of the bar, excluding any usage_update attributable to a subagent tool call
Notes: the full aggregate spend/cost dashboard (SS7.9) is a separate v2 usage-monitoring epic; this issue only needs the live per-session meter this bar requires.
Depends on: Implement the append-by-id transcript reducer for ACP v1 message/thought chunks

issues.title: Add copy/export affordance to every transcript item
issues.body:
Goal: Add a copy affordance (icon on hover/long-press) to every transcript item, a diff, a raw tool command/output, a thought, or a message, across both the bespoke-widget tier and the generic fallback tier.
Spec: SS7.24 "Copy & export" bullet.
Acceptance:
- every transcript item kind exposes a working copy action, tested for at least one bespoke widget and the generic fallback row
- the affordance is reachable on both hover (desktop) and long-press (touch)
Notes: SS16 grounding: emdash `chat-ui/CopyButton.tsx`.
Depends on: Build the tier-1 bespoke tool-call widget table (Claude + Codex), Build the generic ToolKind fallback tool-call row

issues.title: Build the client-side image attach pipeline with magic-byte validation
issues.body:
Goal: Implement paste/drop/file-pick attach producing an immediate local preview (object URL plus thumbhash placeholder) with a client-side magic-byte and size check that rejects an oversized or unsupported file before any upload starts.
Spec: SS7.25 attach bullet.
Acceptance:
- pasting, dropping, or picking an image shows an instant local preview with no network round trip
- file type is identified by sniffed magic bytes, never by declared mimeType or extension
- an oversized file (>10MB) or unsupported type is rejected client-side with a clear message, before any upload attempt
- default caps: 10MB per image, 20 images per prompt, png/jpeg/gif/webp
Notes: defaults follow Happy's own image-handling limits per SS16 (`processImage.ts`, `useImagePicker.ts`).
Depends on: Build the follow-up prompt composer with mid-turn queueing
issues.epic: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)

issues.title: Reject HEIC/HEIF attachments client-side with a clear message
issues.body:
Goal: Detect a HEIC/HEIF file at attach time and reject it client-side with a clear "convert and re-upload" message, since reliable client-side HEIC decode is not available in a web PWA outside Safari.
Spec: SS7.25 HEIC/HEIF deferred bullet.
Acceptance:
- a HEIC/HEIF file (detected by magic bytes) is rejected before any upload attempt
- the rejection message explicitly tells the user to convert and re-upload
Notes: real HEIC conversion (bundled WASM decoder or server-side) has no committed milestone in the spec; do not build it as part of this issue.
Depends on: Build the client-side image attach pipeline with magic-byte validation
issues.epic: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)

issues.title: Implement client-side E2E encrypt and optimistic blob upload for attachments
issues.body:
Goal: Encrypt an attached image client-side with the session's derived blob key and upload it to the relay's blob store addressed by an opaque ref, ciphertext only, starting the upload the moment a file is attached rather than deferred until send.
Spec: SS7.25 "Encrypt and upload" bullet, SS8 E2E scheme.
Acceptance:
- the upload starts immediately on attach, not on send
- the relay only ever receives and stores ciphertext for the blob, addressed by an opaque ref
- the upload uses the same per-device E2E blob-key derivation as the rest of the session
Notes: depends on the shared crypto package's blob-key derivation (crypto epic, out of this scope).
Depends on: Build the client-side image attach pipeline with magic-byte validation
issues.epic: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)

issues.title: Send the encrypted file event on its own channel, decoupled from the bounded-queue backpressure rule
issues.body:
Goal: Once a blob upload confirms, send a tiny encrypted "file event" (ref, mimeType, name, dimensions, thumbhash, never the bytes) over the normal session channel, ensuring the attachment bytes themselves never enter the live session/update fan-out so a multi-megabyte blob can't starve another client's resync marker.
Spec: SS7.25 "Encrypt and upload" bullet (file-event portion), SS7.16 backpressure cross-ref.
Acceptance:
- the file event contains only metadata fields, never attachment bytes
- the file event is only ever sent after the blob upload has confirmed, never speculatively
- a large attachment upload does not delay or drop unrelated session/update messages under the bounded-queue rule
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement client-side E2E encrypt and optimistic blob upload for attachments
issues.epic: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)

issues.title: Implement attachment upload failure and retry UX
issues.body:
Goal: Block sending a prompt that has an attachment still mid-upload or failed, show a per-attachment failed state with manual retry, and auto-retry once on reconnect after a dropped connection mid-transfer.
Spec: SS7.25 "Upload failure & retry" bullet.
Acceptance:
- the composer's send action is disabled while any attachment is mid-upload or in a failed state
- a failed upload shows a manual retry control
- a dropped connection mid-upload triggers exactly one automatic retry on reconnect
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement client-side E2E encrypt and optimistic blob upload for attachments
issues.epic: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)

issues.title: Fetch and decrypt attachment blobs on the executing host over the existing supervisor channel
issues.body:
Goal: Have the agent-supervisor fetch the attachment ciphertext by ref through the existing node-to-supervisor control channel and decrypt it locally, reusing that channel rather than opening a new direct supervisor-to-relay path.
Spec: SS7.25 "Deliver to the executing host" bullet.
Acceptance:
- the supervisor resolves a file-event ref into decrypted bytes using only the existing node-to-supervisor channel
- no new direct supervisor-to-relay connection is introduced
- works identically for a `local` and an `ssh:` target
Notes: this issue spans into the node/supervisor process; coordinate with the node/supervisor epic (out of this scope) on the control-channel contract. A supervisor-owned independent relay connection is a deliberate future option, not this issue.
Depends on: Send the encrypted file event on its own channel, decoupled from the bounded-queue backpressure rule
issues.epic: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)

issues.title: Build the Claude Code inline base64 image hand-off
issues.body:
Goal: Build the inline base64 `ContentBlock::Image` hand-off for the Claude Code adapter, gated on the session's negotiated `image` prompt capability, re-sniffing the actual bytes rather than trusting the declared mimeType, with no filesystem write.
Spec: SS7.25 "Hand off to the agent" bullet (Claude portion).
Acceptance:
- an attached image reaches Claude Code as an inline base64 content block, only when the session's negotiated capabilities include image support
- bytes are re-sniffed server-side before hand-off, not trusted from the client's declared mimeType
- no temp file is written for this path
Notes: SS16/SS10/SS12 flag this needs verifying at build time whether the chosen Claude Code ACP adapter actually advertises `session.prompt.image`; grounded in `claude-agent-acp` `acp-agent.ts`.
Depends on: Fetch and decrypt attachment blobs on the executing host over the existing supervisor channel
issues.epic: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)

issues.title: Build the Codex inline base64 image hand-off
issues.body:
Goal: Build the inline base64 image hand-off for the Codex adapter, converting the decrypted image into a data: URL exactly like the Claude Code path so the two adapters' image hand-off is unified rather than special-cased.
Spec: SS7.25 "Hand off to the agent" bullet (Codex portion).
Acceptance:
- an attached image reaches Codex as an inline base64 data: URL block
- the hand-off code path is shared/unified with the Claude Code hand-off wherever the two adapters' behavior is genuinely identical
Notes: SS16 grounding verified against `codex-acp` `CodexAcpClient.ts` source.
Depends on: Fetch and decrypt attachment blobs on the executing host over the existing supervisor channel
issues.epic: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)

issues.title: Build the generic ACP fallback image hand-off via temp file and ResourceLink
issues.body:
Goal: For any adapter without the negotiated `image` capability, write a supervisor-owned temp file (outside the project/worktree, 0700 dir / 0600 file, random name, deleted at end of turn, with a 24-hour sweep) and send a `ContentBlock::ResourceLink` instead of inline base64.
Spec: SS7.25 "Hand off to the agent" bullet (generic fallback portion).
Acceptance:
- an adapter lacking the image capability receives a `ResourceLink` pointing at a supervisor-managed temp file
- the temp file is 0700 dir / 0600 file, random-named, outside the project/worktree, and deleted at end of turn
- a 24-hour sweep cleans up any temp file that outlives its turn
Notes: none
Depends on: Fetch and decrypt attachment blobs on the executing host over the existing supervisor channel
issues.epic: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)

issues.title: Build the @file reference picker backed by the file-tree panel
issues.body:
Goal: Build a picker, backed by the minimal file-tree panel, that inserts a `ResourceLink`/`EmbeddedResource` for a file already on the target, costing nothing beyond the reference since the agent reads its own filesystem directly.
Spec: SS7.25 "@file references" bullet, SS7.4.
Acceptance:
- typing an @-trigger (or equivalent UI) opens a picker over the project's file tree
- selecting a file inserts a `ResourceLink`/`EmbeddedResource` reference into the prompt, with no upload/encryption round trip
- works against both a `local` and an `ssh:` target project
Notes: none
Depends on: Build the minimal read-only file-tree panel over local and ssh targets
issues.epic: Rich input & attachments (images, local and over SSH)

issues.title: Generate and manage self-owned VAPID keys and the push subscription endpoint
issues.body:
Goal: Generate and store a self-owned VAPID keypair on the relay and expose the endpoint clients use to register a push subscription, with no third-party push relay involved.
Spec: SS7.11, SS8 ("Self-owned push, no third-party push relay").
Acceptance:
- the relay generates and persists a VAPID keypair on first setup
- a documented endpoint accepts and stores a client's push subscription
- sending a test push through `web-push` to a registered subscription is demonstrated end to end
Notes: SS16 grounding: hapi `hub/config/vapidKeys.ts` + `pushService.ts` (the `web-push` npm package), RFC 8291/8292. This issue is relay-side work required to unblock the client-side notification issues in this epic.
Depends on: none

issues.title: Register the client push subscription via the service worker
issues.body:
Goal: Add the service-worker-side push subscription registration flow and the permission-prompt UX on both desktop and mobile.
Spec: SS7.11.
Acceptance:
- the app requests notification permission at an appropriate, non-intrusive point, not on first load unconditionally
- a granted permission results in a push subscription registered against the relay's VAPID endpoint
- works on both a desktop browser and a mobile PWA install
Notes: grounded in the Notifications/Push APIs (MDN).
Depends on: Generate and manage self-owned VAPID keys and the push subscription endpoint, Scaffold the SvelteKit PWA app shell with manifest and service worker

issues.title: Implement presence-aware notification delivery routing
issues.body:
Goal: Route a notification-worthy event to the device the user is actively using, fall back to push on the others, and suppress it entirely on a session the user is actively watching.
Spec: SS7.11 presence-aware bullet.
Acceptance:
- an event on a session currently open on one device does not trigger a push to that same device
- the same event does trigger push on the user's other registered devices
- an event on a session the user is actively watching (foreground) is suppressed everywhere, not just deduplicated
Notes: SS16 grounding: model on Nimbalyst's per-doc awareness (`CollabLexicalProvider.ts`), scoped per session; flagged as novel plumbing.
Depends on: Register the client push subscription via the service worker

issues.title: Render desktop and mobile notifications for the four attention event classes
issues.body:
Goal: Render notifications for the four events that populate the attention inbox: a tool call awaiting approval, a session finished or errored, a CI failure, and a review request, on both desktop and mobile.
Spec: SS7.11, SS7.13 cross-ref.
Acceptance:
- each of the four event classes produces a distinguishable notification with enough context to act on
- tapping/clicking a notification opens directly to the relevant session/item
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement presence-aware notification delivery routing, Build the unified attention-inbox view aggregating approvals, session outcomes, CI failures and reviews

issues.title: Implement actionable mobile push notifications
issues.body:
Goal: Add OS-actionable push using the Notifications API `actions[]` and `event.action` handling, so a mobile approval notification can act directly (e.g. Allow/Deny) without opening the app, where the platform supports it.
Spec: SS7.11 ("OS-actionable push where allowed"), SS7.3 mobile approval cards.
Acceptance:
- an approval-class push notification exposes action buttons on a supporting platform
- tapping an action button resolves the corresponding permission request without a full app open
- gracefully degrades to a plain open-the-app notification where actions aren't supported (documented as iOS 16.4+ only)
Notes: SS16 grounding: Notifications API `actions[]`/`event.action` (MDN); iOS 16.4+ only.
Depends on: Render desktop and mobile notifications for the four attention event classes

issues.title: Build per-project mute and quiet-hours settings
issues.body:
Goal: Let the user mute notifications for a project and define quiet-hours windows during which notifications are suppressed.
Spec: SS7.11 ("Per-project mute and quiet-hours").
Acceptance:
- a project can be muted, suppressing its notifications until unmuted
- a quiet-hours window suppresses notifications during that window across all non-muted projects
- settings persist across sessions/devices for the account
Notes: none
Depends on: Implement presence-aware notification delivery routing

issues.title: Build the unified attention-inbox view aggregating approvals, session outcomes, CI failures and reviews
issues.body:
Goal: Build the one cross-project, cross-node view of everything needing the user now: pending tool-call approvals, sessions finished or errored, failing CI, and review requests.
Spec: SS7.13.
Acceptance:
- the inbox lists items from all four classes across every project and node the user has access to
- items are visually distinguishable by class and link back to their originating session/project
- the inbox updates live as new items arrive
Notes: none
Depends on: Build app navigation and cross-node session list bound to relay metadata

issues.title: Make attention-inbox items actionable inline
issues.body:
Goal: Let the user approve/deny, open, or reply to an inbox item directly from the inbox, without navigating into the originating session first.
Spec: SS7.13 ("Items are actionable inline").
Acceptance:
- an approval-class item can be approved/denied directly from the inbox row
- an open action navigates to the originating session/item
- a reply action lets the user send a follow-up without leaving the inbox
Notes: none
Depends on: Build the unified attention-inbox view aggregating approvals, session outcomes, CI failures and reviews, Implement the inline FIFO tool-call permission queue

issues.title: Ensure inbox item resolution stays consistent with the per-session permission queue
issues.body:
Goal: Guarantee an inbox item resolved from either the inbox or the originating session's own view resolves everywhere, since the inbox is a live view onto the same per-session FIFO queue state, not a separate copy.
Spec: SS7.13, SS7.24 "Cross-session surfacing" bullet.
Acceptance:
- resolving an item in the inbox removes the matching card from the session view immediately, and vice versa
- a request already resolved elsewhere never appears actionable in the inbox
Notes: this issue is the inbox-side half of the shared cross-session surfacing contract; see the matching transcript-epic issue for the queue-side half.
Depends on: Build the unified attention-inbox view aggregating approvals, session outcomes, CI failures and reviews, Wire the permission queue as the single source of truth for cross-session surfacing

issues.title: Wire the attention inbox to drive push notification triggers
issues.body:
Goal: Ensure every item that lands in the attention inbox also drives the corresponding push notification, so the inbox and notifications are two views of the same event stream.
Spec: SS7.13 ("drive the push notifications"), SS7.11.
Acceptance:
- a new inbox item of any of the four classes triggers the matching notification path
- no inbox-eligible event silently fails to notify
Notes: none
Depends on: Build the unified attention-inbox view aggregating approvals, session outcomes, CI failures and reviews, Generate and manage self-owned VAPID keys and the push subscription endpoint

issues.title: Build the minimal read-only file-tree panel over local and ssh targets
issues.body:
Goal: Build a file-tree panel for any project, local or on an `ssh:` target, that lazily lists directories and files over the same transport the session already uses, sufficient to back the @file reference picker.
Spec: SS7.4 (browse the tree; remote files browsed over the same transport), SS7.25 @file references bullet.
Acceptance:
- the tree lists a project's files/directories for both a `local` and an `ssh:` target project
- directory contents load lazily, not the whole tree up front
- this is read-only in v1: no create/rename/delete affordances
Notes: none

issues.title: Build the PTY terminal component wired to a relay-proxied terminal session
issues.body:
Goal: Build an interactive PTY terminal component (create/write/resize/close) that connects through the relay to a terminal running on the session's target, local or `ssh:`, reachable from both desktop and phone.
Spec: SS7.5.
Acceptance:
- opening a terminal creates a real PTY on the target and streams its output to the client
- typed input, resize, and close all work correctly through the relay proxy
- the same terminal works identically whether the target is `local` or `ssh:`
Notes: SS16 grounding: message shape modeled clean-room (hapi's `terminal.ts` is AGPL, design reference only, no code copied), plus node-pty + `@xterm/xterm` + ssh2 drain/backpressure per emdash's `ssh2-pty.ts`.
