issues.body:
Goal: Let the user open more than one terminal for the same project, all sharing that session's working directory or worktree.
Spec: SS7.5 ("Multiple terminals per project, sharing the session's working directory / worktree").
Acceptance:
- a second terminal for the same project opens in the same working directory/worktree as the first
- closing one terminal doesn't affect the others

issues.body:
Goal: Confirm and test that a terminal on a `local` target and a terminal on an `ssh:` target are both fully reachable and usable from the desktop browser and the mobile PWA.
Spec: SS7.5 ("exposed through the relay, so they are reachable from the PWA on desktop and phone").
Acceptance:

- both `local` and `ssh:` targets are covered by this parity check
- covered by a Playwright test at both a desktop and a phone viewport
Notes: none

issues.title: Scaffold packages/providers/core and implement ACP initialize/capability negotiation
issues.body:
Goal: stand up the packages/providers/core package and implement the ACP JSON-RPC `initialize` handshake, capturing each session's negotiated capabilities so every other core mechanism and the UI can gate on them.

Spec: §5.5 (Generic ACP core paragraph, capability negotiation bullet), §10 ("an ACP-generic backend on the Agent Client Protocol reference types"), §10.1 (packages/providers layout).

Acceptance:
- packages/providers/core exists in the pnpm workspace with its own package.json/exports, plus an empty packages/providers/gemini stub package reserved per §5.5's "next one reserved" note
- `initialize` request/response is implemented against the ACP JSON-RPC transport, and the negotiated capability set (image/audio attach, MCP-server picker, additional-directories, session delete, etc.) is captured on a per-session object
- unit tests cover fixtures with a capability present and the same capability absent
- a provider that implements nothing beyond bare ACP compiles and initializes successfully through core alone

Notes: no dedicated §16 line (ACP's own initialize spec is the reference); see §16's "Generic ACP fallback tier" entry for the adjacent zero-code-fallback framing this scaffold enables.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Implement ACP session lifecycle (new/resume/list) and replay in packages/providers/core
issues.body:
Goal: implement session/new, session/resume, session/list, and cancellation, plus deterministic replay of a session's buffered history so the same reducer path serves both a live stream and a reconnect replay.

Spec: §5.5 ("session/new/session/resume + replay... session/list, and cancellation"), §7.22 (resume-on-reopen), §7.24 ("The same reducer runs identically for a live stream and for replayed history on reconnect").

Acceptance:
- session/new, session/resume, session/list, and a cancellation call are implemented and typed against the ACP schema
- replaying a buffered transcript through the core reducer produces the same in-memory state as processing the same events live
- a resumed session round-trips through the API with no duplicated or dropped items when fed a recorded fixture with an intentional gap

Notes: none beyond the general protocol-version-handshake reference in §16 (relay/protocol scope); core here just consumes ACP's own session methods.

Depends on: Scaffold packages/providers/core and implement ACP initialize/capability negotiation

issues.title: Implement the append-by-id message/thought/tool-call/plan/usage state reducer in packages/providers/core
issues.body:
Goal: implement the single ACP-v1-baseline reducer that appends `user_message_chunk`/`agent_message_chunk`/`agent_thought_chunk` by `messageId`, tracks `tool_call`/`tool_call_update` state (including diff/patch fields), replaces the current plan wholesale on `plan_update`, and records `usage_update` per session — the shared data model every client surface reads from.

Spec: §7.24 ("One reducer, append-only by id"; "Plans, rendered twice from one truth"; tool_call bullet), §5.5 (core owns tool_call/tool_call_update, plan_update, usage_update).

Acceptance:
- a chunk with a repeated `messageId` appends to the existing item; a new id starts a new item; ids are scoped by turn + kind, not raw `messageId` alone, per §7.24
- `tool_call_update` mutates the existing tool-call entry in place (never appends a duplicate row) and preserves diff/patch fields (`{path, oldText, newText}`, ACP v1 shape)
- `plan_update` replaces the session's entire current plan list atomically, never diffed client-side
- `usage_update` is recorded per session and the reducer marks whether an update is attributable to a nested/subagent tool call, so a v2 UI can later exclude it from the live percentage meter per §7.9
- unit tests cover out-of-order chunk arrival and an interleaved thought+message pair reusing the same id within one turn

Notes: §16 flags "usage_update is session-level (no per-tool attribution) — subagent-exclusion is a client-side heuristic" — implement the attribution as a documented heuristic, not a protocol guarantee.

Depends on: Scaffold packages/providers/core and implement ACP initialize/capability negotiation

issues.title: Implement the session/request_permission FIFO queue state machine in packages/providers/core
issues.body:
Goal: implement the per-session, cross-surface single source of truth for pending `session/request_permission` calls — a FIFO queue keyed by request id that any UI surface can subscribe to and resolve, with the multi-request/Stop/cancellation ordering rules the spec requires.

Spec: §7.24 ("Tool-call permissions" bullet and its "Cross-session surfacing"/"Multi-request ordering" sub-bullets), §5.5 (core owns session/request_permission).

Acceptance:
- pending requests for a session queue in arrival order and expose the request's raw `toolCall` (a `ToolCallUpdate`: title, rawInput, content, locations) for a UI to render directly
- resolving a request from any subscriber removes it everywhere (no duplicate resolution, no leaking an elsewhere-resolved request as still pending)
- on a session-level Stop, every open request for that session resolves immediately as cancelled (optimistic, does not wait for the agent's own follow-up update)
- denying one request while siblings from the same turn are still queued leaves those siblings queued, unresolved, in original order
- a stale approve/deny call against an already-resolved request id returns a distinguishable "no longer applies" result rather than silently succeeding, per §7.3

Notes: per §7.17, this queue only covers what the agent chooses to surface via ACP (MAY, not MUST) — it is not the enforcement layer; hard guardrails/sandboxing (separate epic) are.

Depends on: Implement the append-by-id message/thought/tool-call/plan/usage state reducer in packages/providers/core

issues.title: Implement config-option (model/mode/reasoning-effort) state handling in packages/providers/core
issues.body:
Goal: implement state management for ACP's config-option vocabulary (`model`, `model_config`, `thought_level`, `mode`, and any unrecognized future category) so a UI can always re-render the complete control set from one source rather than patching one control in isolation.

Spec: §7.24 ("Model, mode & reasoning effort" bullet), which explicitly calls this "core interaction UX for v1, not later polish."

Acceptance:
- the full config-option list is exposed as one object per session, re-derived wholesale on any user change or unprompted `config_option_update`
- an unprompted `config_option_update` (e.g. an automatic model fallback after a rate limit) updates state and is separately flagged as attention-worthy data for the inbox epic to consume, without this issue building inbox UI
- an unrecognized/future non-underscore-prefixed category name is preserved in state rather than dropped
- provider/agent choice is immutable once a session object exists (enforced at the type/API level, not just by convention)

Notes: none dedicated in §16; ACP's own `initialize` response is the source of the option list.

Depends on: Scaffold packages/providers/core and implement ACP initialize/capability negotiation

issues.title: Export a capability-negotiation-gated feature-flag API from packages/providers/core
issues.body:
Goal: turn each session's negotiated `initialize` capabilities into a single, typed feature-flag surface (image/audio attach, MCP-server picker, additional-directories, session delete, and any other optional affordance) that client UI reads to show/hide affordances, so a plain generic-ACP session with image support behaves identically to a Claude Code one.

Spec: §5.5 ("Capability negotiation gates the UI, not provider branding").

Acceptance:
- a single exported function/selector takes a session's negotiated capabilities and returns a flat set of UI-facing flags, one per optional affordance named in §5.5
- two fixture sessions (a plain generic-ACP session with the `image` capability on, and a Claude Code session) produce identical flag values for that shared affordance, proving branding plays no role
- flags recompute correctly when fed a capability set missing a given optional field (treated as off, not as an error)

Notes: no dedicated §16 line; this is the architectural spine the spec calls out as the design's key differentiator.

Depends on: Scaffold packages/providers/core and implement ACP initialize/capability negotiation

issues.title: Implement the provider-module registry and enrich() hook extension point in packages/providers/core
issues.body:
Goal: implement the plugin point per-provider adapter modules use — a registry a provider module registers into (by ACP provider id), plus the `enrich(update, raw)` hook contract a module may implement to promote vendor `_meta` fields into first-class core fields, defaulting to a no-op pass-through when a module implements neither.

Spec: §5.5 ("Per-provider adapter modules... an enrich(update, raw) hook", "A module that adds neither simply falls back to the generic tier everywhere: no-op enrich...").

Acceptance:
- a provider module registers under a provider id and is looked up by that id when a session starts
- `enrich(update, raw)` is invoked for every incoming session/update when a provider module supplies one, and is skipped (pure pass-through) when it doesn't
- registering a stub provider module supplying neither `enrich` nor UI wiring still produces a fully working session through the generic tier alone (fixture test)
- the registry has an unregistered `gemini` provider id reserved (no adapter logic) so a future v2 module can register into it without a core API change

Notes: this is the hook the Claude adapter later uses to promote `_meta.claudeCode.parentToolUseId` → `parentToolCallId` — that specific promotion is v2 subagent-tree work (§7.24); this issue only builds the hook plumbing, not that promotion.

Depends on: Implement the append-by-id message/thought/tool-call/plan/usage state reducer in packages/providers/core

issues.title: Spike: verify Codex's ACP completeness at build time
issues.body:
Goal: run codex-acp against the packages/providers/core initialize/session/tool-call surface and determine, with evidence, whether its ACP support is complete enough to ship a Codex adapter in v1 — the explicit gate the spec sets before committing to a Codex adapter module.

Spec: §10 ("Codex's ACP support is verified at build time - if incomplete, v1 ships Claude Code only and Codex lands once it's ready"), §5.5 ("v1 ships Claude Code + Codex adapter modules (Codex's ACP completeness verified at build time)").

Acceptance:
- a written finding states, with evidence (protocol traces or source references), which ACP methods/capabilities codex-acp actually implements against core's expectations: initialize, session/new, tool_call, session/request_permission, image content blocks
- a go/no-go recommendation for shipping the Codex adapter in v1 is recorded, with any gaps enumerated
- if go: the specific `options[]`/`kind` verbs Codex's permission requests actually send are documented for the downstream adapter issue to consume

Notes: §16 flags the analogous Gemini caution ("the ACP registry lists Gemini CLI 0.50.0, but emdash drives Gemini via plain CLI, not ACP — verify its ACP flag at build time") as the template for this kind of verification; §7.25 already states (verified against source) that codex-acp's `CodexAcpClient.ts` converts an image block into a `data:` URL like Claude's adapter — this spike should corroborate that rather than re-derive it from scratch.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Implement packages/providers/generic — the zero-code ACP fallback adapter
issues.body:
Goal: implement the adapter every ACP-speaking agent gets automatically with no bespoke module: a flat tool-call list, `ToolKind`-generic rows, `ResourceLink` handling, and a plain Allow/Deny(+always) permission mapping derived from the protocol's own vocabulary.

Spec: §5.5 ("Generic ACP adapter... flat tool-call list, ToolKind-generic rows, plain permission buttons, ResourceLink for file/image references"), §7.24 ("the generic ACP tier maps the protocol's own options[]/kind vocabulary (allow_once/allow_always/reject_once/reject_always) onto a plain Allow/Deny (+ always) pair").

Acceptance:
- registering any ACP-speaking agent under a new provider id with no adapter module produces working sessions through packages/providers/generic alone
- every tool call without a bespoke widget renders through the `ToolKind`-generic classification (read/edit/delete/move/search/execute/think/fetch/other)
- the permission-mapping function reduces any `options[]`/`kind` combination from the ACP spec onto Allow/Deny(+always) correctly, including an `options[]` set that omits an "always" variant
- a `ResourceLink` content block round-trips for a file/image reference with no bespoke handling required

Notes: §16 "Generic ACP fallback tier — grounded in ACP baseline (ContentBlock::Text/ResourceLink + 4 permission kinds); the zero-code fallback package is a novel architectural choice (every reference pairs 'speaks ACP' with a bespoke module)."

Depends on: Export a capability-negotiation-gated feature-flag API from packages/providers/core; Implement the provider-module registry and enrich() hook extension point in packages/providers/core
issues.epic: Provider adapters (Claude Code, Codex, generic; Gemini reserved)

issues.title: Implement packages/providers/claude — the Claude Code adapter module
issues.body:
Goal: implement the Claude Code adapter module: its `enrich()` registration, its permission button set/verbs, and its bespoke tool-call widgets for Edit/Write/Bash/TodoWrite.

Spec: §5.5 (per-provider adapter modules paragraph), §7.24 ("Claude's Allow-once / Allow-all-edits / Bypass-everything / Allow-for-session / Deny"; tool-call widget bullet: "Claude's Edit/Write/Bash/TodoWrite... each wrapped in its own error boundary").

Acceptance:
- the module registers under the Claude Code provider id against core's registry and supplies an `enrich` function (v1: pass-through/no-op body, typed and wired so v2 can fill in the `_meta.claudeCode.parentToolUseId` promotion without a registry change)
- the permission-request mapping exposes Claude's actual verb set (Allow-once / Allow-all-edits / Bypass-everything / Allow-for-session / Deny) rather than the generic Allow/Deny pair
- bespoke widgets exist for Edit, Write, Bash, and TodoWrite tool calls, each wrapped in its own error boundary so one bad widget cannot take down the rest of the transcript
- a tool call already covered by a bespoke widget is suppressed from the generic ToolKind fallback row (no duplicate rendering during streaming)
- the actual Claude Code ACP adapter build is checked for whether it advertises the `session.prompt.image` capability, and the finding is recorded

Notes: §16 "Tool-widget tiers grounded — nimbalyst CustomToolWidgets/ + ToolWidgetErrorBoundary.tsx; the ToolKind generic-row rendering is new UI." The image encrypt/upload/hand-off pipeline itself (§7.25) is out of scope here — see the rich-input & attachments epic; this issue only covers the adapter-level capability check and widget/permission wiring.

Depends on: Implement packages/providers/generic — the zero-code ACP fallback adapter

issues.title: Implement packages/providers/codex — the Codex adapter module
issues.body:
Goal: implement the Codex adapter module, gated on the build-time verification spike: its `enrich()` registration, its permission button set/verbs, and its bespoke tool-call widgets for patch/diff/bash.

Spec: §5.5 (per-provider adapter modules paragraph), §7.24 ("Codex's Yes / Yes-for-session / Stop-and-explain (an abort, not a deny)"; "Codex's patch/diff/bash" widget bullet), §10/§12 (Codex ships only if its ACP support is ready).

Acceptance:
- the module registers under the Codex provider id, gated on the verification spike's go decision; if the spike returned no-go, this issue is descoped to whatever subset the spike found working, with the gap recorded
- the permission-request mapping exposes Codex's actual verb set (Yes / Yes-for-session / Stop-and-explain), with Stop-and-explain modeled as an abort rather than a deny
- bespoke widgets exist for Codex's patch/diff and bash tool calls, each wrapped in its own error boundary
- a tool call already covered by a bespoke widget is suppressed from the generic fallback row

Notes: §16's Codex image-handoff note ("codex-acp CodexAcpClient.ts converts an image block into a data: URL exactly like Claude's") is corroborated by the build-time spike, not re-verified here; the attachment pipeline itself is out of scope (see the rich-input & attachments epic, §7.25).

Depends on: Spike: verify Codex's ACP completeness at build time; Implement packages/providers/generic — the zero-code ACP fallback adapter

issues.title: Add Vitest fixtures and protocol-conformance tests for packages/providers
issues.body:
Goal: give the provider-layer packages a shared fixture harness (recorded ACP session/update streams for a plain generic agent, Claude Code, and Codex) so core, generic, claude, and codex all have real regression coverage from commit one.

Spec: AGENTS.md ("Testing/CI: Vitest... every package ships tests from commit one"); §5.5/§7.24 (the mechanisms under test).

Acceptance:
- recorded/fixture session/update streams exist for at least: a bare generic-ACP session, a Claude Code session with a permission request and a tool-call diff, and a Codex session with the same shapes
- each of packages/providers/{core,generic,claude,codex} has a Vitest suite exercising its fixtures through the real reducer/registry code (not mocks of it)
- CI runs these suites as part of the existing lint/typecheck/test gate

Notes: no §16 entry specific to test harnessing; this operationalizes the AGENTS.md testing convention for this package cluster.

issues.title: Design and implement the MCP server configuration data model (per-project + global)
issues.body:
Goal: build the data model and storage for per-project and global MCP server configuration — add/enable/disable a server, generalized across providers through the ACP layer rather than tied to one CLI's own config format.

Spec: §7.7 ("Per-project and global configuration of the coding agents: add and enable MCP servers... Modeled on Nimbalyst's MCP configuration, generalized across providers through the ACP layer").

Acceptance:
- a project-level and a global-level MCP server config record can be created, listed, enabled, and disabled independently
- a project's effective MCP server set resolves as global-plus-project-overrides (a project can add its own servers or disable an inherited global one)
- the config shape is provider-agnostic, not modeled on any single CLI's own MCP config file format
- config persists across a node restart

Notes: no dedicated §16 line beyond the general "reference tools store env inline per server" framing under the secret-grant issue below.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Implement quick-add MCP server presets
issues.body:
Goal: ship a small catalog of common MCP servers a user can add with one click rather than hand-typing a server command/config, per §7.7's "quick-add presets."

Spec: §7.7 ("add and enable MCP servers (quick-add presets, secret handling)").

Acceptance:
- a small starter catalog of presets (server name, command/args or remote URL, declared required secrets if any) is defined and addable through the config API from the data-model issue
- adding a preset produces the same config record shape a hand-entered server would, so presets are not a special downstream code path
- a preset requiring a secret prompts for it through the per-server secret-grant mechanism, never pre-filling a value automatically

Notes: no dedicated §16 line; this is the low-effort convenience layer on top of the config model.

Depends on: Design and implement the MCP server configuration data model (per-project + global)

issues.title: Implement per-server secret grant for MCP servers
issues.body:
Goal: implement the explicit, per-server secret-grant mechanism so an added MCP server receives project secrets only when a user explicitly grants that specific server access, never automatically, and never by inheriting whatever secrets the project happens to hold.

Spec: §7.7 ("An added MCP server receives project secrets only by explicit per-server grant, never automatically, and its output is treated as untrusted input (§7.17)"), §7.17 ("Untrusted content: tracker/issue and MCP content is untrusted input, never operator instruction").

Acceptance:
- granting a secret to an MCP server is a distinct, explicit action per (project, server, secret) tuple — no server receives a project secret by default on creation
- revoking a grant removes that server's access to the secret without affecting other servers' grants on the same secret
- the config surface (or its docs) states plainly that MCP server output is untrusted input, not operator instruction, matching §7.17's framing
- secrets are resolved and injected only at the executing node (never sent to the relay or clients), consistent with §7.17/§8's node-local-secrets rule

Notes: §16 "MCP per-server secret grant — (novel: define ACL/approval semantics; reference tools store env inline per server)" — there is no reference implementation to copy; the ACL/approval semantics must be designed fresh.

Depends on: Design and implement the MCP server configuration data model (per-project + global)

issues.title: Wire configured MCP servers into ACP session initialization
issues.body:
Goal: connect the MCP server configuration surface to an actual running session — a session's `session/new` call carries the effective, enabled MCP server set (with any granted secrets already resolved) for the chosen provider.

Spec: §7.7 (configured MCP servers feed the actual agent session); §5.5 (session/new is a core-owned ACP method).

Acceptance:
- starting a session passes the project's effective enabled MCP server list into the ACP session's MCP configuration, in the shape each provider's ACP adapter expects
- a server missing a granted secret it requires fails session creation with a clear, actionable error rather than starting silently without it
- disabling a server after a session has started does not require restarting unrelated sessions on the same project

Notes: no additional §16 line beyond the secret-grant novelty already cited; this issue is the integration point between the MCP-config epic and the ACP-core session-lifecycle issue.

Depends on: Design and implement the MCP server configuration data model (per-project + global); Implement per-server secret grant for MCP servers; Implement ACP session lifecycle (new/resume/list) and replay in packages/providers/core

issues.title: Implement agent plugin/extension management per project
issues.body:
Goal: extend the agent-configuration surface to manage agent plugins/extensions (beyond MCP servers) per project, per §7.7's "manage agent plugins/extensions."

Spec: §7.7 ("manage agent plugins/extensions").

Acceptance:
- a project's enabled plugin/extension list can be viewed, added, and removed independently of its MCP server list
- the plugin config shape is provider-agnostic where ACP itself doesn't distinguish "plugin" from other config, and is documented as such for any provider (Claude Code/Codex) with no native plugin concept beyond MCP servers at v1
- config persists and is resolvable the same way as MCP server config (global + per-project)

Notes: no §16 line; the spec references "plugins/extensions" without a concrete per-provider mechanism, so this issue should record what, concretely, "plugin" means for Claude Code and Codex today before building config around it.

Depends on: Design and implement the MCP server configuration data model (per-project + global)

issues.title: Set up Playwright e2e testing for the web PWA
issues.body:
Goal: Wire up Playwright as the end-to-end test runner against apps/web so PWA flows can be exercised in CI, complementing Vitest's unit/integration coverage.
Spec: §10 "Testing / CI: ... Playwright (PWA e2e)"; AGENTS.md "Testing / CI".
Acceptance:
- Playwright is configured in apps/web with a playwright.config.ts targeting the dev/preview server
- At least one smoke e2e test loads the app shell and asserts it rendered
- The GitHub Actions CI workflow runs Playwright tests (with browser install caching) as part of the gate

Notes: not called out separately in §16 beyond the general Vitest+Playwright pairing in §10/AGENTS.md.

issues.title: Set up Changesets for versioning and GitHub Releases
issues.body:
Goal: Add Changesets so every package in the monorepo gets independent semver versioning, changelog generation, and automated GitHub Releases on merge.
Spec: §10 "Release / versioning: Changesets + GitHub Releases (semver + changelog) across the monorepo; supervisor binaries are signed (§7.23)"; §16 "Release/versioning — Changesets (@changesets/cli + action); deliberate departure from emdash/happy/hapi (none use it)".
Acceptance:
- @changesets/cli is installed and initialized at the repo root with per-package versioning config
- A .github/workflows/release.yml runs the Changesets GitHub Action to open/update a Version Packages PR and publish GitHub Releases on merge to main
- CONTRIBUTING.md documents the `pnpm changeset` workflow for contributors/agents
- A dry-run changeset + version bump succeeds locally against the scaffolded skeleton packages
Notes: §16 explicitly calls this a deliberate departure from all four reference tools (none use Changesets) — there is no in-repo precedent, use the official @changesets/action recipe.

issues.title: Design the loombox logo and brand mark
issues.body:
Goal: Produce the loombox monoline logo mark described in the spec — a woven warp/weft glyph that also reads as a container — in formats usable across favicon, app icon, and PWA manifest.
Spec: §4 "Brand... Logo: a minimal monoline mark — interwoven warp/weft lines forming (or passing through) a square 'box'. Reads as both a woven grid and a container. Works as a single-color glyph at favicon size."
Acceptance:
- SVG source of the mark exists at a single-color glyph resolution, verified legible at 16x16/32x32 favicon size
- Exported favicon set (ico/png) and PWA manifest icons (192/512 maskable) are generated from the same source
- The mark works standalone (no wordmark) and paired with the lowercase 'loombox' wordmark
- Assets are committed and referenced from apps/web's manifest
Notes: no §16 grounding item (visual design has no reference-tool precedent); build fresh per §4's description.
Depends on: none

issues.title: Establish the dark-first design token system (color, spacing, elevation)
issues.body:
Goal: Define the shared design tokens implementing the spec's dark-mode-first ink/paper palette with a single warm thread accent, so every UI surface in apps/web draws from one source.
Spec: §4 "Visual style: dark-mode-first (it is a dev tool). Ink/paper monochrome base plus one warm 'thread' accent (a loom-thread hue — warm coral/amber), used sparingly for active state and the woven motif."
Acceptance:
- A token set (dark theme default, light theme as a secondary variant) defines ink/paper neutrals plus one accent hue, exported as CSS custom properties consumable from apps/web
- Tokens cover background layers, text (primary/muted), borders, the thread accent (default + active state), and a semantic error/warning/success set
- A living style-reference route in apps/web renders token swatches so future UI work can check against it
- Tokens pass a basic WCAG AA contrast check for body text, per §11's baseline-semantic-markup floor
Notes: no §16 grounding item; original brand work per §4, not borrowed from any reference tool.
Depends on: none

issues.title: Set up the typography system: Inter + JetBrains Mono
issues.body:
Goal: Wire the two brand typefaces into the shared design tokens/CSS — a grotesk for UI chrome and a monospace for agent output, code, and diffs — self-hosted rather than loaded from a third-party CDN.
Spec: §4 "Typography: a clean grotesk for UI (e.g. Inter); a monospace (e.g. JetBrains Mono) for agent output, code, and diffs."
Acceptance:
- Inter and JetBrains Mono are self-hosted (variable-font woff2, Latin subset) rather than pulled from Google Fonts at runtime, for offline-tolerant PWA use
- Font tokens (--font-ui, --font-mono) are added to the token system and consumed by a base stylesheet
- The style-reference page demonstrates both faces at representative weights/sizes (body text, code block, diff line)
- A fallback stack is defined so there is no invisible text before web fonts load
Notes: no §16 grounding item; standard self-hosted variable-font setup.
Depends on: Establish the dark-first design token system (color, spacing, elevation)

issues.title: Scaffold the loombox-landing repo (SvelteKit + adapter-node)
issues.body:
Goal: Create the separate loombox-landing repository (kept out of the product monorepo) as a minimal SvelteKit site using @sveltejs/adapter-node, matching how pitchbox and embertold are built for prodbox hosting.
Spec: §10.1 "loombox-landing — a separate repo for the marketing landing page, built in SvelteKit (@sveltejs/adapter-node) and hosted on prodbox (Docker + Caddy on loombox.dev), exactly like pitchbox and embertold. Kept out of the product monorepo."; AGENTS.md "The marketing landing page lives in a separate repo".
Acceptance:
- A new private GitHub repo loombox-landing exists with a SvelteKit app using @sveltejs/adapter-node, building and running locally (`pnpm dev`)
- A Dockerfile + docker-compose overlay mirrors pitchbox's prodbox deployment pattern (app bound to a loopback port, no bundled reverse proxy)
- Basic CI (lint/typecheck/build) runs on this repo independent of the product monorepo's workflow
- README documents the intended prodbox deployment path (Caddy fronting loombox.dev) as a follow-up, not yet performed
Notes: §16 "Compose file for prodbox — copy Lorenzo's own pitchbox compose overlays (happy-server ships k8s, not compose)" — reuse pitchbox's compose overlay pattern rather than designing a new one.

issues.title: Implement device-switch state preservation across sessions
issues.body:
Goal: Preserve per-session UI/cache state (scroll position, last-viewed item) when the user switches from one device to another mid-session, borrowing Happy's per-session invalidate/caching approach.
Spec: SS7.3 ("switch device mid-session with state preserved, borrowing Happy's per-session invalidate/caching approach").
Acceptance:
- switching devices mid-session resumes at a sensible point (not a cold reload to the top)
- a stale client's cached view is invalidated correctly when another device has advanced the session

issues.title: Spike: verify Codex parent-tool-call signal availability for subagent trees

issues.title: Implement subagent and nested tool-call tree rendering
issues.body:
Goal: Render any tool-call item referenced as another's parent (via the provider adapter's `enrich()`-promoted `parentToolCallId`) as a collapsible, recursively-nestable group: auto-collapsed with an auto-scrolling preview while a child is running, full expand on demand, every child dispatching to its own native tier-1/2 renderer. A session/provider that never populates a parent link degrades to a flat list automatically. On a narrow viewport, replace inline nesting with a terse "last 3 tool calls + N more" summary that opens a detail view.
Spec: SS7.24 subagents bullet (v2).
Acceptance:
- a Claude Code session with `parentToolUseId`-linked tool calls renders as a nested, collapsible tree
- a child item renders through its normal tier-1/2 widget, just indented, with no special-casing
- a session/provider without a parent link renders flat, unchanged from v1 behavior
- a narrow viewport shows the "last 3 + N more" summary instead of indentation
Notes: none

Goal: Add a persistent per-session sidebar rendering the same current-plan data as the inline plan card, grouped pending/in-progress/completed with a completion bar, so the plan stays visible after the user scrolls away from where it was inline-rendered.

issues.title: Harden terminal backpressure and resize handling for slow mobile clients
issues.body:
Goal: Apply SS7.16's bounded-queue backpressure rule (drop-oldest plus a resync marker on overflow) to the terminal output stream specifically, so a phone on cellular never blocks the terminal for faster clients or the supervisor's own persistence, and polish resize behavior under that constraint.
Spec: SS7.5, SS7.16 backpressure cross-ref.

issues.title: Define TrackerMode config and the pluggable TrackerBackend interface
issues.body:
Introduce the shared `TrackerMode` discriminated union (`{kind:'native'}` vs `{kind:'live', provider, connectionId, target}`), the `GitHubTarget`/`JiraTarget` shapes, and the `TrackerBackend` interface with its `TrackerBackendCapabilities` flags in the shared/protocol packages, as the extension point every backend and the UI build against.

Spec: §7.10 (TrackerMode/GitHubTarget/JiraTarget code block; TrackerBackend interface and TrackerBackendCapabilities code block).

Acceptance:
- `TrackerMode`, `GitHubTarget`, `JiraTarget` types exist in a shared package and are Zod-validated per the protocol convention.
- `TrackerBackend` interface and `TrackerBackendCapabilities` are defined exactly as specced (list/get/create/update plus optional comments/transitions/boards/sprints methods).
- A project's `TrackerMode` is a persisted, per-project setting with no default silently assumed.
- Type-level test/compile check confirms a stub backend implementing only required methods still satisfies the interface (optional methods absent).

Notes: Pluggable TrackerBackend interface is reframed from Nimbalyst's read-only, one-shot `trackerImporter` SDK shape (`nimbalyst/packages/extension-sdk/src/types/trackerImporter.ts:1-149`) into full CRUD (§16, 'Pluggable TrackerBackend interface').

issues.title: Native tracker: TrackerRecord data model and storage
issues.body:
Implement loombox's own local ticketing data model on the node: a `fields` JSONB bag for business data, a `system` object (author, linked commits/PRs/sessions, activity, comments), and real indexed SQL columns (`id`, `primary_type`, `type_tags`, `issue_number`, `archived`, timestamps) around the JSONB blob, matching Nimbalyst's post-refactor shape rather than its older flat `tracker_items` row.

Spec: §7.10 ('Native mode' bullet).

Acceptance:
- Schema has the split fields-bag/system-object/indexed-columns shape, not a flat row.
- Built-in types (Task/Bug/Epic) and a project-defined custom type with its own `roles` mapping (title/workflowStatus/priority/assignee/...) both work against the same schema.
- A kanban board, priority sort, and assignee filter all work identically for a built-in type and a custom type.
- No `syncStatus`/team-sync columns are present (native tracker is per-operator, not multi-user collaborative, by design).

Notes: Data model copies Nimbalyst's post-refactor, schema-driven shape (`nimbalyst/packages/runtime/src/core/TrackerRecord.ts:36-70`), superseding its older `tracker_items` row (`nimbalyst/docs/nimbalyst-schema.prisma:71-82`) — grounded in §16 'Native tracker data model'.

Depends on: Define TrackerMode config and the pluggable TrackerBackend interface

issues.title: Native tracker: MCP tool contract for agent access
issues.body:
Expose the native tracker to agents via MCP tools (`tracker_list`, `tracker_get`, `tracker_create`, `tracker_update`, `tracker_link_session`), reusing Nimbalyst's contract shape so an agent can read and write native tracker items and link them to the current session.

Spec: §7.10 ('Agents access native projects through tracker_list/tracker_get/tracker_create/tracker_update/tracker_link_session MCP tools...').

Acceptance:
- All five MCP tools are registered and callable from an agent session against a native-mode project.
- `tracker_link_session` writes the session id into the item's `system` object (linked sessions).
- Tool input/output schemas match the `fields`/`system`/indexed-column split from the data-model issue, not an ad-hoc shape.
- MCP per-server secret-grant rule from §7.7 does not apply here (native tracker needs no external credential) — verified no credential prompt appears.

Notes: MCP tool contract reused verbatim per §7.10, referencing `nimbalyst/design/trackers/unified-tracker-system.md:259-336`.

Depends on: Native tracker: TrackerRecord data model and storage

issues.title: Native tracker: kanban/list UI with custom type support
issues.body:
Build the client UI for the native tracker: a kanban board, list view with priority sort and assignee filter, and item create/edit forms that adapt to a project-defined custom type's `roles` mapping as well as built-in Task/Bug/Epic types.

Spec: §7.10 ('Native mode' bullet, custom-type `roles` mapping per `nimbalyst/UserDocs/creating-custom-trackers.md`).

Acceptance:
- Kanban board renders columns from a type's `workflowStatus` role, and cards can be dragged between columns.
- List view supports priority sort and assignee filter for both a built-in and a custom type.
- Creating a custom type and mapping its `roles` (title/workflowStatus/priority/assignee/...) changes how existing generic UI components render it, with no per-type UI code.
- UI is exercised against the MCP-created/updated records so agent writes show up live.

Notes: none beyond §7.10 itself; UI behavior is the direct consequence of the schema-driven roles design.

issues.title: Live tracker slice 1: GitHub issues + comments backend
issues.body:
Implement the GitHub `TrackerBackend` for the first delivery slice: list/get/create/update issues and add comments via GitHub REST, resolving credentials through the connected-accounts registry.

Spec: §7.10 ('GitHub, full feature set' bullet — REST for issues/comments/labels/milestones/assignees; 'Delivery order within v2' slice 1).

Acceptance:
- `list`/`get`/`create`/`update`/`addComment` are implemented against GitHub REST (`docs.github.com/en/rest/issues/*`) for a bound `owner/repo`.
- Credentials come only from `resolveCredential(connectionId)`; the backend never runs its own OAuth or stores a token.
- `capabilities` correctly reports `transitions: false, boards: false, sprints: false` for this slice (added in later slices).
- Runs server-side only (node/supervisor process), never in a client bundle.

Notes: GitHub REST endpoints confirmed at `docs.github.com/en/rest/issues/*`, per §16 'Live GitHub backend' and §7.10.

Depends on: Define TrackerMode config and the pluggable TrackerBackend interface, GitHub connect: OAuth App Device Authorization Grant (default path)

issues.title: Live tracker slice 1: Jira issues + comments backend (REST v3, ADF)
issues.body:
Implement the Jira `TrackerBackend` for the first delivery slice: list/get/create/update issues and add comments via Jira REST v3, using `POST /rest/api/3/search/jql` for search and writing comment bodies as Atlassian Document Format (ADF).

Spec: §7.10 ('Jira, full feature set' bullet — two REST bases, `search/jql`, ADF comments).

Acceptance:
- Issue search uses `POST /rest/api/3/search/jql`, not the deprecated `GET /rest/api/3/search`.
- Comment/update bodies are built as a minimal `{type:'doc', version:1, content:[...]}` ADF document, not raw markdown.
- For an OAuth 3LO connection, every call is routed through `https://api.atlassian.com/ex/jira/{cloudId}/rest/...`; for an API-token connection, calls go straight to the site.
- `capabilities` reports `transitions: false, boards: false, sprints: false` for this slice.

Notes: Two REST bases and the deprecated-search migration are confirmed from the live OpenAPI spec (`developer.atlassian.com/cloud/jira/platform/swagger-v3.v3.json`) and emdash's own history (`jira-issue-provider.ts:248`); ADF write path is the inverse of emdash's `flattenAdf()` (`jira-issue-provider.ts:326-345`) — §16 'Live Jira backend'.

Depends on: Define TrackerMode config and the pluggable TrackerBackend interface, Jira connect: API token path (Basic auth)

issues.title: Live tracker slice 2: GitHub two-state transitions
issues.body:
Add transition support to the GitHub tracker backend: since GitHub has no built-in transition concept, implement `transition` as a fixed open/closed two-state `PATCH .../issues/{n} {state, state_reason}` and update capabilities accordingly.

Spec: §7.10 ('GitHub has no built-in transition concept...a transition is PATCH .../issues/{n} {state, state_reason}'; 'Delivery order within v2' slice 2).

Acceptance:
- `listTransitions`/`transition` are implemented and return exactly the open/closed pair, never a discovered per-project workflow.
- `capabilities.transitions` flips to `true` and the UI reflects the two-state limitation rather than presenting a Jira-style workflow picker.
- Existing slice-1 issue/comment behavior is unchanged.

Notes: §7.10 explicitly calls out this degraded two-state model versus Jira's discovered workflow.

issues.title: Live tracker slice 2: Jira workflow transition discovery
issues.body:
Add transition support to the Jira tracker backend: discover the available transitions for an issue via `GET .../issue/{key}/transitions` before posting one, and support a transition's `fields.resolution`/`update.comment` bodies in ADF.

Spec: §7.10 ('discover transitions via GET .../issue/{key}/transitions before posting one'; 'Delivery order within v2' slice 2).

Acceptance:
- `listTransitions` calls `GET .../issue/{key}/transitions` and returns the project's actual discovered workflow, not a hardcoded set.

issues.title: Live tracker slice 3: Jira boards/sprints via Agile REST
issues.body:
Add board and sprint support to the Jira tracker backend using the Agile REST base: list boards/sprints, and move issues into a sprint.

Spec: §7.10 ('Sprints: GET /rest/agile/1.0/board/{id}/sprint to list, POST /rest/agile/1.0/sprint/{sprintId}/issue {issues:[...]} to move issues...'; 'Delivery order within v2' slice 3).

Acceptance:
- `listBoards` and `listSprints` call the `/rest/agile/1.0/...` base, separate from the `/rest/api/3/...` issue base.
- `moveToSprint` posts to `POST /rest/agile/1.0/sprint/{sprintId}/issue` with the issue-key array.
- Sprint create/start/close via `POST`/`PUT /rest/agile/1.0/sprint[/{id}]` is supported where the UI needs it.
- `capabilities.boards`/`capabilities.sprints` flip to `true`; OAuth 3LO routing (`api.atlassian.com/ex/jira/{cloudId}/...`) is honored for this base too.

Notes: Endpoints confirmed against `developer.atlassian.com/cloud/jira/software/swagger.v3.json` — §16 'Live Jira backend'.

issues.title: Live tracker slice 3: GitHub Projects v2 boards via GraphQL
issues.body:
Add GitHub Projects v2 board support: link issues/PRs onto a project board and move cards between columns/iterations via GraphQL, including the required field-option-id lookup and GraphQL rate-limit budgeting.

Spec: §7.10 ('GitHub...GraphQL for Projects v2...Mutation.addProjectV2ItemById...Mutation.updateProjectV2ItemFieldValue...A required extra round trip...budget for GitHub's GraphQL rate limits'; 'Delivery order within v2' slice 3).

Acceptance:
- `Mutation.addProjectV2ItemById` links an issue/PR onto the configured `projectNumber` board.
- Before any column/iteration move, the backend resolves the target `singleSelectOptionId`/`iterationId` from the field's `options`/`iterations`, then calls `Mutation.updateProjectV2ItemFieldValue`.
- Rate-limit budgeting accounts for GitHub's 5,000 pts/hr primary and 2,000 pts/min secondary GraphQL limits in any batched board update.
- `capabilities.boards` is `true` only when a `projectNumber` is configured on the `GitHubTarget`.

Notes: Exact GraphQL shapes confirmed against `docs.github.com/public/fpt/schema.docs.graphql` (`addProjectV2ItemById` line 26747/input line 824; `updateProjectV2ItemFieldValue` input line 69107; `ProjectV2SingleSelectField` line 41809/`.options` ~41838; `ProjectV2IterationField` line 41514) — §16 'Live GitHub backend, Projects v2'.

Depends on: Live tracker slice 2: GitHub two-state transitions

issues.title: Live tracker: connectivity-error state and attention-inbox failure surfacing
issues.body:
When a node/target or the external tracker API is unreachable or rate-limited for a live-mode project, show an explicit connectivity-error state in the tracker view and fail any agent tool call needing tracker data with a retryable error surfaced in the attention inbox, with no fallback to a queued local write.

Spec: §7.10 ('If a node/target or the external API is unreachable or rate-limited, the tracker view shows an explicit connectivity-error state, and any agent tool call needing tracker data fails with a retryable error surfaced in the attention inbox...it does not fall back to a queued local write').

Acceptance:
- Simulated GitHub/Jira API unreachability renders a distinct connectivity-error state in the tracker view (not a blank/loading state).
- An agent tool call against a live tracker during an outage returns a retryable error and creates an attention-inbox item (§7.13).
- No write-queue or local-snapshot table exists anywhere in the live-mode code path.
- Recovery (API reachable again) clears the connectivity-error state without a manual reset.

Notes: §16 '"Live, no sync" behavior' — explicitly the novel scope cut replacing the old two-way-sync design; no conflict-merge policy exists because there is no local copy to merge against.

Depends on: Live tracker slice 1: GitHub issues + comments backend, Live tracker slice 1: Jira issues + comments backend (REST v3, ADF)

Build the one-time, per-project settings UI for choosing native vs. live tracker mode, and for a live project, picking the connected account and target (GitHub owner/repo/projectNumber, or Jira cloudId/projectKey).

Spec: §7.10 (TrackerMode/GitHubTarget/JiraTarget; 'Every project chooses, once, how it tracks work').

issues.title: ConnectedAccount data model and relay metadata sync
issues.body:
Define the provider-agnostic `ConnectedAccount` type and its relay-synced metadata row (no secret in the synced row), so a device picker can render from any device while the actual token stays node-local.

Spec: §7.26 ('Data model — ConnectedAccount' code block and the paragraph on metadata-only sync).

Acceptance:
- `ConnectedAccount` matches the specced shape exactly, including `id` composed as `${provider}:${host-or-site}:${providerAccountId}`, `providerAccountId` (never login/email), `credentialSource`, `scopes`, `capabilities`, and `secretRef`.
- Only the metadata row (no token) syncs through the relay; `secretRef` names a keyring entry, never embeds a secret value.
- A second device shows the same connected-account list without holding any credential itself.
- No `nodePresence` field exists on the synced type (computed lazily per the node-locality design, tracked in a separate issue).

Notes: Generalized from emdash's shipped `GitHubAccountRegistry` (`emdash/apps/emdash-desktop/src/main/core/github/accounts/github-account-registry.ts:57-190`) — §16 'Connected-account registry shape (GitHub)'.

Depends on: none

issues.title: GitHub connect: OAuth App Device Authorization Grant (default path)
issues.body:
Ship the RFC 8628 device flow as the default GitHub connect path, using a public OAuth App client (no client secret), requesting `repo`, `read:user`, `read:org`, and `read:project` scopes, and resolving identity via `GET /user` keyed on the numeric id.

Spec: §7.26 ('GitHub — connect flow (default: OAuth App Device Authorization Grant)').

Acceptance:
- Device flow completes with a public OAuth App client id only, no secret shipped or required.
- Requested scopes include `repo`, `read:user`, `read:org`, `read:project`.
- Identity resolution calls `GET /user` and keys the new `ConnectedAccount.providerAccountId` on the numeric `id`, never `login`.
- The resulting token is written to the node's OS keyring, referenced only by `secretRef` in the synced metadata row.

Notes: Same mechanism emdash already ships (`emdash/apps/emdash-desktop/src/main/core/github/services/github-device-flow-service.ts:1-114`); identity resolution per `github-identity-client.ts:28-56` — §16 'Connected-account registry shape (GitHub)'. `read:project` scope is new versus emdash, added for §7.10 Projects v2.

Depends on: ConnectedAccount data model and relay metadata sync
issues.epic: Connected accounts: multi-account GitHub/Jira registry + per-project pinning

issues.title: GitHub connect: gh CLI import path
issues.body:
Add a one-shot import path that reads every host+account the local `gh` CLI already holds (including GitHub Enterprise Server hosts) into the ConnectedAccount registry.

Spec: §7.26 ('gh CLI import (github-cli-account-import.ts:56-92), one shot imports every host+account the local gh CLI already holds, including GitHub Enterprise Server hosts)').

Acceptance:
- Running the import against a machine with `gh auth status` logged into multiple hosts creates one `ConnectedAccount` row per host+account.
- Enterprise Server hosts (non-`github.com`) are captured with their own `host` value, not coerced to `github.com`.
- Identity resolution still uses `GET /user` keyed on numeric id per host, consistent with the device-flow path.

Notes: `emdash/apps/emdash-desktop/src/main/core/github/accounts/github-cli-account-import.ts:56-92` — §16 'Connected-account registry shape (GitHub)'.

Depends on: ConnectedAccount data model and relay metadata sync

issues.title: GitHub connect: fine-grained PAT manual paste path
issues.body:
Add a manual fine-grained personal-access-token paste path for orgs that enable OAuth App access restrictions, which can block the device flow outright.

Spec: §7.26 ('a manual fine-grained PAT paste, needed specifically for orgs that enable OAuth App access restrictions...which can block the device flow outright').

Acceptance:
- A PAT paste form validates the token against `GET /user` before saving, using the same numeric-id identity resolution as the other GitHub paths.
- The resulting `ConnectedAccount.credentialSource` is `fine_grained_pat`.
- Failure mode (invalid/expired/insufficiently-scoped token) gives an explicit, actionable error rather than a silent no-op.

Notes: OAuth App access restrictions are real and enabled-by-default per GitHub's own docs (`docs.github.com/en/organizations/restricting-access-to-your-organizations-data/about-oauth-app-access-restrictions`) — §16.

Depends on: ConnectedAccount data model and relay metadata sync

issues.title: Jira connect: API token path (Basic auth)
issues.body:
Add the zero-infrastructure Jira connect path using an account-scoped API token over Basic auth (`base64(email:token)`), keyed on the stable `accountId` from `GET /rest/api/3/myself` rather than the mutable email.

Spec: §7.26 ('API token (Basic auth, base64(email:token), Atlassian's account-scoped token model — the zero-infrastructure default, generalized to multiple registry rows)').

Acceptance:
- Connect form takes `{siteUrl, email, apiToken}`, calls `GET /rest/api/3/myself`, and stores `ConnectedAccount` keyed on `(siteUrl, accountId)`.
- Connecting a second Jira site does not overwrite the first (unlike emdash's current single-row behavior).
- `credentialSource` is `api_token`; the token itself lives only in the node keyring.

Notes: Fixes emdash's single-account limitation (`emdash/apps/emdash-desktop/src/main/core/jira/jira-connection-service.ts:8,21,39-56`, keyed on mutable `email`) — §16 'Connected-account registry shape (Jira)'; token docs at `support.atlassian.com/atlassian-account/docs/manage-api-tokens-for-your-atlassian-account/`.

Depends on: ConnectedAccount data model and relay metadata sync
issues.epic: Connected accounts: multi-account GitHub/Jira registry + per-project pinning

issues.title: Jira connect: OAuth 2.0 (3LO) with multi-site accessible-resources discovery
issues.body:
Add the Jira OAuth 2.0 (3LO) connect path as the upgrade over API tokens: after the redirect-code exchange, enumerate every `{cloudId, url, name}` the grant covers via `accessible-resources` and register one `ConnectedAccount` per site from a single consent.

Spec: §7.26 ('Jira OAuth 2.0 (3LO)...specifically because one consent can register several sites in a single grant: after the redirect-code exchange, call GET https://api.atlassian.com/oauth/token/accessible-resources...').

Acceptance:
- A single OAuth consent flow can register two or more Jira sites as separate `ConnectedAccount` rows when the granting user has access to multiple sites.
- `credentialSource` is `oauth_3lo`; `scopes` is populated (introspectable), unlike the `null` scopes on Basic-auth tokens.
- loombox's own registered Atlassian OAuth app (client id/secret) is documented as an explicit, ongoing maintenance dependency, not assumed free.
- All subsequent Jira REST calls for a 3LO-sourced account route through `api.atlassian.com/ex/jira/{cloudId}/rest/...`, confirmed by the tracker-backend issues.

Notes: `developer.atlassian.com/cloud/jira/platform/oauth-2-3lo-apps/`; granular scopes at `developer.atlassian.com/cloud/jira/software/scopes-for-oauth-2-3LO-and-forge-apps/` — §16 'Connected-account registry shape (Jira)'.

Depends on: Jira connect: API token path (Basic auth)

issues.title: Per-project per-capability account pinning with hard-fail resolution
issues.body:
Generalize emdash's tri-state `ProjectSettings.githubAccountId` resolver into a per-capability pin map (`{github?, jira?, [capability]: string|null|undefined}`), with resolution that hard-fails on a host/site mismatch instead of silently falling back, and requires an explicit pin for any write-back action.

Spec: §7.26 ('Per-project binding, one pinned account per capability').

Acceptance:
- Pin map supports absent-key (unconfigured), explicit `null` (opted-out), and a string (pinned `ConnectedAccount.id`) tri-state per capability, matching emdash's resolver semantics.
- Resolving a pinned account whose host/site does not match the project's configured target raises a hard error, never a silent fallback to a different account.
- Any write-back action (comment, transition, status change) refuses to run without an explicit pin; read-only actions may use a silent default only when one is unambiguous.
- Unit tests cover all three pin states plus the mismatch-hard-fail case.

Notes: Generalizes `project-github-auth-context-resolver.ts:51-102` and its `githubApiAccountHostMismatch` guard (`github-api-auth-service.ts:40-67`) — §16 'Connected-account registry shape (GitHub)'.

Depends on: ConnectedAccount data model and relay metadata sync
issues.epic: Connected accounts: multi-account GitHub/Jira registry + per-project pinning

issues.title: Node-locality: lazy per-node connected-account presence check
issues.body:
Since connecting an account on one node does not make its secret usable on another node, compute node-locality lazily at point of use: when a project picker or write action targets a node, ask that node whether it holds a local secret for the pinned connectionId, and warn before a session on an uncredentialled node silently fails a tracker write.

Spec: §7.26 ('Node-locality — a real gap this area introduces, kept lazy rather than a new synced subsystem').

Acceptance:
- No synced per-node presence map is added to the `ConnectedAccount` type or relay schema.
- Targeting a node for a write action triggers a live query to that node (reusing the §7.21 node-health reachability channel) asking whether it holds the pinned connection's secret.
- If the node lacks the secret, the user sees an explicit warning before the write action proceeds, rather than the action failing opaquely mid-flight.
- Behavior is verified across at least two nodes (e.g. devbox + a second target) where only one holds a given connected account's local secret.

Notes: Explicitly designed to avoid over-engineering for the realistic solo-operator topology of §9 (one or two nodes) — §7.26.

Depends on: Per-project per-capability account pinning with hard-fail resolution, Node/target status view: reachability, agent-process health, CPU/RAM/disk

issues.title: Safe-disconnect: scan and warn before removing a pinned account
issues.body:
Before letting a user disconnect a `ConnectedAccount` that is still pinned in one or more project capability maps, scan all project settings and warn which projects/capabilities would be affected.

Spec: §7.26 ('Before letting a user disconnect an account still pinned somewhere, scan all project settings and warn').

Acceptance:
- Disconnect flow runs a scan across all projects' pin maps for the target `ConnectedAccount.id` before completing.
- If any project/capability is pinned to it, the user sees a specific list of affected projects/capabilities and must confirm.
- Disconnecting an account with no pins proceeds without the extra confirmation step.

Notes: Generalizes `count-projects-using-github-account.ts:17-33` — §7.26.

Depends on: Per-project per-capability account pinning with hard-fail resolution
issues.epic: Connected accounts: multi-account GitHub/Jira registry + per-project pinning

issues.title: Connected accounts management UI (connect, list, pin, disconnect)
issues.body:
Build the client UI for the connected-accounts registry: list connected GitHub/Jira accounts with their label/avatar, trigger each connect path, show per-project per-capability pin pickers, and surface the disconnect-safety warning.

Spec: §7.26 (overall section; boundary-with-login paragraph on keeping this UI separate from account login).

Acceptance:
- Account list renders `label`/`avatarUrl` derived from the identity call, never free text, for both GitHub and Jira rows.
- UI exposes all connect paths built in this epic (device flow, CLI import, PAT paste, API token, 3LO) behind clear provider-specific entry points.
- Per-project capability pin picker only lets the user select accounts whose `host`/site matches the project's configured target.
- UI is visibly and functionally distinct from the loombox account-login screen (§8) — logging out of loombox does not disconnect any account here, and vice versa.

Notes: Mirrors emdash's own `signIn(provider)` vs `linkProviderAccount(provider)` UI split (`emdash-account-service.ts:95-171`) — §7.26.

Depends on: Per-project per-capability account pinning with hard-fail resolution, Safe-disconnect: scan and warn before removing a pinned account
