issues.title: Commit graph and branch tree view (local + ssh: targets)
issues.body:
Build the commit-graph and branch-tree viewer for a project that is a git repo, working identically on `local` and `ssh:` targets.

Spec: §7.6 ('view the commit graph and branch tree').

Acceptance:
- Commit graph renders parent/child relationships and branch labels for a repo on a `local` target.
- The same view works unmodified against an `ssh:` target project, sourcing git data through the node/supervisor rather than a client-side git binary.
- View updates when a session on that project makes a new commit.

Notes: none beyond §7.6 itself.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Staged/unstaged diff viewer with hunk-level stage/unstage/discard
issues.body:
Build the diff-review surface for inspecting staged/unstaged changes and per-file diffs, with hunk-level stage, unstage, and discard actions.

Spec: §7.6 ('inspect staged/unstaged changes and per-file diffs; stage, unstage, and discard hunks').

Acceptance:
- Per-file diff view distinguishes staged vs. unstaged hunks.
- A user can stage, unstage, or discard an individual hunk (not just a whole file) from the UI.
- Discarding a hunk is confirmed before applying (destructive action) and works on both `local` and `ssh:` targets.

Notes: none beyond §7.6 itself.

Depends on: Commit graph and branch tree view (local + ssh: targets)

issues.title: Commit workflow with AI-generated commit messages
issues.body:
Let the user commit staged changes, with the agent drafting a commit message from the diff that the user can accept or edit before committing.

Spec: §7.6 ('commit (with AI-generated messages)'; 'AI assists explain a diff, draft a commit or PR description...').

Acceptance:
- Commit action offers an AI-drafted message generated from the staged diff.
- User can edit the drafted message before committing; committing with no edits uses the draft verbatim.
- Commit succeeds on both `local` and `ssh:` targets and the new commit appears in the commit-graph view.

Notes: none beyond §7.6 itself.

Depends on: Staged/unstaged diff viewer with hunk-level stage/unstage/discard

issues.title: Branch operations: create, switch, merge, stash
issues.body:
Implement branch create/switch/merge and stash operations from the git UI.

Spec: §7.6 ('create, switch, and merge branches; stash').

Acceptance:
- User can create a new branch from any existing branch/commit and switch the working tree to it.
- Merge of one branch into another surfaces a clear success/conflict result (conflict handling covered by the merge-conflict-assist issue).
- Stash and stash-pop are available and reflected in the branch-tree view.
- All operations work on both `local` and `ssh:` targets.

Notes: none beyond §7.6 itself.

Depends on: Commit graph and branch tree view (local + ssh: targets)

issues.title: Push workflow
issues.body:
Implement pushing a branch (with upstream tracking) from the git UI, on both local and ssh: targets.

Spec: §7.6 ('...and push').

Acceptance:
- Push sets upstream tracking on first push of a new branch.
- Push failure (e.g. non-fast-forward) surfaces a clear, actionable error rather than a silent no-op.
- Works identically on `local` and `ssh:` targets, using the node's own credentials (never the relay/client) per §8's SSH-credential rule.

Notes: SSH credentials never leave the node that uses them (§8).

Depends on: Branch operations: create, switch, merge, stash

issues.title: AI diff-explain assist
issues.body:
Add an AI assist that explains a selected diff in plain language on request, for a reviewer who wants a summary before reading the raw hunks.

Spec: §7.6 ('AI assists explain a diff...').

Acceptance:
- Selecting a diff (or a single hunk) offers an 'explain' action that returns a plain-language summary.
- Explanation reflects only the selected diff scope (not the whole session transcript).
- Feature degrades gracefully (clear disabled state) when no agent/provider is available to generate it.

Notes: none beyond §7.6 itself.

Depends on: Staged/unstaged diff viewer with hunk-level stage/unstage/discard

issues.title: AI-assisted merge-conflict resolution
issues.body:
When a merge produces conflicts, let the agent propose a resolution for each conflicted hunk that the user can review and accept before completing the merge.

Spec: §7.6 ('...help resolve conflicts').

Acceptance:
- A conflicted merge presents each conflict hunk with an AI-proposed resolution alongside the raw conflict markers.
- User must explicitly accept a proposed resolution per hunk (or edit it) before the merge can be finalized; nothing auto-applies unattended.
- Merge only completes once every conflicted hunk has an accepted resolution.

Notes: none beyond §7.6 itself.

Depends on: Branch operations: create, switch, merge, stash

issues.title: Open a pull request from a session's branch via gh
issues.body:
Let the user open a PR from a session's pushed branch directly from the cockpit, using the `gh` CLI, with an AI-drafted PR description.

Spec: §7.14 ('open a pull request from a session's branch...' via gh); §7.6 ('draft a commit or PR description').

Acceptance:
- PR-open action shells out to `gh pr create` (or its API equivalent) against the session's pushed branch.
- PR description is AI-drafted from the branch's commits/diff and editable before creation.
- Created PR's URL/number is stored against the session so later CI/review steps can reference it.

Notes: none beyond §7.14 itself (GitHub first, per spec; GitLab explicitly later).

Depends on: Push workflow

issues.title: CI check status watcher with agent auto-iterate on failure
issues.body:
Watch a PR's CI checks and surface failures back to the agent so it can auto-iterate a fix, closing the loop from red check to a new fix commit.

Spec: §7.14 ('watch CI checks, surface failures back to the agent (which can auto-iterate a fix)').

Acceptance:
- CI status for an open PR is polled/watched and its pass/fail state is visible in the cockpit.
- A failing check can be sent to the session as a prompt (failure output attached) so the agent can attempt a fix.
- A red check creates an attention-inbox item per §7.13.

Notes: none beyond §7.14 itself; feeds the test-runner epic for local reproduction of the failure.

Depends on: Open a pull request from a session's branch via gh

issues.title: Review comment handling and merge workflow
issues.body:
Surface PR review comments in the cockpit and let the user (or the agent, on request) respond, and implement the merge action once checks and reviews are satisfied.

Spec: §7.14 ('handle review comments, and merge').

Acceptance:
- Review comments on an open PR are fetched and displayed against their commit/line context.
- A review comment can be sent to the session as a prompt for the agent to address.
- Merge action is available once CI is green and required reviews are satisfied, and reports success/failure clearly.
- A review request creates an attention-inbox item per §7.13.

Notes: none beyond §7.14 itself.

Depends on: CI check status watcher with agent auto-iterate on failure

issues.title: PR linkage write-back for native tracker projects
issues.body:
When a PR is opened/merged for a native-tracker project, record the linkage on the tracker item's own system fields (linkedPullRequests, linkedCommitSha).

Spec: §7.14 ('for a native project, a merged PR is recorded on the tracker item's own system.linkedPullRequests/linkedCommitSha fields').

Acceptance:
- Opening a PR against a native-tracker item appends it to `system.linkedPullRequests`.
- A merge writes `linkedCommitSha` for the merge commit.
- Linkage is visible in the native tracker UI (kanban/list) for the affected item, with no separate sync step.

Notes: none beyond §7.14 itself; depends on the native TrackerRecord schema from the tracker epic.

Depends on: Native tracker: TrackerRecord data model and storage, Open a pull request from a session's branch via gh

issues.title: PR linkage write-back for live tracker projects
issues.body:
For a live-tracker project, write the same PR-linkage event back through that provider's TrackerBackend: rely on GitHub's own issue-closing keywords for auto-link, and post an explicit comment/link via addComment/update for Jira.

Spec: §7.14 ('for a live project, the same event is written back through that provider's TrackerBackend (a GitHub PR auto-links via its own issue-closing keywords; a Jira issue gets an explicit comment/link via addComment/update)').

Acceptance:
- A GitHub live-mode project relies on issue-closing keywords in the PR description (e.g. `Closes #123`) rather than a separate API call to link.
- A Jira live-mode project gets an explicit `addComment`/`update` call linking the PR when one is opened/merged.
- Behavior is verified against both slice-1 backends from the tracker epic.

Notes: none beyond §7.14 itself.

Depends on: Live tracker slice 1: GitHub issues + comments backend, Live tracker slice 1: Jira issues + comments backend (REST v3, ADF), Open a pull request from a session's branch via gh

issues.title: Attention-inbox integration for CI/review events
issues.body:
Ensure a red CI check or a review request lands in the cross-project attention inbox uniformly, regardless of which tracker mode the project uses.

Spec: §7.14 ('Either way, a red CI check or a review request lands in the attention inbox (§7.13) the same way, regardless of which tracker mode the project uses.').

Acceptance:
- A failing check and an incoming review request each create exactly one attention-inbox item, tagged with the source project/session.
- Behavior is identical whether the project is native-tracker or live-tracker mode.
- Items are actionable inline (open the PR/session) per §7.13's inbox contract.

Notes: none beyond §7.13/§7.14.

Depends on: CI check status watcher with agent auto-iterate on failure, Review comment handling and merge workflow

issues.title: Test/lint/build runner surface with streaming results
issues.body:
Build a first-class UI surface (not a raw terminal) to run a project's tests, lint, and build commands and stream their results live.

Spec: §7.15 ('A first-class surface to run a project's tests/lint/build (not just a raw terminal), stream results...').

Acceptance:
- Runner UI has distinct actions for test/lint/build (or a configured subset) and streams stdout/stderr as the command runs, not just a final result.
- Pass/fail state is shown per run with the command's exit code.
- Works on both `local` and `ssh:` targets through the node/supervisor channel.

Notes: State shape modeled on nimbalyst `playwright/testRunner.ts` — §16 'Test runner'.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Per-project test/lint/build command configuration and auto-detection
issues.body:
Let a project's test/lint/build commands be configured once, with a heuristic to auto-detect sensible defaults (e.g. from package.json scripts) so most projects need no manual setup.

Spec: §7.15 ('Per-project commands are configured once (or auto-detected).').

Acceptance:
- A project settings panel lets the user set/override the test/lint/build commands explicitly.
- Auto-detection proposes commands for at least the common cases in this monorepo's own stack (pnpm scripts) without requiring manual entry.
- Auto-detected commands are shown to the user for confirmation before being saved, not silently applied.

Notes: Command auto-detection heuristic is explicitly called out as novel (no in-repo precedent) — §16 'Test runner'.

Depends on: Test/lint/build runner surface with streaming results

issues.title: Agent auto-iterate-until-green loop
issues.body:
Let the agent automatically re-run the configured test/lint/build commands after a fix attempt and keep iterating until the run is green or a bound is hit.

Spec: §7.15 ('...let the agent iterate on failures automatically until green.').

Acceptance:
- On a failing run, the agent can be invoked with the failure output and produce a fix, then the runner re-executes automatically.
- Loop has an explicit stop condition (max iterations or user cancel) so it cannot run unbounded.
- Each iteration's result is visible in the runner UI's history, not just the final one.

Notes: none beyond §7.15 itself.

Depends on: Per-project test/lint/build command configuration and auto-detection

issues.title: Test runner integration with PR/CI loop and attention inbox
issues.body:
Wire the test runner into the PR/CI lifecycle and the attention inbox: a local test failure that blocks a PR should be reproducible from the runner, and a runner failure needing attention should surface in the inbox.

Spec: §7.15 ('Feeds the PR/CI loop and the inbox.').

Acceptance:
- A CI failure surfaced from the PR/CI epic can be re-run locally from the test runner with one action.
- A runner failure that the agent cannot auto-resolve within the iteration bound creates an attention-inbox item.
- No duplicate inbox items are created for the same underlying failure across CI-watcher and runner paths.

Notes: none beyond §7.15 itself.

Depends on: Agent auto-iterate-until-green loop, CI check status watcher with agent auto-iterate on failure

issues.title: Per-session usage meter with subagent-exclusion rule
issues.body:
Track per-session context-window usage, tokens, and estimated cost from ACP usage_update events, with a live meter, excluding any usage_update attributable to a nested/subagent tool call from the session's live context-fill percentage.

Spec: §7.9 (whole section, especially the subagent-exclusion paragraph).

Acceptance:
- Live meter shows current context-fill percentage and token/cost counters for a session, updating on each `usage_update`.
- A subagent tool call's `usage_update` is excluded from the parent session's context-fill percentage calculation (meter does not visibly bounce to a smaller subagent context size).
- The same subagent usage is still included in that session's cumulative cost figure.
- Meter surfaces a near-context-limit warning at a sensible threshold.

Notes: `usage_update` is session-level with no per-tool attribution in ACP itself; the subagent-exclusion split is flagged as a client-side heuristic, not an ACP-native field — §16 'Agent interaction (ACP) — corrections applied'.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Aggregate spend-over-time view per project/provider
issues.body:
Build the aggregate cost view showing spend over time, rolled up per project and per provider, including subagent usage in the rollup.

Spec: §7.9 ('shows spend over time per project/provider'; 'Subagent usage is still included in the cumulative cost figure and the per-project/provider spend-over-time view').

Acceptance:
- View aggregates cost across sessions, grouped/filterable by project and by provider.
- Subagent-attributed cost is included in this rollup (unlike the per-session live meter's percentage).
- Data underlying this view is the same rollup consumed by the spend-cap feature (§7.16), verified by a shared aggregation source rather than two divergent calculations.

Notes: none beyond §7.9 itself.

Depends on: Per-session usage meter with subagent-exclusion rule

issues.title: Context-limit-near warning surfacing
issues.body:
Surface a clear warning when a session is near its provider's context limit, distinct from the ongoing live meter, so the user knows to wrap up or start fresh.

Spec: §7.9 ('Surfaces when a session is near its context limit...').

Acceptance:
- A distinct, dismissable warning appears in the session view once context-fill crosses a defined near-limit threshold.
- Warning does not fire from subagent-attributed usage per the exclusion rule.
- Warning is visible from the attention inbox as well as the live session view.

Notes: none beyond §7.9 itself.

Depends on: Per-session usage meter with subagent-exclusion rule

issues.title: Per-project and per-session spend caps with auto-pause
issues.body:
Implement a per-project and per-session cost cap that auto-pauses the session once exceeded and raises it in the attention inbox, consuming the same cost rollup as the spend-over-time view (including subagent cost).

Spec: §7.16 ('Spend caps: a per-project and per-session cost cap that auto-pauses the session and raises it in the attention inbox (§7.13) — governance covers dollars, not only CPU/RAM (§7.9 is meter-only).').

Acceptance:
- A configurable spend cap can be set per project and per session.
- Crossing the cap auto-pauses the running session (agent process paused/suspended, not silently killed) and creates an attention-inbox item.
- Cap evaluation includes subagent-attributed cost, matching §7.9's cumulative-cost rollup (a runaway subagent can trip the cap).
- Resuming a paused session requires an explicit user action (e.g. raise the cap or confirm continuing).

Notes: Explicitly distinguished from §7.9 (meter-only) — this is enforcement, per §7.16.

Depends on: Aggregate spend-over-time view per project/provider

issues.title: Per-target concurrency caps with overflow queue
issues.body:
Enforce a configurable maximum number of concurrently running agent sessions per target, queuing overflow sessions instead of starting them immediately.

Spec: §7.16 ('Per-target concurrency caps, a queue for overflow...').

Acceptance:
- Starting more sessions than a target's configured cap queues the excess rather than launching them.
- Queued sessions start automatically as running ones finish or are stopped, in FIFO order (or a documented alternative policy).
- Cap is configurable per target and defaults to a sane value reflecting that target's known resources.

Notes: none beyond §7.16 itself.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Per-target CPU/RAM/disk resource sampling
issues.body:
Sample CPU/RAM/disk usage per execution target so sessions can be throttled or queued based on real resource pressure rather than session count alone.

Spec: §7.16 ('...resource awareness (CPU/RAM/disk per target — e.g. the devbox's 8 cores / 16 GB) so sessions are throttled or queued instead of OOM-ing.').

Acceptance:
- Per-target CPU, RAM, and disk usage is sampled at a regular interval and exposed to the concurrency/queueing logic.
- A target under high memory pressure queues new session starts even if under its raw concurrency cap.
- Disk-space sampling is included, not just CPU/RAM.

Notes: Resource sampling modeled on emdash `resource-monitor/resource-sampler.ts` (`pidusage`); disk-per-target via `check-disk-space` is flagged as the novel part — §16 'Resource sampling'.

Depends on: Per-target concurrency caps with overflow queue

issues.title: Bounded fan-out backpressure with drop-oldest and resync marker
issues.body:
Implement bounded per-client output queues for session event fan-out so a slow client (e.g. a phone on cellular) never blocks faster clients or the supervisor's own persistence, dropping oldest events and inserting a resync marker on overflow.

Spec: §7.16 ('Fan-out backpressure: bounded per-client output queues with drop-oldest + a resync marker on overflow, so a slow client...never blocks faster clients or the supervisor's own persistence.').

Acceptance:
- Each client connection has a bounded output queue independent of other clients' queues and the supervisor's own persistence path.
- When a client's queue overflows, the oldest buffered events are dropped and a resync marker is delivered instead, prompting that client to re-sync state.
- A deliberately slow/disconnected test client does not delay event delivery to a fast client on the same session.

Notes: Node `stream` highWaterMark + ring buffer keyed by a `seq`, or adopting socket.io `connectionStateRecovery` — §16 'Fan-out backpressure' flags this as a pick-one decision.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Load and concurrency-limits UI
issues.body:
Show current load per target and let the user set per-target concurrency limits from the UI, backed by the sampling and cap enforcement built in this epic.

Spec: §7.16 ('Shows current load and lets the user set limits per target.').

Acceptance:
- UI shows current running/queued session counts and CPU/RAM/disk usage per target.
- User can edit a target's concurrency cap from this view and see it take effect on the next session start/queue decision.
- View updates live as sessions start, finish, or get queued.

Notes: none beyond §7.16 itself.

Depends on: Per-target CPU/RAM/disk resource sampling

issues.title: Per-project permission policy: allow/deny command and network patterns
issues.body:
Implement a per-project permission policy that lets the operator define allow/deny patterns for commands and network access an agent may execute, as the hard guardrail layer independent of ACP's own discretionary approval gate.

Spec: §7.17 ('a per-project permission policy (allow/deny command and network patterns)'; 'the hard guardrails and sandboxing above are the enforcement that does not depend on the agent cooperating').

Acceptance:
- A project's permission policy supports allow and deny rules matched against both the command being run and the network destination.
- A command/network access matching a deny rule is blocked at the enforcement layer even if the agent's own ACP `session/request_permission` never asks (since ACP permission is agent-discretionary, MAY not MUST).
- Policy violations are logged and surfaced to the user, not silently swallowed.

Notes: ACP `session/request_permission` is agent-discretionary (MAY, not MUST); hard guardrails are the real enforcement — §16 'Agent interaction (ACP) — corrections applied', and §7.17 itself.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Sandboxing: namespace/bind-mount scoping to the session worktree
issues.body:
Design and implement sandboxing of agent execution toward namespace/bind-mount scoping to the session's worktree, using OS containers where available on Linux, with a documented weaker fallback on macOS, and ensure it is on by default once shipped.

Spec: §7.17 ('Sandboxing target: design toward namespace/bind-mount scoping to the session's worktree (OS containers where available; a documented weaker fallback on macOS). "Optional" must not mean "off by default" once it ships (v2).').

Acceptance:
- On Linux, an agent process for a session is confined via OS-level namespacing/bind-mounts scoped to that session's worktree.
- macOS runs with a documented, explicitly weaker fallback (not silently identical protection), with the gap called out in user-facing docs/UI.
- Sandboxing is enabled by default for new sessions once this ships; disabling it requires an explicit, visible opt-out, not a default-off toggle.
- An escape attempt (e.g. a command trying to touch a path outside the worktree) is blocked or clearly logged as denied.

Notes: Open design point per §14 ('Agent guardrail/sandbox model on a headless box...is an open design point'); no existing in-repo precedent, so this must be designed and tested fresh per §17's novelty framing.

Depends on: Per-project permission policy: allow/deny command and network patterns

issues.title: Per-project scoped secret/env injection for agent execution
issues.body:
Implement per-project secret/env injection (for running servers and tests) that reaches only the executing node's agent process, and never the relay or any client.

Spec: §7.17 ('secret/env injection scoped per project (for running servers and tests) that never reaches the relay or clients'); §8 ('env/secrets scoped per project and injected only on the executing node — never sent to the relay or clients').

Acceptance:
- Project secrets are stored via the node's OS-native keyring binding (per §8's `@napi-rs/keyring` rule), with a documented fallback for a headless box with no keyring session.
- Injected env vars are visible to the spawned agent/test process only, and are confirmed absent from any relay-bound message or client payload (transcript, logs, usage events).
- A secret can be scoped to one project and is not accessible from a session on a different project on the same node.

Notes: Secrets at rest use `@napi-rs/keyring` (headless Node, not Electron `safeStorage`) — §8, §16 'Secrets at rest'.

Depends on: Sandboxing: namespace/bind-mount scoping to the session worktree

issues.title: Session templates/presets (target, provider, MCP set, starting prompt)
issues.body:
Build reusable session templates/presets that bundle a target, provider, MCP server set, and starting prompt, so starting a common session type (e.g. 'bugfix' or 'feature') is one click.

Spec: §7.18 ('reusable session templates/presets (a "bugfix" or "feature" session with target, provider, MCP set, and starting prompt in one click)').

Acceptance:
- A template captures target, provider, MCP server set, and starting prompt as one saved unit.
- Launching a session from a template pre-fills all of those fields and starts (or stages, pending one confirm) the session immediately.
- Templates can be created, edited, and deleted from the UI, and are project-scoped or global as the user chooses.

Notes: none beyond §7.18 itself.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Per-project agent instructions surface (AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md)
issues.body:
Surface and let the user view and edit a project's own AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md agent-instructions file directly from the cockpit.

Spec: §7.18 ('per-project agent instructions (surface and edit the project's AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md)').

Acceptance:
- Opening a project's settings shows its AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md content (whichever exists) in an editable view.
- Saving an edit writes back to the file on the executing node/target, not just client-local state.
- If neither file exists, the UI offers to create one rather than showing a blank/broken state.

Notes: none beyond §7.18 itself.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Reusable prompt/snippet library
issues.body:
Build a reusable prompt/snippet library the user can save phrasing to and insert into any session's composer.

Spec: §7.18 ('a reusable prompt/snippet library').

Acceptance:
- User can save a prompt/snippet with a name/label from the composer.
- Saved snippets are searchable/browsable and insertable into any session's composer, not just the one they were saved from.
- Library persists across sessions and devices (synced like other account-scoped metadata).

Notes: none beyond §7.18 itself.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Searchable session transcript archive
issues.body:
Build the archive view listing every session's transcript and actions for audit purposes (what each agent did, when, at what cost), browsable across projects.

Spec: §7.19 ('A searchable archive of every session's transcript and actions (audit: what each agent did, when, at what cost)...').

Acceptance:
- Archive lists past sessions with project, timestamp, and cost summary (reusing §7.9's cost rollup).
- Selecting a past session opens its full transcript in read-only form.
- Archive spans multiple projects in one view, not just one project at a time.

Notes: none beyond §7.19 itself.

Depends on: Per-session usage meter with subagent-exclusion rule

issues.title: Client-side transcript search via CSS Custom Highlight API
issues.body:
Implement search over session transcript content that runs entirely client-side, over content the device has already decrypted, using the CSS Custom Highlight API to highlight matches, acknowledging that cross-device completeness is bounded by what each device has synced.

Spec: §7.19 ('Search runs client-side, over content a device has already decrypted...cross-device completeness is bounded by what each device has synced.'); roadmap §12 ('client-side transcript search via the CSS Custom Highlight API (§7.19, §7.24)').

Acceptance:
- Search executes entirely in the client against already-decrypted transcript content; no plaintext search query or index is sent to the relay.
- Matches are visually highlighted using the CSS Custom Highlight API (not manual DOM-wrapping of matched text).
- A device that has not yet synced a given session's content correctly shows that session as absent from results rather than erroring, with this limitation reflected in the UI copy.

Notes: This mechanism has no in-repo precedent and is flagged novel per the roadmap's own callout in §12.

Depends on: Searchable session transcript archive

issues.title: Fork a past session as the start of a new one
issues.body:
Let the user fork a past session: start a new session pre-seeded with that session's transcript/context and workspace state, diverging from that point.

Spec: §7.19 ('the ability to fork...a past session as the start of a new one').

Acceptance:
- Forking a past session creates a new session whose starting context includes the source session's transcript/history.
- The new session's workspace state matches the source session's state at fork time (coordinating with the checkpoint mechanism where a git worktree or checkpoint exists).
- The original session is left untouched by the fork.

Notes: none beyond §7.19 itself; coordinates with the checkpoint/rollback epic for workspace-state reproduction.

Depends on: Searchable session transcript archive

issues.title: Replay a past session
issues.body:
Let the user replay a past session's transcript as a step-through or continuous playback, for review or onboarding purposes, distinct from forking (which starts new work).

Spec: §7.19 ('...or replay a past session as the start of a new one').

Acceptance:
- Replay steps through a past session's messages/tool calls/diffs in original order at a controllable pace.
- Replay is read-only by default and clearly distinguished in the UI from live session steering.
- Replay can be paused/resumed/scrubbed.

Notes: none beyond §7.19 itself.

Depends on: Searchable session transcript archive

issues.title: Git-based checkpoint and rollback for git projects
issues.body:
Snapshot a session's git worktree state at a point in time and let the user roll back an agent's changes safely, independent of git commits (i.e. without requiring the agent to have committed).

Spec: §7.20 ('Snapshot a session's workspace (worktree state) at a point in time and roll back an agent's changes safely if a run goes wrong, independent of git commits.').

Acceptance:
- A checkpoint can be taken at any point during a session, capturing full worktree state (staged, unstaged, and untracked-but-relevant changes) without requiring a commit.
- Rolling back to a checkpoint restores the worktree to that exact state, discarding any changes made after it.
- Rollback works even when the agent never committed anything since the checkpoint.

Notes: none beyond §7.20 itself; no in-repo precedent cited in §16 for this mechanism.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Filesystem-snapshot checkpoint for non-git projects
issues.body:
For a project that is not a git repository, implement checkpointing as a filesystem snapshot / content-hash of the working set rather than a git state, per the folder-not-repo project model.

Spec: §7.20 ('For non-git projects (§6), a checkpoint is a filesystem snapshot / content-hash of the working set rather than a git state.'); §6 ('Project — any folder...It does not have to be a git repository.').

Acceptance:
- Checkpointing a non-git project computes a content-hash-based snapshot of the working set without requiring `git init`.
- Rolling back restores files to the snapshotted content/hash state.
- Feature works for the same class of project that §7.1's worktree-optional/folder-not-repo model already supports.

Notes: none beyond §7.20 itself.

Depends on: Git-based checkpoint and rollback for git projects

issues.title: Checkpoint/rollback UI flow
issues.body:
Build the UI for creating, listing, and rolling back to checkpoints from a session view, working uniformly for both git and non-git projects.

Spec: §7.20 (whole section).

Acceptance:
- Session view lets the user create a named or auto-labeled checkpoint on demand.
- A list of a session's checkpoints is browsable with timestamps and shows which backing mechanism (git snapshot vs. filesystem hash) applies.
- Rollback requires explicit confirmation (destructive action) and reports success/failure clearly.

Notes: none beyond §7.20 itself.

Depends on: Git-based checkpoint and rollback for git projects, Filesystem-snapshot checkpoint for non-git projects

issues.title: Node/target status view: reachability, agent-process health, CPU/RAM/disk
issues.body:
Build a status view of nodes and targets showing reachability, agent-process health, and each target's CPU/RAM/disk, so the user can see why a session stalled and whether a target is overloaded.

Spec: §7.21 ('A status view of nodes and targets: reachability, agent-process health, and each target's CPU/RAM/disk (the devbox included), so the user can see why a session stalled and whether a target is overloaded (pairs with §7.16).').

Acceptance:
- View lists every known node and target with a reachability indicator (up/unreachable) refreshed on a regular interval.
- Each target shows current CPU/RAM/disk figures, reusing the sampling built for §7.16's resource governance.
- A stalled session's view links back to this status view for its target, so the cause (overload vs. unreachable vs. process crash) is visible in one click.

Notes: none beyond §7.21 itself; shares its sampling source with the concurrency-governance epic.

Depends on: Per-target CPU/RAM/disk resource sampling

issues.title: Relay /health endpoint for external uptime monitoring
issues.body:
Expose a `/health` endpoint on the relay for an external uptime check, since alerting cannot depend on the relay itself being up to notify about its own downtime.

Spec: §7.21 ('The relay also exposes a /health endpoint watched by an external uptime check, since alerting can't depend on the relay itself.').

Acceptance:
- `GET /health` on the relay returns a fast, unauthenticated 200 when the relay's core dependencies (Postgres, Redis) are reachable, and a non-200 otherwise.
- Endpoint does not require any session/auth token, since an external uptime checker has none.
- Documentation notes an external uptime service (e.g. a third-party ping check) should be pointed at this endpoint, since the relay cannot alert on its own outage.

Notes: none beyond §7.21 itself.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Stalled-session diagnostics pairing with resource governance
issues.body:
Add diagnostics that pinpoint why a specific session stalled (target overload, node unreachable, agent process crashed, queued behind a concurrency cap) surfaced directly from the session view.

Spec: §7.21 ('...so the user can see why a session stalled and whether a target is overloaded (pairs with §7.16).').

Acceptance:
- A stalled session shows a specific diagnosis (e.g. 'queued: target at concurrency cap', 'target unreachable since Ttime', 'agent process exited unexpectedly') rather than a generic 'not responding' state.
- Diagnosis correctly distinguishes a concurrency-queue wait (§7.16) from a genuine node/target outage.
- Diagnostics link to the relevant status view (node/target health, or the concurrency-limits UI) for deeper investigation.

Notes: none beyond §7.21 itself; consumes both the node-status and concurrency-governance epics' data.

Depends on: Node/target status view: reachability, agent-process health, CPU/RAM/disk, Per-target concurrency caps with overflow queue

issues.title: Spike: verify Gemini CLI's ACP support at build time
issues.body:
Confirm whether the installed/targeted Gemini CLI version actually speaks ACP (not just plain-CLI driving, which is how emdash integrates it today) before committing to building the adapter module.

Spec: §5.5 ('A Gemini adapter module is the next one reserved...'); §16 ('Gemini ACP: the ACP registry lists Gemini CLI 0.50.0, but emdash drives Gemini via plain CLI, not ACP — verify its ACP flag at build time before promising the module.').

Acceptance:
- Spike documents, against the actual Gemini CLI version available at build time, whether it exposes an ACP mode/flag (not just plain CLI invocation).
- If ACP support is absent or incomplete, the spike records the concrete gap (missing methods/capabilities) rather than assuming full parity.
- Go/no-go recommendation is written down for the adapter-module issue to consume.

Notes: §16 explicitly flags this as unconfirmed and something to verify before promising the module, mirroring the same build-time verification the spec requires for Codex in v1.

issues.title: Gemini provider adapter module (enrich hook + UI wiring)
issues.body:
Build packages/providers/gemini: the enrich(update, raw) hook promoting Gemini-specific _meta fields into core ACP fields, and the provider-specific UI wiring (per-tool-name widgets, permission button set/verbs, image hand-off quirks), falling back to the generic ACP tier wherever Gemini adds nothing.

Spec: §5.5 ('Per-provider adapter modules...add exactly the two things ACP deliberately leaves to the client: an enrich(update, raw) hook...and the provider-specific UI wiring...A module that adds neither simply falls back to the generic tier everywhere.').

Acceptance:
- `packages/providers/gemini` exists as its own small package, structurally mirroring `packages/providers/claude`/`codex`.

- A Gemini session in the cockpit is fully usable (messages, tool calls, permissions, plan updates) through the generic core alone, with adapter enhancements layered on top.

issues.title: Route voice audio through the encrypted relay pipeline
issues.body:
Get recorded audio from a client device to the executing host's voice backend (and synthesized audio back) reusing the same E2E-encrypted blob pipeline built for image attachments, so voice works identically on `local` and `ssh:` targets.

Spec: §7.12; §7.25 (attachment delivery pattern this reuses); §8 (E2E envelopes)

Acceptance:
- A recorded voice clip is encrypted client-side with the session's derived key and uploaded to the relay's blob store as ciphertext, addressed by an opaque ref, exactly like §7.25's image pipeline.
- The agent-supervisor fetches and decrypts the clip locally over the existing node↔supervisor control channel (no new direct supervisor-relay path), mirroring §7.25's "v1: proxied, not a new device class" scoping choice.
- Synthesized speech returned to the client follows the same encrypted-blob path in reverse.
- A large audio blob never rides the live session/update fan-out channel directly — §7.16's bounded-queue backpressure rule still holds.

Notes: architecture directly reuses §7.25's attachment delivery design rather than inventing a new transport.

Depends on: Implement ElevenLabs voice backend (TTS + STT)

issues.title: Voice-driven tool-call approval
issues.body:
Let the operator approve or deny a pending permission request (§7.24's FIFO queue) by voice command, so steering by voice covers the interactive-approval loop, not just narration/dictation.

- An unrecognized utterance leaves the request queued rather than guessing or auto-resolving it.
- Voice approval is gated behind the same negotiated capability/queue state as the keyboard path — no separate permission model.

Notes: no dedicated §16 grounding entry beyond §7.12/§7.24.

issues.title: Spike: verify ACP compliance of the next candidate provider
issues.body:
Before adding a new provider beyond Claude Code/Codex/Gemini, confirm at build time that the candidate agent actually speaks ACP (not just a plain CLI), the same verification discipline already applied to Codex and Gemini.

Spec: §5.5 ("register a provider id... write an enrich/widget module later only if warranted"); §10 ("Codex's ACP support is verified at build time"); §11 (loombox does not chase provider breadth for its own sake)

Acceptance:
- A written check (script or documented manual steps) confirms the candidate's ACP `initialize` handshake and capability set, the same way Codex/Gemini were checked (§10, §16).
- The result (works generically / needs a bespoke module / not viable) is recorded before any adapter code is written.
- If the candidate turns out to be CLI-only with no ACP support, the issue closes as "not viable" rather than building a non-ACP integration, which is out of the spec's scope.

Notes: §16 grounding: "Gemini ACP: ... verify its ACP flag at build time before promising the module" — the same discipline applied one tier further out.

Depends on: none
issues.epic: Provider adapters (Claude Code, Codex, generic; Gemini reserved)

issues.title: Register an additional ACP provider behind the generic tier
issues.body:
Add one more ACP-speaking agent (an OpenCode-family agent or equivalent confirmed by the verification spike) as a provider id that runs entirely on the existing generic ACP adapter (§5.5), with no bespoke code required to get a working cockpit.

Spec: §5.5 ("Generic ACP adapter... which is what makes adding a future ACP agent... near-free: register a provider id"); §7.24 (generic `ToolKind` fallback tier this rides on)

Acceptance:
- The new provider id is selectable when creating a session, using only the generic ACP core/adapter (no per-tool bespoke widgets, no `enrich` hook).
- Sessions with this provider get the full v1/v2 baseline: message/thought reducer, generic tool-call rows, plain Allow/Deny(+always) permission mapping, model/mode/config-option bar.
- The provider's own setup notes (binary location, auth) are documented alongside the existing Claude/Codex/Gemini ones.

Notes: §16's "Generic ACP fallback tier" grounding applies unchanged; no new mechanism is invented, only a new registration.

Depends on: Spike: verify ACP compliance of the next candidate provider
issues.epic: Provider adapters (Claude Code, Codex, generic; Gemini reserved)

issues.title: Bespoke enrich/widget module for the new provider (if warranted)
issues.body:
If the newly-registered provider's own conventions (a vendor `_meta` subagent signal, distinctive tool names) turn out to warrant it, promote it from the generic tier to its own small adapter module, following the same pattern as the Claude/Codex modules.

Spec: §5.5 (per-provider adapter modules: an `enrich(update, raw)` hook plus provider-specific UI wiring)

Acceptance:
- A decision record states explicitly what, if anything, this provider needs beyond the generic tier (an `enrich` hook, specific tool widgets, a distinct permission button set) — "nothing" is an acceptable, documented outcome.
- If something is warranted, a new `packages/providers/<name>` module ships with only that delta, falling back to the generic tier for everything else.

Notes: §5.5 explicitly frames "write later only if warranted" as the intended default path — this issue exists to make that decision, not to presuppose a module is needed.

Depends on: Register an additional ACP provider behind the generic tier
issues.epic: Provider adapters (Claude Code, Codex, generic; Gemini reserved)

issues.title: Spike: design loombox-initiated nested session trees
issues.body:
Design how loombox itself spawns and tracks a tree of child sessions (not just a single provider's vendor-supplied subagent metadata), generalizing v2's `parentToolCallId` rendering (§7.24) to trees the orchestrator creates on purpose for multi-agent workflows.

Spec: §12 far-future ("multi-agent collaboration: planner/implementer/reviewer roles, or best-of-N racing a task with a pick-best"); §7.24 (subagent/nested tool-call tree rendering, the v2 primitive this generalizes); §6 (session/run data model)

Acceptance:
- A data-model doc defines a `parentSessionId`/session-tree relation distinct from (but reusing the rendering shape of) §7.24's `parentToolCallId` tool-call nesting.
- The design states which providers/targets a child session can run on (same node? cross-node?) and how a child's usage/cost rolls up into the parent's aggregate figures, using §7.9's subagent-usage-rollup rule as precedent.
- The doc explicitly scopes this as loombox creating and coordinating multiple real sessions, not one agent's internal subagent tool calls.

Notes: builds directly on §7.24's v2 nested-tool-call-tree rendering and §7.9's rollup rule as the closest existing precedent; no §16 grounding entry exists for orchestrator-initiated multi-agent trees specifically — this is genuinely novel design work.

Depends on: none (assumes v2's §7.24 subagent rendering has shipped)

issues.title: Implement planner/implementer/reviewer role presets
issues.body:
Ship a session template (§7.18's template mechanism as precedent) that spawns a coordinated group of child sessions with distinct roles — a planner that breaks down work, implementers that execute it, a reviewer that checks the result — rendered as one nested tree.

Spec: §12 far-future ("multi-agent collaboration (planner/implementer/reviewer roles...)"); §7.18 (session templates/presets, the mechanism this extends to multi-session); §7.24 (tree rendering)

Acceptance:
- A "planner/implementer/reviewer" preset creates a parent session plus role-tagged child sessions in one action, following the session-tree design from the prior spike.
- Each child's transcript renders using its own native tier-1/2 widgets, nested under the parent per §7.24's existing collapsible-group rendering.
- The parent's board/list entry (§7.2) shows the whole group's aggregate status, not just the parent session's own state.

Notes: no dedicated §16 grounding entry beyond the spike above.

Depends on: Spike: design loombox-initiated nested session trees

issues.title: Implement best-of-N racing with pick-best
issues.body:
Let one prompt spawn N parallel sessions racing the same task, then present a pick-best comparison UI so the operator chooses a winner and discards the rest.

Spec: §12 far-future ("best-of-N racing a task with a pick-best")

Acceptance:
- A "race" action spawns N child sessions from the same starting prompt/workspace snapshot, using §7.20's checkpoint mechanism as the basis for N isolated starting points.
- A comparison view shows each candidate's diff/output side by side.
- Picking a winner keeps that session's worktree/changes and tears down (or archives) the others' workspaces cleanly.

Notes: reuses §7.20's checkpoint/rollback mechanism to fork N identical starting points; no dedicated §16 grounding entry exists for best-of-N specifically.

Depends on: Spike: design loombox-initiated nested session trees

issues.title: Spike: design unattended/headless session run model
issues.body:
Define how a session can run with no client ever attached — driven purely by the supervisor, reporting completion/failure only through the attention inbox and push — as the foundation for both scheduled and webhook-triggered runs.

Spec: §12 far-future ("scheduling and webhook-triggered/headless runs"); §5.6/§7.22 (supervisor owns sessions independently of any connected client, the primitive this reuses); §7.13 (attention inbox as the completion surface)

Acceptance:
- A design doc specifies how a headless run is created (what triggers it, what prompt/workspace it starts from) without assuming a client is present at start time.
- Completion/failure/needs-approval states for a headless run land in the attention inbox and trigger push (§7.11) exactly like a client-started session that finished while the user was away (§7.22).
- The doc states what happens to a permission request raised mid-run with no client watching — queued per §7.24's existing FIFO behavior, never auto-approved.

Notes: builds on §7.22's "supervisor owns every session" persistence primitive and §7.13's inbox; no dedicated §16 grounding entry for headless triggering exists.

Depends on: none (assumes v1/v2 supervisor persistence has shipped)

issues.title: Cron-scheduled session runs
issues.body:
Let the operator schedule a recurring session (e.g. nightly maintenance) against a project/target/provider/prompt template, on the headless-run model above.

Spec: §12 far-future ("scheduling ... headless runs (nightly maintenance...)"); §7.18 (session templates/presets, reused as the "what to run" definition)

Acceptance:
- A schedule (cron-style expression) attached to a session template fires a headless run on the node at the scheduled time, even with no client connected.
- A missed/failed scheduled run (target unreachable, concurrency cap hit per §7.16) surfaces in the attention inbox rather than silently vanishing.
- Schedules are visible and editable from the PWA, listed per project.

Notes: no dedicated §16 grounding entry beyond §7.18/§7.16.

Depends on: Spike: design unattended/headless session run model

issues.title: Webhook-triggered session runs from tracker events
issues.body:
Let an external event (a new/updated tracker ticket) auto-spin a headless session, closing the loop from "ticket created" to "agent working on it" without the operator manually starting anything.

Spec: §12 far-future ("or a tracker ticket that auto-spins a session"); §7.10 (live-mode tracker backends this listens to)

Acceptance:
- A per-project rule maps a tracker event (a new issue with a label, a specific status transition) to a session template plus a starting prompt derived from the ticket's fields.
- The relay exposes a webhook receiver endpoint (GitHub/Jira webhook) that authenticates the sender and dispatches to the owning node.
- A run started this way is tagged with its triggering ticket so the tracker item's linkage fields (per §7.10's native/live linkage pattern) record it.

Notes: reuses §7.10's `TrackerBackend`/tracker-linkage shape; the webhook-receiver mechanism itself has no §16 grounding entry and is novel.

Depends on: Spike: design unattended/headless session run model

issues.title: Spike: confirm WebAuthn PRF platform support & Better Auth passkey readiness
issues.body:
Re-check the current PRF support matrix and Better Auth's passkey plugin `extensions`/`clientExtensionResults` exposure before building the PRF unlock path, since §14 explicitly cut this for gaps that may since have narrowed.

Spec: §14 ("Deferred: WebAuthn PRF convenience unlock"); §16 grounding for this item

Acceptance:
- A written check of current platform PRF support (notably Firefox-Android and CTAP2 security keys, the gaps §14 called out) confirms whether the gap has closed enough to proceed.
- Better Auth's passkey plugin is confirmed to still expose the `extensions`/`clientExtensionResults` fields this design needs.
- A go/no-go decision is recorded; "still not worth it" is an acceptable, documented outcome per §14's own framing.

Notes: §16 grounding: PRF mechanics/support matrix at `developers.yubico.com/WebAuthn/Concepts/PRF_Extension/` and `corbado.com/blog/passkeys-prf-webauthn`; Better Auth's passkey plugin docs at `docs.better-auth.com/docs/plugins/passkey` versus Stytch's non-pass-through SDK at `stytch.com/docs/sdks/webauthn/register`.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Implement WebAuthn PRF convenience unlock as a second AMK-escrow path
issues.body:
Register a passkey with the PRF extension, derive a wrapping key from its PRF output via HKDF, and escrow a second wrapped-AMK blob under it, so a device already holding that passkey can unlock without typing the Recovery Code.

Spec: §14 ("A code-free unlock layered on the AMK escrow (register a passkey with a PRF extension, use the PRF output through HKDF as a second wrapping key for a second escrowed AMK blob...)"); §8 (the AMK escrow model this layers onto)

Acceptance:
- Registering a PRF-capable passkey produces a second wrapped-AMK blob (independent of the Recovery Code blob from §8) stored on the relay as ciphertext only.
- Unlocking via the passkey derives the same AMK the Recovery Code path would, verified by round-tripping a known test session key.
- The Recovery Code path remains fully functional and is never removed or weakened by adding this — PRF is additive, not a replacement.
- The UI states plainly, per §14's required framing, what this does and does not change about key custody.

Notes: §16 grounding as in the prior spike issue (Yubico PRF docs, Corbado writeup, Better Auth passkey plugin docs).

Depends on: Spike: confirm WebAuthn PRF platform support & Better Auth passkey readiness

issues.title: Gate check: confirm v1 AMK/account-login model has survived a real lost-device/rebuilt-relay drill
issues.body:
Before any managed-cloud work starts, explicitly verify and record that the self-hosted AMK bootstrap, revocation re-wrap, and relay-rebuild re-enrollment paths have been drilled for real, since §12/§17 gate the entire edition on this.

Spec: §17 ("explicitly gated on v1's AMK/account-login model surviving a real self-hosted lost-device/rebuilt-relay drill"); §14 (the drill itself, first described as an open risk)

Acceptance:
- A documented drill record exists: a device was actually lost/revoked and recovered via the Recovery Code path, and a relay was actually rebuilt and devices re-enrolled, on a real (not simulated) deployment.
- The outcome (pass/fail, issues found) is written down; a fail blocks every other issue in this epic.
- This issue is the explicit dependency every other managed-cloud issue lists.

Notes: no dedicated §16 grounding entry beyond §14/§17 themselves.

Depends on: none

issues.title: Extend account_id scoping to every account-scoped table and Redis channel
issues.body:
Make the relay genuinely multi-tenant-safe by extending the `owner_account_id` scoping §8 already introduces to every account-scoped table (metadata, blob store, wrapped-AMK escrow, push subscriptions) and namespacing Redis pubsub/fan-out channels by account.

Spec: §17.2 ("extend the same account_id scoping to every account-scoped table... and namespace Redis pubsub/fan-out channels by account")

Acceptance:
- Every account-scoped Postgres table carries and filters on `account_id`/`owner_account_id`, audited row by row against §17.2's list (metadata, blob store, wrapped-AMK escrow, push subscriptions).
- Redis pub/sub channels used for fan-out are namespaced per account so a shared-infra bug cannot cross-deliver between tenants.
- Shared multi-tenant Postgres/Redis is implemented per §17.2's decision — not a dedicated-per-tenant container model.

Notes: §17.1/§17.2 cite the Sentry/PostHog shared-infra precedent for this model; no separate §16 entry exists beyond §17 itself.

Depends on: Gate check: confirm v1 AMK/account-login model has survived a real lost-device/rebuilt-relay drill

issues.title: Cross-tenant isolation test suite
issues.body:
Ship an explicit, automated test suite that proves no account-scoped query or Redis channel can leak across tenants, required before any managed-cloud launch per §14's stated risk.

Spec: §14 ("require an explicit cross-tenant-isolation test suite before any managed-cloud launch, not just code review"); §17.2

Acceptance:
- Automated tests create two or more tenant accounts and assert that no API response, WebSocket fan-out message, or blob fetch for tenant A is ever reachable using tenant B's session/credentials.
- The suite specifically covers every table/channel touched by the account_id-scoping issue.
- The suite gates the managed-cloud release pipeline in CI, not just an advisory manual check.

Notes: no dedicated §16 grounding entry beyond §14 itself.

Depends on: Extend account_id scoping to every account-scoped table and Redis channel

issues.title: Design PWA build trust story for managed-cloud clients
issues.body:
Address the one honest gap §17.3 identifies — that "same relay code" doesn't mean "same trust" once loombox-the-company also serves the client PWA bundle — by designing a concrete mitigation (reproducible/verifiable builds, or treating the signed native mobile wrapper as the trusted client).

Spec: §17.3 ("A concrete mitigation (reproducible/verifiable PWA builds, and/or treating a signed native mobile wrapper... as the trusted client rather than a live browser bundle) is left as a design question for whenever this edition is actually built")

Acceptance:
- A design doc picks and justifies at least one mitigation (reproducible build plus published hash/subresource integrity, or native-wrapper-as-trust-anchor).
- The doc states plainly, per §17.3's own honesty requirement, what residual trust gap remains even after the mitigation — the standard browser-E2E caveat every such product carries.
- The chosen mitigation is scoped to be actually buildable given the native mobile wrapper (§12 v3) already exists by this point.

Notes: §17.3 cites Signal/WhatsApp's reliance on native app-store review/reproducible builds as the precedent for why this can't rest on server-code parity alone.

Depends on: Native mobile build, signing & store release pipeline

issues.title: Managed-cloud billing/provisioning/admin package
issues.body:
Build the cloud-only glue (account billing, tenant provisioning, an admin dashboard) as a separately-licensed package per §17.5's decision, keeping the relay/protocol/crypto/node/supervisor packages MIT and untouched.

Spec: §17.5 ("if freerider protection is ever wanted, the pattern to imitate is Cal.com's /ee split: new cloud-only glue... in a separately licensed package — never the relay/crypto core"); §13 (MIT throughout the core)

Acceptance:
- A new package (outside the MIT core, e.g. a `cloud-admin` package) houses billing/provisioning/admin UI under its own explicit license, not MIT.
- No relay/protocol/crypto/node/supervisor package gains a license header change or a hard dependency on this package.
- The self-hosted relay build works identically with this package entirely absent.

Notes: no dedicated §16 grounding entry beyond §17.5's own citations (Cal.com's `/ee` split, Sentry's FSL scoping).

Depends on: Cross-tenant isolation test suite

issues.title: Self-host to managed-cloud portability: re-point and re-bootstrap flow
issues.body:
Ship the day-one portability path §17.6 commits to — pointing a node/client at a different relay URL and re-running an AMK-bootstrap path against the new account — without yet building full history export/import.

Spec: §17.6 ("Ship the free re-point/re-bootstrap path on day one; treat a full-history export/import tool as a separate later roadmap item")

Acceptance:
- A node/client can be re-pointed at a new relay URL through configuration alone, with no code change.
- Re-running either AMK-bootstrap path (§8) against the new account works against the new relay exactly as it would against a fresh self-hosted one.
- The UI states explicitly that relay-side history (sessions, device registry) does not move automatically, per §17.6.

Notes: full history export/import is explicitly out of scope for this issue per §17.6's own framing; grounded in Sentry's "Moving to SaaS" migration-guide precedent §16 cites for this section.

Depends on: Extend account_id scoping to every account-scoped table and Redis channel
